sources.ref: R. Bauckham in Carson (ed.) 1982: Sunday observance 'already normal Christian practice at the beginning of the second century' with no record of non-observing groups except one Ebionite faction
sources.independence_note: Mainstream synthesis independent of both confessional poles.

rulings.would_change_this: A re-dating of Barnabas and Justin's evidence to a later period, or new documentary evidence that the Gentile churches of Rome and Alexandria kept the seventh-day Sabbath through the 2nd century, would reopen the as-written ruling.
rulings.notes: Originally anachronistic (it does match Andrews's 1873 narrative); for synthesis this improbable-family ruling qualifies for the period_standard tag. Largest framing gap in the dossier (1 to 5).

components.component: The 1201 Sunday roll actually proceeded from the pontifical palace at Rome.
components.rationale: Asserted as fact ('in fact') on no documentary basis. The roll is a recension of the 'Letter from Heaven' apocryphon attested from the 6th century (condemned by Licinianus of Cartagena, c. 584), circulating across the Greek, Latin, Irish and Old English worlds as a chain letter; its origin is unknown and no scholarship locates it in the papal curia (Priebsch 1936; Lees 1985; Haines 2010; NASSCAL). The contemporary source for Eustace's campaign (Roger of Howden) has no papal provenance; the pope enters only through the later Matthew Paris tradition, which transmits the legend's own framing. The papacy is meanwhile documented condemning a heaven-letter at the Roman synod of 745 (Zachary vs. Aldebert). Not discredited outright, because a negative about a single roll's transmission path cannot be decisively proven and one medieval chronicle does connect the promulgation to papal commission.

rulings.weakened_version: The Sunday roll was a human clerical forgery, a version of the centuries-old 'Letter from Heaven,' deployed by churchmen to enforce Sunday observance rather than a message from heaven.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest defense is Andrews's inference chain (History of the Sabbath, 1873, pp. 388-390): Matthew Paris reports the epistle was referred 'to the judgment of the Roman pontiff,' who 'ordained heralds' including Eustace, therefore 'we trace the responsibility for this roll directly to the pope of Rome.' Its actual argument is real primary material and is engaged as such. It fails as provenance evidence: Paris wrote decades after 1201 and reproduces the abbot's own promotional narrative; the pope-frame is a recurring internal feature of the apocryphon's recensions (NASSCAL), not external attestation; the contemporary Howden, who personally heard Eustace at York, records no papal chain; and the letter's six-century prehistory excludes 13th-century curial authorship of the text itself. White additionally hardened Andrews's inference ('responsibility... directly to the pope') into a claim of physical origin ('the pontifical palace... was the source whence it proceeded').

sources.ref: NASSCAL e-Clavis, 'Epistle of Christ from Heaven' (nasscal.com)
sources.independence_note: Scholarly apocrypha catalogue; independent of the Sabbath/Sunday polemic entirely.

sources.ref: R. Priebsch, Letter from Heaven on the Observance of the Lord's Day (Oxford, 1936); C. Lees, 'The Sunday Letter and the Sunday Lists', Anglo-Saxon England 14 (1985); D. Haines, Sunday Observance and the Sunday Letter in Anglo-Saxon England (Brewer, 2010)
sources.independence_note: The standard philological tradition on the letter; consulted via scholarly metadata and Cambridge Core; one genealogical line (Priebsch's successors), counted accordingly.

sources.ref: Roman synod of 745 under Pope Zachary condemning Aldebert's heaven-letter (Boniface correspondence; Catholic Encyclopedia s.v. 'Pope St. Zachary')
sources.independence_note: Documented papal condemnation of the genre, independent of the philological line.

sources.ref: Roger of Howden, Annals, and Matthew Paris, Historia Major p. 201, via Andrews 1873 (local, lines 17686-17953)
sources.independence_note: The medieval chronicle base, quoted verbatim in Andrews; Andrews's own inference is a position.

rulings.would_change_this: A papal chancery register, letter, or datable manuscript connecting the Eustace roll's production or dispatch to the Roman curia would move the ruling toward the claim; conversely, a critical edition establishing the roll's textual dependence on a non-Roman recension would push toward discredited.

quantifier_audit: the bishops of the church (collective definite)
inspired by ambition and thirst for power

components.component: Bishops urged Constantine to enact the Sunday law of 321.
components.rationale: No contemporary evidence of episcopal urging exists. The law itself (CJ 3.12.2) names no instigator and uses neutral solar language; Eusebius (VC 4.18), the contemporary episcopal witness, attributes the ordinance to Constantine's own act ('He ordained...'). The modern debate (Girardet vs. Wallraff) is entirely about Constantine's own religiosity, not about episcopal lobbying, which no specialist asserts as documented. The urging cannot be decisively excluded (private counsel leaves no trace; Ossius of Cordoba was at court), which is why the ruling is improbable rather than discredited.

components.component: The bishops were 'inspired by ambition and thirst for power,' seeking a shared festival to promote nominal pagan acceptance and advance the church's power and glory.
components.rationale: Undocumented motive attribution. Per charter §3 as amended, the cap is improbable only if the rival motive account has documentary support; it does: the law's own 'dies solis' framing is documentary, and Eusebius's contemporary account attributes the unifying-missionary aim to Constantine himself ('his earnest desire being gradually to lead all mankind to the worship of God', VC 4.18). The motive component therefore caps at improbable.

rulings.weakened_version: Church leaders welcomed and reinforced Constantine's Sunday legislation, which served the church's growth by easing pagans into Christian observance.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest position toward the claim is K. M. Girardet ('Vom Sonnen-Tag zum Sonntag', ZAC 2007): the 321 law was Christian in substance, continuous with Constantine's church-favoring legislation of 312/313, which makes Christian input at court (Ossius) historically plausible. Engaged and partially conceded: Girardet strengthens the plausibility of episcopal influence but supplies no document of urging, and himself treats the law as the emperor's measure. On the other flank, sabbatarian-leaning analysts themselves conclude the influence likely ran the other way (Eusebius deriving his Sunday-rest theology from the law). The 'ambition and thirst for power' smear has no documentary basis on any side; White's own period sources carried it as Protestant polemic, not evidence.

sources.ref: Eusebius, Vita Constantini 4.18 (NPNF2-01, local: scholarship/npnf2-01-eusebius-church-history-life-constantine.txt, lines 62365-62388)
sources.independence_note: Contemporary episcopal account attributing act and motive to Constantine; read with its known panegyric bias.

sources.ref: Constantine's law, CJ 3.12.2 (Ayer, Source Book, local, lines 13263-13273)
sources.independence_note: The legislative text itself; no ecclesiastical instigator, solar denomination.

sources.ref: K. M. Girardet, 'Vom Sonnen-Tag zum Sonntag', Zeitschrift für Antikes Christentum (De Gruyter, 2007); M. Wallraff, 'Constantine's Devotion to the Sun after 324' (LMU repository); U. Agnati, 'Constantine's Statutes on Sunday Rest', Calumet 1 (2015)
sources.independence_note: The two poles of the modern motive debate plus a juridical study; mutually independent, none asserts episcopal urging.

rulings.would_change_this: Any contemporary document (council minutes, episcopal correspondence, a court source) showing bishops petitioning Constantine for the 321 law would move the fact component; documentary evidence of episcopal deliberation about attracting pagans via a shared day would move the motive component.
rulings.notes: Fact and motive components tie at the minimum; failing_component_kind takes fact by the §3 priority, tie recorded.

components.component: Sunday observance as a Christian institution rests on no command of Christ or the apostles recorded in Scripture and became an obligatory institution through post-biblical development.
components.rationale: Agreed across the spectrum: the Carson volume (defending Sunday) concludes the NT nowhere transfers Sabbath obligation to Sunday and knows no Sunday command; Rordorf dates the rest-obligation to Constantine and after; the Catholic catechetical tradition grounds the change in church authority precisely because a scriptural command is absent.

components.component: Sunday observance did not stem from apostolic practice or sanction, but from a post-apostolic development (the institution as the child of the corrupting process, not of the apostles).
components.rationale: Qualified scholars disagree on comparable evidence. For apostolic-era origin: Rordorf (1962), Bauckham (1982, arguing from early ubiquity without controversy), Llewelyn (Novum Testamentum 2001, reading Acts 20:7, 1 Cor 16:2, Rev 1:10 as evidence of Sunday meetings). Against its demonstrability: N. H. Young's response (Novum Testamentum 2003, Adventist-located but peer-validated venue) arguing the NT texts do not establish Sunday observance; Bacchiocchi's post-apostolic dating; and the independent methodological caution of Bradshaw (Oxford UP 2002) that early uniform-practice reconstructions outrun the evidence. The disagreement does not reduce to confessional alignment (Bradshaw is not a party to the Sabbath question), and the three NT texts genuinely underdetermine the answer.

components.component: That development was the 'mystery of lawlessness' of 2 Thess 2, i.e. an apostasy/corruption relative to the abiding Sabbath obligation, already at work in Paul's day.
components.rationale: A prophetic identification conditional on the theological status of the Sabbath and on the reading of 2 Thess 2; this adjudication does not evaluate it (§9). The claim-level ruling is expressly conditional: it covers the historical genealogy, not the apostasy characterization.

rulings.weakened_version: Sunday observance as an obligatory Christian institution is not commanded in Scripture and developed gradually in the early post-apostolic church.
rulings.minority_engagement: Both sides documented per §1's requirement for genuinely-open: apostolic-origin case (Rordorf; Bauckham's ubiquity-without-controversy argument; Llewelyn 2001) versus non-demonstrability case (Young 2003 in Novum Testamentum; Bacchiocchi 1977; Bradshaw's Oxford UP methodological caution). Neither side holds decisive primary evidence: the NT texts are individually ambiguous, and the earliest unambiguous witnesses (Barnabas, Ignatius, Justin) postdate the apostles by one to three generations.

sources.ref: S. R. Llewelyn, 'The Use of Sunday for Meetings of Believers in the New Testament', Novum Testamentum 43 (2001) 205-223; N. H. Young, 'A Response', Novum Testamentum 45 (2003) (JSTOR 1561012)
sources.independence_note: A single peer-reviewed exchange carrying both sides; Young's Adventist location noted, venue peer-validated (§4.1).

sources.ref: P. Bradshaw, The Search for the Origins of Christian Worship (Oxford UP, 2002)
sources.independence_note: Non-confessional liturgical scholarship; independent of the Sabbatarian debate.

sources.ref: R. Bauckham in Carson (ed.), From Sabbath to Lord's Day (1982); W. Rordorf, Sunday (1968)
sources.independence_note: The apostolic-origin mainstream; two related but distinct lineages (Bauckham engages Rordorf critically).

rulings.would_change_this: New early evidence fixing the reference of Acts 20:7 / 1 Cor 16:2 / Rev 1:10 (e.g., a 1st-century papyrus attesting weekly first-day assembly, or conversely early evidence of exclusively Sabbath-based assembly in Pauline churches) would close the open component in either direction.
rulings.notes: Only claim in the dossier whose as-written ruling stays at genuinely-open; the hermeneutic core ('mystery of lawlessness') is out of scope, and the ruling is conditional as stated in the component record.

evidence_review: The dossier covers two evidence pools that meet in GC chapters 18 and 20. (1) The Litch/1840 pool. Josiah Litch's 'The Probability of the Second Coming of Christ, about A.D. 1843' (June 1838) applied the year-day reading of Revelation 9 to Ottoman history: 150 years from Othman's attack of 27 July 1299 to 1449 (Constantine 'Deacozes' ascending 'by permission' of the Sultan), then 391 years 15 days ending in August 1840 (Wellcome 1874, lines 3841-3862, 6996-7069, reproduces the whole scheme from Litch's own text). In Signs of the Times, and Expositor of Prophecy of 1 Aug 1840 Litch pinned the terminus to 11 August 1840 in the very words GC quotes, hedged ('may be expected to be broken... And this, I believe, will be found to be the case'); the same article added a further hedge GC omits ('no positive evidence that the first period was exactly, to a day, fulfilled'). Neither critical nor apologetic literature disputes the quotation's authenticity; the dispute is entirely about fulfillment. The documented diplomatic sequence of the Second Eastern Crisis: the Porte accepted great-power intervention against Muhammad Ali after the collective note of July 1839; the Convention for the Pacification of the Levant was signed at London on 15 July 1840 by the four powers together with the Ottoman plenipotentiary (this is the act GC's phrase 'through her ambassadors accepted the protection of the allied powers' actually describes); the envoy Rifat Bey left Constantinople 5 Aug, his steamer reached Alexandria on 11 Aug and sat in quarantine until the 16th; his first audience with the Pasha was 16 Aug and the written communication of the ultimatum followed on 19 Aug (Wellcome 1874 reproducing the Millerite dossier itself; U.S. FRUS 1879 doc. 518; standard accounts of the Oriental Crisis). The empire was not overthrown: the intervention was undertaken by the Quadruple Alliance AND the Ottoman Empire and preserved it (Ozavci, Dangerous Gifts, OUP 2021, ch. 'Return of the Ashes'); the empire endured until 1922. The academic case study of the Millerite use of this episode is Eric Anderson, 'The Millerite Use of Prophecy: A Case Study of a Striking Fulfillment', in Numbers & Butler (eds.), The Disappointed (Indiana UP, 1987): nothing decisive happened on 11 Aug itself; the 'fulfillment' was constructed retrospectively from newspaper reports in the autumn of 1840; Litch himself noted how few were convinced in New England. Litch abandoned the year-day principle in 1867 and in A Complete Harmony of Daniel and the Apocalypse (1873) no longer defended the 11 Aug fulfillment; Adventist reviewers W. A. Spicer (letter, 30 Nov 1914: the ultimatum 'was not delivered to the Pasha of Egypt on Aug. 11, 1840') and W. W. Prescott (1916: the date 'would not stand the test of historical facts') conceded the mismatch internally. The standing published defense (Uriah Smith; Froom, Prophetic Faith of Our Fathers IV; Edwin de Kock) relocates the fulfillment to Rifat Bey's arrival at Alexandria on 11 Aug, arguing control of war and peace passed out of the Sultan's hands that day. (2) The independence pool. That advent expectation appeared in many lands in the early 19th century is uncontested (Sandeen, The Roots of Fundamentalism, U Chicago Press 1970, treats the British and American revivals as one transatlantic phenomenon; the Phase-1 verifier already ruled the simultaneity claim GC-20-015 supported). What the historiography does not support is isolation and sola-Scriptura derivation: Lacunza's manuscript was translated by Edward Irving in 1827 and, in GC's own words, 'served to deepen the interest already awakening in England' (GC ch. 20, par. 20); Joseph Wolff, GC's leading exemplar, attended the first Albury prophetic conference of 1826 convened by Henry Drummond alongside Irving (documented even by Froom, PFOF vol. 3, an admission against interest); Bengel's views spread through his published writings ('spread throughout Christendom', GC par. 23); Gaussen was set on Daniel by Rollin's Ancient History (GC par. 24); the whole wave worked inside the inherited Protestant historicist year-day tradition (Mede-Newton-Faber lineage) transmitted in print. The mutual independence of Bengel (d. 1752), Lacunza (wrote c. 1790), and Miller (reached his scheme 1816-1818, and historians treat Millerism as largely indigenous) is real and carries the weakened version; genuinely 'isolated' bodies deriving the belief 'solely' from Scripture are not documented, and the best candidate sometimes offered (the Swedish child criers of 1841-43) was an ecstatic trance phenomenon inside the Lutheran pietist revival, not a body arriving at belief by study.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет два сюжета миллеритской истории: тезис, что адвентистская весть возникла в разных странах независимо, «исключительно через изучение Писания», и рассказ о предсказании Джосайи Литча про падение османской власти 11 августа 1840 года. Цитату Литча книга передаёт точно, с его собственными оговорками и с верной датировкой, поэтому это утверждение переведено в подтверждённые. Сам «исполнившийся точный срок» проверки не выдерживает: Порта приняла защиту держав конвенцией 15 июля 1840 года, ультиматум довели до Мухаммеда Али 16-19 августа, а 11 августа в Александрию лишь прибыл пароход с посланником, и позднее сам Литч отказался от этого толкования. Тезис о возникновении веры «исключительно из Писания» в изолированных группах противоречит задокументированным связям: перевод Лакунсы Ирвингом, конференции в Олбери с участием Вольфа, общая традиция толкования пророчеств; в ослабленной форме («сходные выводы при малом прямом контакте», как у Бенгеля, Лакунсы и Миллера) картина правдоподобна. Оценки сдвинули бы османский документ о формальном акте подчинения именно 11 августа или архивные свидетельства о группах, пришедших к датированному адвентистскому ожиданию вне этих сетей и литературы.

quantifier_audit: solely by the study of the Scriptures

components.component: Bodies of Christians in different lands arrived at the belief that the advent was near, in the early 19th century
components.rationale: Uncontested across the historiography: Sandeen documents a transatlantic millenarian revival c. 1800-1845; GC's own chapter catalogs Wolff, Lacunza, Bengel's school, Gaussen, the Scandinavian criers; the Phase-1 verifier independently ruled the parallel simultaneity claim (GC-20-015) supported. See evidence_review, pool 2.

components.component: These bodies were 'isolated', i.e. mutually unconnected, arriving at the belief independently of one another
components.rationale: The documented cases were networked, several of them by GC's own account: Irving's 1827 translation of Lacunza deepened 'the interest already awakening in England' (GC ch. 20 par. 20); Wolff attended the 1826 Albury conference at the center of the British network (Froom PFOF 3 concedes this); Bengel's influence spread via his publications; the Morning Watch and missionary/print networks tied the wave together. Real mutual independence exists for Bengel, Lacunza, and Miller as individuals separated in time, but no historian documents 'isolated bodies' plural in different lands, and under the burden rule the existential-sounding sentence, functioning rhetorically as a universal independence claim, required exactly that documentation.

components.component: They arrived at this belief 'solely by the study of the Scriptures', excluding transmitted interpretive tradition, publications, and preaching
components.rationale: Every documented case worked inside the inherited Protestant historicist year-day tradition transmitted in print (Mede-Newton-Faber lineage); Gaussen was set on Daniel by Rollin's secular history per GC's own narrative; Miller used the standard historicist apparatus; the Swedish child criers, the best candidate for an isolated body, arrived at their proclamation through ecstatic trance inside a pietist revival, not through study. 'Solely' is an exclusive-source assertion the record contradicts for each identifiable case.

rulings.weakened_version: In several lands, expositors and groups of Christians with little or no direct contact with one another arrived, chiefly through their own study of biblical prophecy, at the belief that the advent was near.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest defense: LeRoy Edwin Froom, The Prophetic Faith of Our Fathers vols. 3-4, which catalogs hundreds of advent expositors on several continents and argues a providential, essentially simultaneous awakening; the SDA encyclopedic tradition follows him. Its real strength is the genuine mutual independence of Bengel (d. 1752), Lacunza (c. 1790), and Miller (1816-1818), who demonstrably did not copy one another. The defense fails for the claim as written because Froom's own catalog documents the connective tissue the sentence denies: Irving translating Lacunza in 1827, Wolff sitting at Albury in 1826, Bengel's printed legacy, the Morning Watch circulation, and the shared historicist method that every party inherited from published commentary. Convergence within one transmitted exegetical culture is what the evidence shows; isolation with Scripture as the sole input is not documented in any case. The confessional location of the defense is noted, not held against it. Phase-1: no defender object exists (disputed skipped the stage); the verifier's diffusion rationale is confirmed and extended here.

sources.ref: E. R. Sandeen, The Roots of Fundamentalism: British and American Millenarianism 1800-1930 (U Chicago Press, 1970), ch. 1 'The Revival of British Millenarism'
sources.independence_note: University-press monograph, the standard treatment of the transatlantic revival; independent of all confessional literature on both sides.

sources.ref: GC ch. 20, pars. 20-24 (source/chapters/gc_ch20.txt): Lacunza translated in London c. 1825, interest 'already awakening in England'; Bengel's writings 'spread throughout Christendom'; Gaussen led to Daniel by Rollin
sources.independence_note: The adjudicated book's own narrative; genealogically independent of Sandeen and evidentially decisive as admission against the 'isolated/solely' framing.

sources.ref: L. E. Froom, The Prophetic Faith of Our Fathers vol. 3 (Review & Herald, 1946), section on the first Albury conference listing Joseph Wolff among the twenty participants (via egwwritings.org)
sources.independence_note: Adventist confessional historiography; used as admission against interest on Wolff's network membership; corroborated by DNB entry on Henry Drummond and standard Albury accounts.

sources.ref: 'Two Hundred Years from Lacunza', JATS (Andrews University) on Irving's 1827 translation and its transmission
sources.independence_note: Adventist scholarly venue conceding the Lacunza-Irving transmission chain; same direction as Sandeen but confessionally located on the claim's own side.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed case study documenting a body of Christians in the 1820s-1840s that reached a dated near-advent expectation demonstrably without access to historicist prophetic literature or to the Anglo-American and continental revival networks would move the as-written ruling toward genuinely-open; several such cases would overturn it.
rulings.notes: Load-bearing claim. Rowe, Doan, and Numbers & Butler were not directly consulted (no OA copies; Land's local AUSS survey is an unreadable scan in this environment); the ruling rests on Sandeen plus transmission facts attested by the book itself and by the claim's own defenders, which deeper consultation is unlikely to reverse. Kept confidence at medium accordingly.

components.component: Litch published, shortly before 11 Aug 1840, the quoted calculation (150 years exactly fulfilled at Deacozes' permission-accession, plus 391 years 15 days, ending 11 Aug 1840) in these words
components.rationale: The wording matches Litch's article in Signs of the Times, and Expositor of Prophecy of 1 Aug 1840 (GC 1911 Appendix note 560), reproduced identically in apologetic and critical literature alike; no published party disputes authenticity. The idiosyncratic 'Deacozes' and the 391y15d scheme are Litch's own, documented from his 1838 book in the insider record (Wellcome 1874, local file, lines 3841-3862, 6996-7069). GC preserves his hedges ('may be expected', 'And this, I believe, will be found to be the case') and correctly sequences the loose 1838 form ('sometime in the month of August') against the day-precise 1 Aug 1840 refinement ('only a few days previous... he wrote'). See evidence_review, pool 1.

components.component: The prophetic calculation itself: Rev 9 trumpets as Ottoman history under the year-day principle
components.rationale: Out of scope under charter section 9: the adjudication rules on the accuracy of the quotation act, not on whether the year-day computation is valid. The claim-level ruling is conditional in this respect: it certifies attribution, not the soundness of what is attributed.

rulings.weakened_version: In the Signs of the Times of 1 August 1840 Litch published a calculation ending the Ottoman prophetic periods on 11 August 1840, himself conditioning the result on the exactness of the starting dates.
rulings.minority_engagement: No published minority denies the attribution; critics of the episode (Anderson in The Disappointed; Casebolt; counter-apologetic sites) treat the quotation as genuine and attack the fulfillment instead. Answer to the Phase-1 verifier, whose 'disputed' rested on the day-precision being 'refined afterward' and on Litch's later retreat: both points are real but neither touches this claim, since GC's own text states the refinement sequence accurately and Litch's later abandonment (year-day dropped 1867; A Complete Harmony, 1873) bears on GC-18-120's fulfillment framing, not on whether he wrote these words in 1840. One caveat recorded: the same 1 Aug 1840 article contained a further hedge GC omits ('no positive evidence that the first period was exactly, to a day, fulfilled'), but the excerpt GC prints keeps the conditional 'Allowing...' and 'may be expected', so the omission does not alter the quoted meaning. No defender object exists (disputed skipped the stage).

sources.ref: I. C. Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874), local file lines 3841-3862 and 6996-7069: the 1838 calculation, the 391y15d scheme, the Deacozes narrative
sources.independence_note: Insider movement history reproducing Litch's own text; predates GC 1888/1911 and is independent of White's compilation.

sources.ref: E. Anderson, 'The Millerite Use of Prophecy', in Numbers & Butler (eds.), The Disappointed (Indiana UP, 1987)
sources.independence_note: Peer-located academic case study of this exact episode; consulted via convergent secondary reports (Casebolt/Spectrum, essaysbyellenwhite, nonegw), which descend from it and are counted as one conduit; treats the quotation as authentic.

sources.ref: nonegw.org, 'Josiah Litch's Fake Date' (fetched): quotes the 1 Aug 1840 article verbatim and Litch's 1873 Complete Harmony
sources.independence_note: Counter-apologetic site; hostile to the fulfillment claim yet confirming the quotation's authenticity, i.e. adversarial corroboration.

sources.ref: GC 1911 Appendix note 560 (Signs of the Times, and Expositor of Prophecy, 1 Aug 1840)
sources.independence_note: The 1911 edition's own source apparatus, produced in the quote-verification revision; identifies the printed source critics have checked against.

rulings.would_change_this: Examination of the physical/scanned Signs of the Times, and Expositor of Prophecy issue of 1 August 1840 showing different wording, or absence of the passage, would overturn the attribution; a documented pre-1840 source for the exact 11 Aug wording would also revise the 'a few days previous' framing.
rulings.notes: Ruling moves toward the book (disputed -> well-supported), per charter section 7 symmetry. The Phase-1 'disputed' conflated attribution accuracy with fulfillment validity.

components.component: Turkey, through her ambassadors, accepted the protection/intervention of the allied powers of Europe in 1840
components.rationale: Documented: the Porte accepted great-power intervention against Muhammad Ali (collective note of July 1839) and its plenipotentiary signed the Convention for the Pacification of the Levant at London, 15 July 1840, with the four powers. See evidence_review, pool 1.

components.component: This acceptance occurred 'at the very time specified' by Litch's calculation, i.e. 11 August 1840
components.rationale: The dated record contradicts the day-match: the acceptance 'through her ambassadors' happened at London on 15 July 1840; the ultimatum reached Muhammad Ali on 16-19 August (Rifat Bey's first audience 16 Aug, written communication 19 Aug per FRUS 1879 doc. 518); the only 11 Aug occurrence was the arrival of Rifat Bey's steamer at Alexandria, where it sat in quarantine until the 16th, a detail the Millerite dossier itself records (Wellcome 1874, lines 7309-7316). Adventist investigators Spicer (1914) and Prescott (1916) conceded the mismatch. Capped at improbable rather than discredited because GC par. 46 prints both the loose 1838 form ('sometime in the month of August') and the day-precise form, and under the month-loose reading the mid-August ultimatum events do fall within the specified period.

components.component: 'and thus placed herself under the control of Christian nations'
components.rationale: The intervention was undertaken by the Quadruple Alliance and the Ottoman Empire jointly and preserved the empire (Ozavci 2021); the empire remained sovereign until 1922, entering the Tanzimat era, with growing but informal European leverage. Contemporary press rhetoric (London Morning Herald: the Sultan 'reduced to the rank of a puppet'; Goodell in Missionary Herald 1841) gives the frame period currency, but modern Ottoman historiography does not describe an August-1840 passage under Christian 'control'.

rulings.weakened_version: In the summer of 1840, within weeks of the date Litch had named, the Ottoman government accepted four-power intervention against Muhammad Ali (Convention of London, 15 July 1840; ultimatum communicated to the Pasha in mid-August), and contemporaries widely read the crisis as exposing the empire's dependence on Christian Europe.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest defense: the line running from Litch's own November 1840 letter through Uriah Smith, Froom (PFOF IV), and Edwin de Kock ('Vindicating Josiah Litch's Dates'): Rifat Bey arrived at Alexandria on 11 August, and from that moment 'the question of war or peace was taken from the Sultan's hands', so Ottoman supremacy departed on the calculated day. Engaged on evidence, it fails for the sentence as written: (a) a steamer's arrival in quarantine effected no transaction, and even the Millerite dossier concedes the envoy could not act until the 16th; (b) the act GC actually describes, acceptance 'through her ambassadors', is the London Convention of 15 July; (c) Litch's quoted prediction concerned Ottoman power 'in Constantinople', where nothing occurred on 11 August; (d) Litch himself dropped the year-day basis in 1867 and no longer defended the fulfillment in 1873, and Adventist reviewers Spicer and Prescott privately conceded the date. The defense keeps a real insight, that contemporaries saw 1840 as the end of Ottoman self-sufficiency, which the weakened ruling credits in full. No Phase-1 defender object exists (disputed skipped the stage); the verifier's rationale is confirmed with tightened dating.

sources.ref: O. Ozavci, Dangerous Gifts: Imperialism, Security, and Civil Wars in the Levant, 1798-1864 (Oxford UP, 2021), ch. 'Return of the Ashes: The Concert of Europe and the 1840 Intervention'
sources.independence_note: Current university-press treatment of the 1840 intervention; independent of all Millerite-related literature.

sources.independence_note: Diplomatic compilation genealogically independent of both Adventist and counter-Adventist positions.

evidence_review: STATE OF THE QUESTION. (1) French revolutionary religious legislation 1793-1797. Modern historiography (John McManners, The French Revolution and the Church, 1969; Nigel Aston, Religion and Revolution in France 1780-1804, 2000; Michel Vovelle on dechristianization; Timothy Tackett) describes the dechristianization of Year II as a decentralized, largely extra-legislative campaign, not a national statutory abolition of Christianity. Sequence: the Convention adopted the revolutionary calendar (5 Oct 1793) and acquiesced in the Cult of Reason (Festival of Reason in Notre-Dame, 10 Nov 1793, after Gobel's abjuration of 7 Nov); the closure of Paris churches (23-24 Nov 1793) was an act of the Paris Commune; on 6 Dec 1793 (16 Frimaire II) the Convention explicitly reaffirmed freedom of worship. No decree proscribing the Bible has ever been located: the GC 1911 revision team itself, per Arthur L. White's account (The Later Elmshaven Years, on the 1911 revision), found that 'painstaking research failed to disclose such specific legislation, but edicts were found that did so in effect' — which is why the 1888 wording ('the decree which prohibited the Bible') was reworded in 1911. Restoration was staged: 18 Sept 1794 (end of the constitutional church's state budget); law of 3 Ventose III, 21 Feb 1795, on the report of the Protestant Boissy d'Anglas (free exercise of all cults, separation of church and state — Assemblee nationale official history; Musee protestant); decree of 11 Prairial III, 30 May 1795 (church buildings returned). The Convention dissolved 26 Oct 1795; the Constitution of Year III replaced it with the Council of Five Hundred and Council of Ancients. A further liberalization under the Directory — Camille Jordan's report on liberty of worship (17 June 1797) and the law of 24 Aug 1797 repealing the measures against refractory clergy, carried by the new post-election majority — was annulled eleven days later by the Fructidor coup (4 Sept 1797), after which persecution of refractory clergy resumed and intensified to 1799 (Jordan himself was proscribed). The 3.5-year construction (Nov 1793 -> June 1797) descends from historicist exposition — George Croly, The Apocalypse of St. John (1827), followed by Uriah Smith — not from period academic historiography. Its strongest modern defense is Jan Voerman, 'The Reign of Terror', AUSS 47/1 (2009): 117-134, with an earlier companion article 'Ellen White and the French Revolution' (AUSS), a refereed confessional venue; published critics include Donald Casebolt (Father Miller's Daughter, 2022) and counter-apologetic compilations (positions only). Mainstream French-Revolution scholarship does not engage the Rev 11 frame at all. (2) Voltaire's 'ecrasez l'infame'. The critical tradition — Theodore Besterman (critical edition of the correspondence; the Voltaire Foundation lineage), Peter Gay (Voltaire's Politics, Princeton UP, 1959), Rene Pomeau (La Religion de Voltaire, 1956), with Will Durant's The Age of Voltaire as an independent popularizing witness — reads l'infame as superstition, fanaticism and the persecuting institutional church: the letters from c. 1760 collocate 'infame superstition', 'infames prejuges', 'infame fanatisme', with the epistolary signature 'Ecr.l'inf.'; Voltaire professed theism throughout and the slogan predates the Revolution (he died in 1778). No scholarly defense of 'l'infame = Christ personally' was located in either direction of search; that gloss circulated in 19th-century Anglo-Protestant homiletics. Separately, revolutionary dechristianizers did denounce Christianity as an imposture: the abjuration ceremonies of Nov 1793 had priests confess they had 'lied and misled', and Hebertist rhetoric attacked Christ's religion directly (Springer, 'November 7, 10, 1793: Dechristianizing'; dechristianization surveys). (3) The Louis XV apocryphon. Quotation scholarship traces 'Apres nous, le deluge' to Mme de Pompadour consoling Louis XV after Rossbach (Nov 1757), first printed in Mme du Hausset's Memoires (1824), with a parallel anecdote from the painter Maurice Quentin de La Tour; Mirabeau's L'Ami des hommes (1756) already calls the phrase 'the most common of all proverbs among us', and classical antecedents exist (the Greek tag 'when I am dead, let earth be mingled with fire', with Nero's variant in Suetonius/Dio). No contemporary document records Louis XV uttering 'Apres moi le deluge'; standard quotation references (Oxford) file the saying under Pompadour, and the attributions to the king appear only in later 19th-20th-century retellings. WHAT CHANGED SINCE THE 19TH CENTURY: the legislative record of 1793-1797 was already in Thiers/Mignet/Alison and has only been consolidated since (the 1911 revisers' failed source-hunt shows the gap was visible then); Voltaire studies were professionalized by Besterman's critical edition and Gay's and Pomeau's monographs; quotation scholarship (du Hausset's provenance, the Mirabeau attestation) moved the deluge saying from royal biography to apocrypha.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет три утверждения главы о Французской революции: интервал ровно в три с половиной года между отменой религии в 1793 году и её восстановлением «тем же собранием», лозунг «раздавите гадину» как направленный лично против Христа и слова «после меня хоть потоп» в устах Людовика XV. Законодательная хроника опровергает первую конструкцию: Конвент подтвердил свободу культов уже 6 декабря 1793 года, закон 3 вантоза (21 февраля 1795 года) восстановил свободное отправление религии через пятнадцать месяцев, а к июню 1797 года Конвента не существовало, так что «то же собрание» ничего отменить не могло. Исследователи Вольтера (Бестерман, Питер Гей, Помо) читают «l'infame» как суеверие, фанатизм и гонящую церковь; чтение «то есть Христос» в науке поддержки не имеет. Фразу о потопе самый ранний источник приписывает мадам де Помпадур, причём уже в 1756 году Мирабо называл её ходячей поговоркой. В ослабленных версиях все три утверждения устойчивы: кампания дехристианизации с публичным сожжением Библий, антихристианский смысл вольтеровского лозунга и беспечный фатализм двора Людовика XV документированы. Картину изменили бы находка в парламентских архивах конкретных актов 1793 и 1797 годов, письмо Вольтера с «l'infame» о самом Христе или прижизненная запись слов короля.

quantifier_audit: "Three years and a half later" (exact interval asserted)
"the same body" (identity of the legislature asserted)
"a resolution rescinding these decrees" (definite reference to a specific adopted legislative act)

components.component: The 3.5-year interval instantiates Rev 11's 'three days and a half' (day-year principle), with France as the power that slew the two witnesses (the Scriptures).
components.rationale: Prophetic identification; out of scope per §9. All in-scope findings below are conditional on not evaluating this premise: the adjudication rules on the calendar and legislative facts only, never on whether Rev 11 predicted them.

components.component: In 1797 a resolution was adopted rescinding the 1793 anti-religion/anti-Bible decrees and thereby 'granting toleration to the Scriptures'.
components.rationale: No such adopted resolution is identifiable. The Scriptures were never proscribed by national legislation, so no act 'granting toleration to the Scriptures' existed to adopt; the 1911 revision team's own research 'failed to disclose such specific legislation' at either end (Arthur L. White). The traditional endpoint, Camille Jordan's report of 17 June 1797, was a committee report, not an adopted resolution; the actual law of 24 Aug 1797 concerned refractory clergy, not the Bible, was passed by a different legislature, and was annulled eleven days later by the Fructidor coup. Kept at improbable rather than discredited only because a real, if short-lived, liberalization was voted in the summer of 1797 ('edicts... in effect').

components.component: The restoration came three and a half years after the 1793 decrees.
components.rationale: The interval is an endpoint-selection artifact. Documented counterexamples inside the alleged 3.5-year suppression window: the Convention's decree of 6 Dec 1793 reaffirming freedom of worship (~2 weeks after the Commune's church closures) and above all the law of 3 Ventose III (21 Feb 1795) establishing free exercise of all cults (~15 months after Nov 1793), followed by the return of church buildings on 30 May 1795. Nov 1793 -> June 1797 yields ~3 years 7 months only if both intervening laws are ignored and a reversed, non-durable 1797 liberalization is treated as 'the' restoration.

components.component: The rescinding resolution was adopted 'by the same body' that passed the 1793 decrees.
components.rationale: Structurally unsatisfiable against undisputed constitutional chronology. The National Convention sat Sept 1792 - 26 Oct 1795 (37 months): no 3.5-year interval fits within its life. Any 1797 act belongs to the Council of Five Hundred / Council of Ancients under the Constitution of Year III — a different body; indeed the mid-1797 liberalization was possible precisely because the March 1797 elections had changed the legislature's composition. Conversely, the one restorative act the Convention itself did adopt (21 Feb 1795) destroys the 3.5-year interval. Every assignment of referents falsifies at least one conjunct of the printed sentence.

rulings.weakened_version: The dechristianization campaign that peaked in late 1793 — with the Convention's acquiescence, church closures, and public Bible burnings — was followed within a few years by a staged legislative restoration of religious toleration in France (18 Sept 1794, 21 Feb 1795, and briefly June-Aug 1797).
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest published defense is Jan Voerman ('The Reign of Terror', AUSS 47/1, 2009: 117-134; earlier 'Ellen White and the French Revolution', AUSS), continuing George Croly's 1827 construction: the anti-religious terror ran from the measures of Nov 1793 to Camille Jordan's report on liberty of worship of 17 June 1797, which sustains White's 'three years and a half' in effect if not in statute. Engaged on its actual argument, it fails on evidence at three independent points: (1) the window is not continuous — the Convention reaffirmed freedom of worship on 6 Dec 1793 and legally restored free exercise on 21 Feb 1795, fifteen months in; (2) the chosen endpoint was neither a resolution nor durable — Jordan's report was never adopted as such, and the law of 24 Aug 1797 was annulled by the Fructidor coup on 4 Sept 1797 with persecution of clergy resuming to 1799; (3) no reading rescues 'the same body', since the Convention dissolved in Oct 1795. The concession the defense is owed is real and is carried by the weakened ruling: the 1793 assault on religion and the Scriptures happened, and a legislative toleration current did culminate in 1795-1797. Note also the admission against interest from the defending institution itself: Arthur L. White records that the 1911 revisers' 'painstaking research failed to disclose such specific legislation'. Voerman's full text was inaccessible (bot-protected repositories; local PDF rendering unavailable); his position was characterized from the article's documented abstract/snippets and its Croly-Smith lineage, and the structural refutation in component 4 is independent of any detail he could add. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the Phase-1 verifier's rationale (1795 law, endpoints interpretation-dependent) is incorporated and sharpened.

sources.ref: Assemblee nationale (official site), 'Boissy d'Anglas (21 fevrier 1795)' — text and context of the law of 3 Ventose III: freedom of worship, separation of church and state
sources.independence_note: French state institutional record of parliamentary history; independent of Anglo encyclopedic digests and of all confessional positions.
