rulings.would_change_this: For the genealogy label: the same evidence class as GC-33-099 (a documented Roman channel of incorporation). For the causal frame: a comparative study showing conditionalist bodies penetrated by Spiritualism at rates equal to non-conditionalist ones would weaken the receptivity component; archival evidence that non-conditionalist churches in practice lacked functioning anti-Spiritualist defenses would strengthen the exclusivity component and raise the as-written ruling.
rulings.notes: Phase-1: verifier ruled disputed, splitting genealogy (imprecise 'papal') from the 'only defense' claim (called theological/unverifiable); defense stage skipped (pass-through). This adjudication keeps the verifier's split, rules the genealogy label improbable on the shared dossier evidence, credits the receptivity mechanism as probable, and rules the historical layer of 'only' improbable while leaving the efficacy layer out of scope.

evidence_review: The dossier covers four D'Aubigne-mediated episodes of the Luther narrative. (1) Worms, 18 April 1521. Luther's substantive final answer -- popes and councils have erred, conscience captive to the word of God, 'I cannot and will not recant... God help me. Amen' -- is universally accepted; it survives in the official transcripts edited in Deutsche Reichstagsakten, Juengere Reihe II. The closing sentence 'Here I stand, I can do no other' is absent from the notarial on-the-spot records but present in the earliest Wittenberg printed edition of the speech (1521, weeks after the event), whence Melanchthon and the whole later tradition. The state of the question, per Samuel L. Young's peer-reviewed study 'How Luther Became the Mythical Here I Stand Hero' (Lutheran Quarterly 36, 2022) and the 500th-anniversary literature (Concordia Theology 2021; CPH; luther2017.de): for roughly half a century most scholars have held the words were 'probably not spoken', while conceding they may be genuine; Roland Bainton's standard biography defends possible authenticity ('the listeners at that moment may have been too moved to write'). Two contemporaneous witness classes conflict, and no scholar claims certainty: a genuine, unresolved dispute. (2) Scala Sancta. Luther verifiably climbed 'Pilate's staircase' on his Rome journey (redated 1511/12 by Hans Schneider, followed by Kaufmann, Moeller, Leppin, Koepf) seeking the indulgence, partly for his grandfather in purgatory; his own 1545 sermon recalls only doubt at the top ('Who knows whether it is true?'). The story that 'the just shall live by faith' came to him on the stairs rests on a single late, secondhand family source: his son Paul Luther's memorandum (preserved at Rudolstadt) of what his father narrated at table in 1544. Schaff (History of the Christian Church VII) reports and credits this attestation; modern specialist scholarship (Schneider; the De Gruyter volume 'Martin Luther in Rom', 2017; cf. Brecht vol. 1) doubts a reformatory awakening on the stairs and reads Paul's account as a harmonizing attempt to connect the later justification insight (dated by Luther's own 1545 preface to the Wittenberg years, c. 1518-19) with Rome. The thunder-voice, the springing up, and the flight 'in shame and horror' exist in no 16th-century source at all; they are D'Aubigne's dramatization, which GC compresses. (3) Peasants' War responsibility. What changed since the 19th century: Guenther Franz's older school read the revolt as a political struggle rooted in pre-Reformation traditions (Bundschuh, 'altes Recht'); Peter Blickle's The Revolution of 1525 established the now-standard view that the 1525 program was framed by the evangelical message ('godly law'; the Twelve Articles, co-drafted by the evangelical preacher Christoph Schappeler with Sebastian Lotzer, ground every demand in Scripture; peasants appealed to Luther's 'freedom of a Christian'), a line carried into Lyndal Roper's Summer of Fire and Blood (2025) and the OUP monograph Luther and the Peasants (2025). At the same time the whole field agrees Luther neither intended nor sanctioned revolt: the Admonition to Peace urged peace and blamed the lords too; Against the Robbing and Murdering Hordes condemned the rising; his two-kingdoms reading of Christian freedom excluded political insurrection ('a great and tragic misunderstanding' in the exculpatory literature: Kolb; McGath/OLL; the Saeculum article the Phase-1 defender cited). So the 19th-century flat exoneration ('no connection whatever', D'Aubigne) survives only in the intentional/doctrinal-warrant sense, not in the causal-contribution sense. (4) Muntzer's motives. The hostile portrait -- proud, ambitious, unwilling to be second -- descends from Luther's polemics and above all Melanchthon's Histori Thome Muntzers (1525), which scholarship (Abraham Friesen, Church History) treats as a humanist oratorical exemplar designed to show revolution as the natural fruit of Schwaermerei, not as history; D'Aubigne transmits exactly this frame ('dissatisfied with the inferior part he was playing'). Modern specialist biography (Tom Scott 1989; Hans-Juergen Goertz 1989/2015; Braeuer-Vogler 2016; Andrew Drummond 2024) grounds Muntzer's course in documented apocalyptic-mystical theology: his own Prague Manifesto (1521), Sermon to the Princes (1524), Vindication and Refutation (1524), and published correspondence (Matheson, Collected Works, 1988). The rivalry itself is undeniable and documented -- Muntzer refused subordination to Wittenberg and attacked Luther as 'Doctor Liar' and 'the soft-living flesh of Wittenberg' -- but the reading of it as careerist ambition for 'position and influence' has no documentary basis in Muntzer's corpus and is rejected as polemical caricature by the specialists.
narrative_ru: Досье объединяет четыре эпизода лютеровской истории, которые «Великая борьба» пересказывает по Д'Обинье. Знаменитая фраза «На сём стою» есть в первом печатном издании речи 1521 года, но отсутствует в протоколах рейхстага, и учёные до сих пор расходятся, произнёс ли её Лютер; суть его ответа в Вормсе подлинна. Сцена на Святой лестнице с «голосом как гром» и характеристика Мюнцера как честолюбца в записанном виде не выдержали проверки: первая опирается на позднее семейное предание, драматизированное Д'Обинье, вторая противоречит документированным апокалиптико-богословским мотивам самого Мюнцера. Абсолютное отрицание «малейшего основания» у обвинения Лютера в Крестьянской войне также расходится с современной историографией, хотя сам Лютер восстание осуждал и к нему не призывал. В ослабленных формулировках три из четырёх утверждений хорошо подтверждены, а картину изменили бы новые документы XVI века: независимое свидетельство о словах в Вормсе, ранняя запись эпизода на лестнице или письмо Мюнцера с карьерными мотивами.

components.component: Luther delivered a final answer at Worms refusing to retract, on the grounds that popes and councils have frequently erred and that his conscience is bound by the word of God, closing 'may God help me. Amen.'
components.rationale: The substance of the answer is preserved in the official transcripts (Deutsche Reichstagsakten JR II) and is accepted by the entire field; the GC wording is D'Aubigne's faithful English rendering (local file, lines 40834-40851), and no scholar disputes the content.

components.component: Luther spoke the specific closing sentence 'Here I stand, I can do no other.'
components.rationale: Two contemporaneous witness classes conflict: the notarial on-the-spot transcripts end at 'God help me. Amen' without the sentence, while the earliest Wittenberg printed edition (1521, within weeks) includes it. The majority position for c. 50 years is 'probably not spoken' (Young, Lutheran Quarterly 2022, traces the mythologization; Roper and Schilling treat it as legend-layer), but qualified scholars keep it open on the same evidence: Bainton's standard biography argues the listeners 'may have been too moved to write', and the print originates in circles that heard the speech. Neither side rests on superseded data or circularity; current evidence underdetermines the answer. This is the rare case where 'genuinely-open' is positively documented rather than a default.

rulings.weakened_version: Luther's final Worms answer as quoted is authentic in substance; the closing 'Here I stand, I can do no other' derives from the earliest 1521 printed edition of the speech and is absent from the official transcripts, so it may be an editorial addition rather than his spoken words.
rulings.minority_engagement: Both sides documented per section 1. Against verbatim authenticity: the transcript silence, weighed by most scholars since the mid-20th century as decisive for the verbatim record (state of the question per Young, LQ 2022; the anniversary scholarship of 2021). For authenticity: Bainton (Here I Stand, 1950) and a live minority note the phrase's presence in a contemporaneous print produced by people with access to eyewitnesses, and explain the transcript silence by the tumult at the close of the session. The Phase-1 defender argued exactly this two-contemporaneous-sources shape and is vindicated: the downgrade from anachronistic to disputed was correct, and this adjudication confirms the dispute is genuine rather than settled against the book. Note that D'Aubigne (and hence GC) did not invent the line; its genealogy runs to the 1521 print.

sources.ref: Samuel L. Young, 'How Luther Became the Mythical Here I Stand Hero', Lutheran Quarterly 36 (2022)
sources.independence_note: Peer-reviewed study of the phrase's textual history and reception; independent of Bainton's biographical tradition.

sources.ref: Roland Bainton, Here I Stand: A Life of Martin Luther (1950), on the earliest printed version ('may nevertheless be genuine... too moved to write')
sources.independence_note: Standard academic biography; the named minority position defending possible authenticity; independent reading of the same primary evidence.

sources.ref: Official Worms transcripts (Deutsche Reichstagsakten, Juengere Reihe II) as discussed in 'Martin Luther's Stand at Worms, Uncut' (Concordia Theology, 2021) and CPH 'Did Luther really say Here I stand?'
sources.independence_note: The transcript evidence itself; mediated by confessional-Lutheran academic outlets that nonetheless report the negative finding against their own tradition's icon, which strengthens credibility.

sources.ref: World History Encyclopedia, 'Luther's Speech at the Diet of Worms'
sources.independence_note: Digests Bainton for the authenticity discussion; genealogically dependent on Bainton, counted with him as one line.

sources.ref: D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation, b.7 ch.8 (local file daubigne-history-of-reformation-complete-1849.txt, lines 40834-40851)
sources.independence_note: Genealogy of GC's text: White reproduces D'Aubigne verbatim, who follows the printed-edition tradition.

rulings.would_change_this: An independent contemporaneous eyewitness record (a letter or diary of a Diet attendee, datable to 1521) either containing or explicitly denying the closing sentence would settle the wording question; short of such a find, the dispute stays open.
rulings.notes: Phase-1 defense engaged and adopted; this is the dossier's clearest vindication of the defender.

components.component: Luther devoutly climbed the Scala Sancta ('Pilate's staircase') on his knees in Rome, seeking the promised indulgence.
components.rationale: Attested by Luther himself (sermon of 15 September 1545: he climbed wishing to redeem his grandfather from purgatory and doubted at the top, 'Who knows whether it is true?'); accepted across the scholarship on the Rome journey (Schneider; the De Gruyter volume Martin Luther in Rom).

components.component: During the climb a voice like thunder seemed to say to him 'The just shall live by faith', whereupon he sprang to his feet and fled the place in shame and horror.
components.rationale: The only 16th-century root of the verse-on-the-stairs story is Paul Luther's late, secondhand memorandum (Rudolstadt) of his father's 1544 table narration, and even that account has the verse 'come into his mind', with no thunder-voice, no springing up, no flight in shame and horror; those elements enter the tradition with D'Aubigne's dramatization (local file, lines 12729-12743: 'a voice of thunder crying from the bottom of his heart... He flies far from the scene of his folly'). Luther's own recollections of the staircase record doubt about the indulgence, not a Romans-1:17 revelation, and his 1545 preface dates the justification breakthrough to the Wittenberg years. Modern specialist scholarship (Schneider and the scholars following him: Kaufmann, Moeller, Leppin, Koepf; cf. Brecht vol. 1) doubts any reformatory awakening on the stairs and reads Paul's account as harmonizing. Preponderance is clearly against the scene as narrated, but a well-attested family tradition and Schaff's credence keep a defense alive, so improbable rather than discredited.

rulings.weakened_version: Luther climbed the Scala Sancta seeking the indulgence and later viewed the act with shame; a 16th-century family tradition (Paul Luther's record of his father's 1544 table account) relates that the words 'the just shall live by faith' came to his mind on the stairs.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest opposing position: the Phase-1 defender, backed by Schaff (HCC VII), who reports the Paul Luther attestation and treats it as plausible testimony 'from the lips of his father in 1544'. The defender's three points: (1) the tradition predates D'Aubigne (16th-c. root, Rudolstadt letter) -- conceded, and it is why the weakened kernel is ruled genuinely-open rather than improbable; (2) GC's 'seemed to say to him' is phenomenological -- conceded as a softening, but the claim still narrates an experience-plus-flight event for which no early source exists in that form; (3) GC does not locate the full breakthrough on the stairs -- partially conceded (GC says 'from that time he saw more clearly than ever before'), yet the printed scene (thunder-voice, springing up, flight in shame and horror) is D'Aubigne's theater, absent from Paul Luther's own account, in tension with Luther's attested staircase memory (doubt, grandfather's purgatory), and rejected as a conversion scene by modern specialists. The defense rescues the kernel, not the scene as written.

sources.ref: Philip Schaff, History of the Christian Church VII (via CCEL), on the staircase incident and Paul Luther's 1544 testimony
sources.independence_note: Older standard scholarly reference; carries the pro-kernel position and documents the attestation's provenance; genealogically the same family-tradition line as D'Aubigne but critically aware of it.

sources.ref: Hans Schneider, 'Martin Luthers Reise nach Rom -- neu datiert und neu gedeutet' (Akademie der Wissenschaften zu Goettingen / De Gruyter, 2011); cf. the volume Martin Luther in Rom (De Gruyter, 2017) and its Sehepunkte review 18 (2018) nr. 11
sources.independence_note: Modern specialist scholarship on the Rome journey; independent of the Schaff/D'Aubigne tradition; source of the 1511/12 redating and of the skeptical reading of Paul Luther's harmonizing account.

sources.ref: Luther's sermon of 15 September 1545 ('Who knows whether it is true?', grandfather/purgatory motive) and his 1545 Latin preface (breakthrough dated to the Wittenberg years), as presented in the scholarship above and at luther2017.de ('Luthers Platz im Suendenpfuhl')
sources.independence_note: Luther's own late recollections, read through critical scholarship; genealogically independent of the Paul Luther family line.

sources.ref: Britannica, 'Martin Luther' (tower-experience dating)
sources.independence_note: Digest of the standard biographical scholarship (Brecht line); used only for the breakthrough-dating commonplace.

sources.ref: D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation (local file, lines 12709-12750)
sources.independence_note: Genealogy of GC's scene: the thunder-voice and the flight are D'Aubigne's additions to the family tradition; White compresses him.

rulings.would_change_this: An attestation of the staircase verse-experience earlier than or independent of Paul Luther's memorandum (e.g., a datable letter from Luther's circle before 1544) would move the kernel toward probable; a scholarly demonstration that the Rudolstadt memorandum is inauthentic or misdated would push the whole episode toward discredited.
rulings.notes: Phase-1 defense engaged: its provenance point is incorporated into the weakened ruling; its reading of GC's caution does not save the dramatized scene as printed.

components.component: Luther did not teach or sanction armed rebellion; he opposed the revolt's doctrines and condemned it (implicit substrate of the exculpation).
components.rationale: Documented in Luther's own 1525 writings (Admonition to Peace; Against the Robbing and Murdering Hordes) and unanimous in the scholarship: his two-kingdoms reading of Christian freedom excluded political insurrection, and he denounced the rising.

components.component: The charge that the rebellion was the fruit of Luther's doctrines was without any foundation whatsoever.
components.rationale: The absolute denial fails against documented counterexamples (section 6.3): the Twelve Articles ground the peasants' demands in Scripture and evangelical principle (co-drafted by the evangelical preacher Schappeler with Lotzer); the peasants explicitly invoked Luther's 'freedom of a Christian' and appealed to him by name; and the post-Blickle consensus (The Revolution of 1525; carried into Roper, Summer of Fire and Blood 2025, and OUP's Luther and the Peasants 2025) treats the evangelical message as a constitutive ingredient of the 1525 program. The charge therefore had real foundation in the causal-contribution sense, even though it fails in the intentional/doctrinal-warrant sense. One elastic reading survives (see minority engagement), which caps the ruling at improbable rather than discredited.

rulings.weakened_version: The charge that Luther intended or doctrinally sanctioned the peasants' rebellion was unfounded: he consistently distinguished spiritual from political freedom, urged peace, and condemned the revolt, which the rebels' leaders held against him.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest case for the claim as written: the Phase-1 defender's narrow-reading argument -- 'the charge' (per GC-10-065) is that the rebellion was the LEGITIMATE fruit of Luther's doctrines, i.e. that his teaching doctrinally warranted revolt, and modern scholarship indeed rejects that (the 'tragic misunderstanding' literature: Kolb, 'Luther on Peasants and Princes'; McGath/OLL; the Saeculum article) -- reinforced by the older Guenther Franz school (the revolt rooted in pre-Reformation political-legal traditions, Bundschuh and 'altes Recht') and by Blickle's own observations that peasant-rights ideas predated Luther and that his writings circulated more in towns than villages. Why this fails for the as-written wording: even on the narrow reading, 'without the SLIGHTEST foundation' overshoots, because the peasants' documented appeals to Luther's language gave the princes' charge visible foundation (the defender himself concedes the absolutist adverb is the claim's honest weakness); and the natural 19th-century sense of 'legitimate fruit' is causal ('natural offspring'), a proposition modern historiography substantially affirms rather than rejects. Franz's minimization of the Reformation factor is superseded on this specific point by the post-1975 consensus from Blickle to Roper. The defender's narrow reading is honored in full in the weakened version, which is well-supported.

sources.ref: Peter Blickle, The Revolution of 1525: The German Peasants' War from a New Perspective (Johns Hopkins UP), state of the question via published discussion/review
sources.independence_note: The field-defining monograph on the evangelical framing of the 1525 program ('godly law'); independent of the D'Aubigne/Protestant-apologetic genealogy of GC's claim.

sources.ref: Lyndal Roper, Summer of Fire and Blood: The German Peasants' War (2025), via Providence review; and Luther and the Peasants: Religion, Ritual, and the Revolt of 1525 (Oxford UP, 2025)
sources.independence_note: Current specialist scholarship, genealogically independent of Blickle's school; converges on the Reformation-entanglement of the revolt.

sources.ref: Twelve Articles of Memmingen (1525) and Luther's Admonition to Peace / Against the Robbing and Murdering Hordes, read through the above scholarship and standard encyclopedic digests (Wikipedia 'Twelve Articles', 'German Peasants' War'; World History Encyclopedia)
sources.independence_note: Primary texts establishing both the evangelical grounding of the peasant program (against the absolute) and Luther's condemnation of the revolt (for the weakened version).

sources.ref: Robert Kolb, 'Luther on Peasants and Princes' (Lutheran Quarterly); McGath, 'The Peasants' War and Martin Luther' (OLL); Saeculum journal article on Luther and the gospel of social unrest
sources.independence_note: The exculpatory 'tragic misunderstanding' line, partly confessional-Lutheran in location; weighed as the named counter-position, and it carries the weakened version.

sources.ref: Guenther Franz, Der deutsche Bauernkrieg (older standard), via historiographical surveys
sources.independence_note: Older school minimizing the Reformation factor; superseded on this point by Blickle and successors but documents that qualified scholarship once leaned closer to GC's framing.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed synthesis demonstrating that the 1525 program formed independently of evangelical preaching (e.g., establishing that the Twelve Articles' scriptural framing was a late instrumental veneer over purely agrarian-legal demands) would rehabilitate the as-written denial; conversely, evidence that Luther privately encouraged resistance would destroy even the weakened version, and nothing of the kind exists.
rulings.notes: Phase-1 defense engaged: its narrow reading is adopted as the weakened version and ruled well-supported; its own concession on 'not the slightest' is decisive for the as-written ruling. Period-standard genealogy confirmed: D'Aubigne's exculpation (local file, lines 62073-62078) is the claim's source layer.

components.component: Muntzer was driven by ambition to obtain position and influence (motive attribution).
components.rationale: No document from Muntzer's hand evidences office- or status-seeking as his motive; the contemporary attributions of pride and ambition come from his adversaries (Luther's polemics; Melanchthon's Histori Thome Muntzers, 1525), which scholarship treats as hostile genre pieces rather than history (Friesen, Church History: Melanchthon composed it as a humanist oratorical exemplar showing revolution as the natural fruit of Schwaermerei). The rival motive account -- apocalyptic-mystical theology, election of the Spirit-taught, imminent judgment -- has direct documentary support in Muntzer's own corpus (Prague Manifesto 1521, Sermon to the Princes 1524, Vindication and Refutation 1524, published letters; Matheson's Collected Works 1988; Franz's critical edition 1968) and carries the specialist consensus (Scott 1989; Goertz 1989/2015; Braeuer-Vogler 2016; Drummond 2024). Under the amended section 3 symmetry rule the undocumented motive attribution caps at improbable because the rival account is documentary; on the merits the preponderance independently supports the same ruling.

components.component: Muntzer was unwilling to be second, even to Luther (characterization of his stance toward Wittenberg).
components.rationale: The behavioral core is documented in Muntzer's own writings: he refused subordination to Wittenberg, claimed a divine commission superseding Luther's reform, and attacked Luther personally ('Doctor Liar', 'the soft-living flesh of Wittenberg'). As a status idiom ('unwilling to be second') the phrase glosses this documented refusal in rivalry terms that Muntzer himself framed theologically, so it is probable as a characterization rather than well-supported.

rulings.weakened_version: Muntzer assertively refused any position subordinate to Luther, claiming a divinely commissioned reform superior to Wittenberg's, and contemporaries beginning with Luther and Melanchthon read this self-assertion as ambition.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest opposing (pro-claim) positions. (a) The Phase-1 defender's three arguments: (1) the sentence sits in a paragraph that immediately supplies Muntzer's theology (GC-10-055/056), so White lists ambition alongside doctrine, not instead of it -- answered: adjudication rules on the sentence as printed, and the printed sentence asserts a determinate psychological motive that the documentary record does not support; accurate theology nearby does not document the ambition motive. (2) Internal consistency with GC-10-052/053, which Phase 1 marked 'supported' as subjective temperament judgments -- answered: Phase-1 verdicts bind nothing here (charter section 0.2), and 054 differs in kind: 'ambitious to obtain position and influence' is a checkable motive attribution contradicted by specialist biography, not a vague temperament remark. (3) The behavioral core (refusal to be second) is confirmed by modern scholarship -- conceded in full; that is exactly the weakened version, ruled well-supported. (b) The older historiographical line descending from Melanchthon's Histori through 19th-century Protestant historiography (D'Aubigne: 'dissatisfied with the inferior part he was playing'), for which the rivalry-as-driver reading was standard -- answered: the fact of the rupture is undeniable, but the ambition reading fails on genre and bias per section 4.2 (its documentary base is adversary polemic, so read by Friesen and the specialists), and it is superseded by work on Muntzer's own corpus. Confessional location of any party plays no role in these dismissals; the polemics fail as motive evidence because of genre, not because Luther and Melanchthon were parties to the conflict per se.

sources.ref: Tom Scott, Thomas Muntzer: Theology and Revolution in the German Reformation (1989)
sources.independence_note: Anglophone specialist biography grounding Muntzer's course in theology and social context; independent of the German radical-Reformation school.

sources.ref: Hans-Juergen Goertz, Thomas Muntzer: Apocalyptic Mystic and Revolutionary (Eng. 1993; rev. German 2015)
sources.independence_note: German specialist tradition, genealogically independent of Scott; converges on apocalyptic-mystical motivation.

sources.ref: Abraham Friesen, 'Philipp Melanchthon, Wilhelm Zimmermann and the Dilemma of Muntzer Historiography', Church History (Cambridge)
sources.independence_note: Peer-reviewed historiographical study establishing the genre and polemical purpose of Melanchthon's Histori, the fountainhead of the ambition portrait GC inherits via D'Aubigne.

sources.ref: Muntzer's own writings and letters (Prague Manifesto 1521; Sermon to the Princes 1524; Vindication and Refutation 1524; Matheson, Collected Works 1988; Franz critical edition 1968)
sources.independence_note: The documentary base of the rival motive account, read through the specialist editions; also documents the behavioral core of the weakened version (refusal of subordination, attacks on Luther).

sources.ref: Andrew Drummond, The Dreadful History and Judgement of God on Thomas Muntzer (2024), via The Nation and Cleveland Review of Books reviews; Duke UP History of the Present 15/1 (2025) special-issue introduction
sources.independence_note: Current reassessment literature; used as convergent positions on the reception history, not as arbiters.

sources.ref: D'Aubigne, History of the Reformation (local file, lines 62215-62233: 'dissatisfied with the inferior part he was playing')
sources.independence_note: Genealogy of GC's sentence: White's 'unwilling to be second, even to Luther' renders D'Aubigne's phrase; the ambition frame is period-standard, matching the Phase-1 anachronistic diagnosis.

rulings.would_change_this: Publication of a document from Muntzer's hand or immediate circle evidencing office- or status-seeking as his operative motive (e.g., correspondence negotiating rank or precedence vis-a-vis Wittenberg) would rehabilitate the as-written motive; a specialist re-evaluation of Melanchthon's Histori as reliable testimony would also move it toward genuinely-open.
rulings.notes: Motive-cap arithmetic per amended section 3: rival account documented in Muntzer's own corpus, so the improbable cap applies; the ruling also holds on preponderance alone.

evidence_review: The dossier's shared question is the 'paganization of Christianity' thesis: did the end of persecution and the Constantinian settlement turn the church into a hybrid of Christianity and paganism, with images, saints, and purgatory as relabeled pagan cult, and with the Bible displaced as the standard of faith? The 19th-century Protestant historiography White used (Wylie, D'Aubigné, Dowling's History of Romanism 1845 — local copy — which calls purgatory 'the pagan doctrine... which he called Purgatory') asserted all of this as settled fact; the 1911 GC appendix note on purgatory cites the same genealogy (Elliott, Hagenbach). Modern scholarship splits the question. (1) CONVERSION QUALITY: the fact core is secure. Ramsay MacMullen (Christianizing the Roman Empire, Yale UP 1984; Christianity and Paganism in the Fourth to Eighth Centuries, Yale UP 1997) documents that post-Constantinian conversion was driven heavily by advantage and later coercion, that pagans were still 'a good half of the population' in AD 400, and that pagan acts and practices entered the church with its converts and persisted for centuries; Eusebius himself (Vita Constantini 4.54, local NPNF2-01) complains of 'the scandalous hypocrisy of those who crept into the church' under imperial favor, and HE 8.1 describes laxity and hypocrisy breeding during the long peace before Diocletian. (2) THE FRAME: whether this amounts to paganism 'conquering' or 'uniting with' Christianity is exactly where the field divides. MacMullen is the strongest academic ally of the book's reading (assimilation so massive that medieval Christianity is an amalgam). Against him, Peter Brown (The Cult of the Saints, U Chicago P 1981) dismantled the 'two-tier' model on which the pagan-survival reading rests, showing the cult of saints as an elite-led, internally Christian development structurally unlike pagan cult; R. A. Markus (The End of Ancient Christianity, CUP 1990) reclassified most 'pagan survivals' as the secular being slowly christianized; David Frankfurter (Christianizing Egypt, Princeton UP 2018) documents massive continuity of gesture (festivals, incubation, healing shrines redirected to saints) while rejecting 'pagan survival' as a category — the practices were re-steered to Christian patrons within a new religious system. The honest state of the question: practice-level continuity and nominal mass conversion are established; 'paganism became the conqueror / a union was formed' is a characterization no current specialist wing sustains as written, though MacMullen comes closest. (3) IMAGES AND RELICS: relic veneration begins inside the martyr cult before Constantine (Martyrdom of Polycarp 18, local ANF01: bones 'more precious than the most exquisite jewels' — 2nd century on the traditional dating; Moss and Ehrman would move the practice after 200, still pre-Constantinian), so it was not 'introduced' to accommodate 4th-century converts. Image veneration proper is late: Kitzinger ('The Cult of Images in the Age before Iconoclasm', Dumbarton Oaks Papers 8, 1954) finds the first hints of proskynesis before images only in the early 6th century and the devotional efflorescence in the late 6th–7th. The motive record is mixed and partly documentary: Paulinus of Nola (Carmen 27, c. 400) states his purpose of using paintings at Felix's shrine to occupy and instruct rustic ex-pagan crowds; Gregory the Great's letter to Mellitus (Register 11.56, AD 601, via Bede HE 1.30; Oxford Cult of Saints database E06424) documents a deliberate substitution strategy — keep the temples, install relics, replace sacrificial feasts with martyr festivals 'so that they may more readily respond to inward joys'; but the same Gregory, writing to Serenus of Marseilles, defended images strictly as 'books of the illiterate' and ordered that adoration of them be prevented. So deliberate accommodation is documented for festivals, temples, and didactic images; deliberate introduction of image/relic ADORATION to ease conversion is not, and the hierarchy's documented stance ran the other way. (4) PURGATORY: Jacques Le Goff (The Birth of Purgatory, U Chicago P 1984) established that purgatorium as noun and place crystallizes in the late 12th century as a Latin-Christian construction; subsequent revision (Peter Brown's 7th-century watershed; Isabel Moreira, Heaven's Purge, OUP 2010) pushes the formation earlier but makes it more, not less, internally Christian, growing from prayer for the dead (2 Macc 12; Perpetua; Tertullian; Cyprian; Augustine's purgatorial fire). Greco-Roman purification eschatology (Plato, Virgil) contributed conceptual material — Alan Bernstein (The Formation of Hell, Cornell UP 1993) maps that contribution — but no specialist derives the doctrine from a pagan institution. On its use: fear-preaching, exempla, and the indulgence economy are documented (Le Goff), while Eamon Duffy (The Stripping of the Altars, Yale UP 1992) documents late-medieval purgatory piety as hope, consolation, and solidarity with the dead; 'employed to terrify' as the doctrine's function is therefore a genuinely divided question between documented pictures. (5) SCRIPTURE AS STANDARD: for GC-02-053 the counterexample base spans the whole implied period — Irenaeus (AH 3.1.1, local ANF01: the Scriptures handed down 'to be the ground and pillar of our faith'), Athanasius arguing against the Arians from Scripture's sufficiency, Vincent of Lérins (Commonitorium 2: the canon 'sufficient of itself for everything'), through the medieval magister in sacra pagina whose theology 'properly uses Sacred Scripture as the sole authority for certain and incontrovertible arguments' (Aquinas, ST I q.1 a.8; MDPI Religions 16/7:845). Heiko Oberman's Tradition I/II framework (The Harvest of Medieval Theology) — the standard scholarly account — holds that the early church treated Scripture as the sole source of revelation interpreted within the rule of faith, with a two-source concept emerging from the 4th century and maturing in the 12th; even the Catholic position (Tavard, Holy Writ or Holy Church, 1959) affirms Scripture's accepted authority while locating discernment in the church. What no wing of scholarship sustains is the flat statement that the Bible 'was not accepted as the standard of faith'. Net finding for the dossier: fact components largely stand (some at well-supported), totalizing frame and motive components fall, and the framing gap — not factual fabrication — is the dossier's signature.
narrative_ru: Это досье разбирает картину «константиновского падения» церкви: слияние христианства с язычеством после прекращения гонений, подмену идолов образами святых и утрату Библии как мерила веры. Фактическое ядро подтвердилось: исследователи (Макмаллен, Браун, Франкфуртер) признают массовый приток поверхностно обращённых язычников и переход многих функций языческого культа к культу святых. Тотальные формулировки книги проверку не прошли: тезисы о «победившем язычестве», о большинстве христиан, понизивших свой уровень, и о чистилище как «изобретении язычества» расходятся с балансом специальных исследований, а слова о том, что Библию не признавали мерилом веры, опровергаются текстами отцов церкви и средневековых богословов. В ослабленных версиях семь из восьми утверждений получили оценку «хорошо подтверждено», ещё одно «вероятно», поэтому главная находка досье состоит в разрыве между риторикой и фактической основой. Картину изменили бы документы эпохи, показывающие сознательное введение поклонения образам ради привлечения язычников или официальный отказ церкви той эпохи от авторитета Писания.

quantifier_audit: was not accepted (flat categorical negation over an undated period)

components.component: In the church of the period ch. 2 ¶17 describes (post-persecution era onward), the Bible was not accepted as the standard of faith.
components.rationale: Well-provenanced counterexamples span the entire implied period: Irenaeus, AH 3.1.1 (Scriptures handed down 'to be the ground and pillar of our faith'; local anf01 ~line 38979); Athanasius arguing the Arian controversy from Scripture on both sides; Vincent of Lérins, Commonitorium 2 (the canon 'sufficient of itself for everything, and more than sufficient'); the medieval university's magister in sacra pagina and Aquinas's rule that theology argues properly only from canonical Scripture (ST I q.1 a.8). Councils of the era defended creeds precisely as scriptural. Per §6.3, multiple independent undisputed counterexamples to a categorical negative yield discredited.

components.component: Scripture did not function as the sole and final standard: doctrine was governed by Scripture as interpreted through tradition and church authority, and lay access was mediated.
components.rationale: This is what the evidence actually shows (Oberman's Tradition I giving way to a two-source Tradition II from the 4th century, mature by the 12th; Tavard's Catholic account agreeing that the church controlled discernment and interpretation). It is recorded here to make explicit what survives of the claim; it is the weakened version, not the printed sentence.

rulings.weakened_version: In the post-Constantinian and medieval church, Scripture did not function as the sole and final standard of faith: doctrine and practice were governed by Scripture as interpreted through tradition and ecclesial authority, and direct lay access to the Bible was limited and mediated.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case for the sentence as written is the Protestant/Adventist functional reading: extra-scriptural doctrines multiplied, tradition became a second source of revelation (Oberman's own Tradition II), and lay Bible reading was restricted — therefore, in practice, the Bible was 'not the standard'. The reading is answered by its own sources: Oberman's framework holds that Scripture remained the acknowledged supreme norm throughout — what changed was whether tradition was a second source beside it; the period's theologians, councils, and even its polemicists on all sides argued from Scripture as the decisive authority (Athanasius vs the Arians; Aquinas's sola canonica scriptura in argument). The functional displacement of exclusive scriptural authority is real and is exactly the weakened version; it cannot carry the flat negation as printed. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's Oberman-based rationale is incorporated and sharpened here.

sources.ref: Irenaeus, Adversus Haereses 3.1.1, in ANF vol. 1 (local scholarship/anf01-ante-nicene-fathers-vol1.txt, ~line 38979)
sources.independence_note: Primary text in Schaff's scholarly edition; independent of all modern secondary lines cited.

sources.ref: H. Oberman, Tradition I / Tradition II framework (The Harvest of Medieval Theology, Harvard UP 1963), as documented in current secondary discussion
sources.independence_note: Standard academic account of scriptural authority and tradition; genealogically independent of the patristic primaries and of Aquinas scholarship.

sources.ref: 'What Is Scripture for Thomas Aquinas?', Religions (MDPI) 16/7:845 (2025), and sacra pagina scholarship on Aquinas (ST I q.1 a.8)
sources.independence_note: Medievalist literature on Aquinas; independent of Oberman's Reformation-studies genealogy.

sources.ref: G. Tavard, Holy Writ or Holy Church (1959)
sources.independence_note: Catholic scholarly position; cited to show even the Catholic side affirms Scripture's accepted authority in the period — convergence from an opposed confessional location.

sources.ref: Vincent of Lérins, Commonitorium 2; Athanasius, Contra Gentes 1 / Orationes contra Arianos
sources.independence_note: Standard patristic texts in critical editions; in-period (5th and 4th c.) counterexamples independent of Irenaeus.

rulings.would_change_this: Documented evidence that the mainstream church of the period formally denied Scripture's normative status — e.g., conciliar or papal acta of the era rejecting scriptural warrant as the doctrinal norm, or a demonstrable body of period theology that argued doctrine without claiming scriptural authority.
rulings.notes: Straggler search spent here (medieval sacra pagina). The sentence is undated in the text; the ruling covers every candidate period, so imprecision of dating cannot rescue it.

quantifier_audit: as long as persecution continued (universal temporal)

components.component: Once persecution ceased and imperial favor began, converts joined who were on average less sincere and devoted.
components.rationale: MacMullen (Christianizing the Roman Empire, Yale UP 1984) documents post-Constantinian conversion driven by advantage and later coercion rather than piety; Eusebius, VC 4.54 (local npnf2-01, ~line 52409) complains contemporaneously of 'the scandalous hypocrisy of those who crept into the church' under Constantine's favor. Independent modern and primary lines converge.

components.component: While persecution continued the church remained comparatively pure (higher average commitment under costly, voluntary membership).
components.rationale: The comparative claim (pre-313 average commitment vs post-313 mass influx) is favored by the balance of assessment: membership was voluntary and costly, with a screening catechumenate; MacMullen's contrast presupposes it. Complications keep it below well-supported: the Decian lapsi crisis (Cyprian, De Lapsis) and Eusebius HE 8.1 (local npnf2-01, ~line 41208: laxity, quarrels, and 'monstrous hypocrisy' bred during the long peace before Diocletian) show that purity tracked the cost of membership, which fluctuated during intra-period intervals of peace — the strict temporal universal is too smooth, though the on/off pattern actually confirms the underlying ease-breeds-laxity mechanism.

components.component: The way was opened for Satan to obtain a foothold.
components.rationale: Supernatural agency within the cosmic-conflict frame; out of scope per §9. The claim-level ruling is conditional on setting this component aside, which this adjudication does not evaluate.

rulings.weakened_version: The pre-Constantinian church's costly, voluntary membership was on average more committed than the mass of converts drawn in by imperial favor after 312, whose influx diluted standards of commitment.
rulings.minority_engagement: Not required for a probable ruling. Noted for completeness: the sharpest counter-reading (persecution-era church already lax between persecutions — Cyprian, Eusebius HE 8.1) does not deny the comparative claim; it refines its chronology, and Eusebius's own explanation of the Diocletianic persecution as judgment on peacetime laxity runs in the same direction as White's mechanism. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's MacMullen-based rationale is incorporated.

sources.ref: R. MacMullen, Christianizing the Roman Empire (A.D. 100-400), Yale UP 1984
sources.independence_note: Modern academic monograph built deliberately on non-Christian and documentary evidence; independent of the patristic apologetic tradition.

sources.ref: Eusebius, Vita Constantini 4.54 and HE 8.1, NPNF II/1 (local scholarship/npnf2-01..., ~lines 52409, 41208)
sources.independence_note: Contemporary insider testimony in a scholarly edition; pro-Constantinian bias makes its complaint about hypocritical converts evidence against interest.

sources.ref: Cyprian, De Lapsis (Decian crisis)
sources.independence_note: 3rd-century primary line independent of Eusebius; used to test the temporal universal.

rulings.would_change_this: A quantitative or prosopographic study showing pre-313 membership no more committed than post-313 (systematic evidence of mass nominal adherence under persecution conditions) would push the comparative frame to genuinely-open; further documentary corpora of opportunistic post-312 conversion would raise it to well-supported.
rulings.notes: One of two claims in the dossier moving toward the book (§7 symmetry).

components.component: After persecution ended, pagans entered the church accepting Christianity partially, retaining pagan beliefs and practices.
components.rationale: MacMullen (Christianity and Paganism, Yale UP 1997, 'Assimilation' chapter) documents pagan acts and practices received into the church with its converts and persisting for centuries; Frankfurter (Christianizing Egypt, Princeton UP 2018) documents the same continuity of practice while reinterpreting its meaning; the evidentiary base is largely clergy (Augustine, Caesarius) condemning such practices among the baptized — converging independent lines.

components.component: These converts thereby 'rejected other essential truths' of the faith.
components.rationale: Directionally matches the scholarship on nominal conversion (minimal doctrinal formation, adherence without internalization — MacMullen's central point), but sharpens non-internalization into deliberate rejection and imports a confessional measure of which truths are 'essential'. The balance favors the substance; the precision overstates it.

rulings.weakened_version: Many pagan converts of the post-Constantinian era adopted Christianity superficially, retaining pagan beliefs and practices without internalizing the faith's doctrinal and moral core.
rulings.minority_engagement: Not required for a probable ruling. The Frankfurter/Markus objection — that 'partial adoption' wrongly imagines a pure Christianity being diluted, when local appropriation was how Christianization worked everywhere — is noted; it targets the interpretive category, not the documented phenomenon of shallow mass conversion, and so lowers precision rather than direction. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's rationale is incorporated.

sources.ref: R. MacMullen, Christianity and Paganism in the Fourth to Eighth Centuries, Yale UP 1997 (reviews: Project MUSE; Klingshirn in Catholic Historical Review)
sources.independence_note: The strongest academic ally of the claim's substance; peer-reviewed university-press monograph.

sources.ref: D. Frankfurter, Christianizing Egypt, Princeton UP 2018 (BMCR 2019.11.07 review)
sources.independence_note: Independent regional evidence base (Egypt); confirms practice-continuity while contesting the 'partial adoption' framing — cited for both directions.

sources.ref: Eusebius, VC 4.54 (local npnf2-01)
sources.independence_note: Contemporary testimony to insincere accession, independent of modern lines.

rulings.would_change_this: Regional studies of the 4th-5th-century catechumenate demonstrating deep doctrinal formation at mass scale would weaken the claim; conversely, further documentation of unformed mass adherence would raise it to well-supported as written.
rulings.notes: Second of two claims moving toward the book (§7 symmetry).

quantifier_audit: at last (marks a culminating collective event)

components.component: The post-Constantinian church absorbed large numbers of superficially converted pagans, and pagan practice became embedded in church life.
components.rationale: Common ground of the field: MacMullen (both monographs) and Frankfurter document mass conversion of convenience and centuries-long persistence of pagan practice among the baptized; MacMullen notes pagans were still 'a good half of the population' in AD 400, so much of the church's late-4th-century membership was necessarily recent and shallowly formed.

components.component: 'Most of the Christians at last consented to lower their standard' — a majority of existing Christians made a culminating collective compromise.
components.rationale: No evidence base can reach the interior consent of a majority of 4th-century Christians, and no specialist describes a collective standard-lowering decision; the mainstream account — gradual, regionally uneven acculturation with continuous clerical resistance (the very sermons and canons MacMullen mines) — is itself well documented. The burden rule (§6.2) requires population-level evidence for a majority claim; none exists, and the documented pattern contradicts the culminating-event shape ('at last consented').

components.component: 'A union was formed between Christianity and paganism.'
components.rationale: MacMullen's assimilation thesis (Yale UP, peer-reviewed) comes close: the two systems 'flowed together' over four centuries, and medieval Christianity received massive pagan material. Brown, Markus, and Frankfurter, on comparable evidential quality, reject 'union' as the wrong category: pagan gesture was re-steered into a new, internally coherent Christian system. Qualified specialists disagree on the characterization, not on the data, and the split does not reduce to confession.

rulings.weakened_version: In the post-Constantinian era the church absorbed large numbers of superficially converted pagans, and much pagan custom and attitude became embedded in Christian practice, with average standards of commitment falling.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest published position for the claim is MacMullen's (Christianity and Paganism, 1997): assimilation so extensive that the church's 'triumph' was 'one not of obliteration but of widening embrace and assimilation'. The engagement cuts both ways: MacMullen's account genuinely supports the weakened version, and it is why the weakened ruling is well-supported; but even MacMullen describes a four-century gradient of practice-level absorption, not a majority of Christians consenting 'at last' to a union — and his framing is contested at comparable quality by Brown (two-tier critique), Markus (christianization of the secular), and Frankfurter (appropriation, not merger). The as-written sentence overshoots its best academic ally. Period note: the 'union' formulation is standard in White's sources (Wylie, D'Aubigné; cf. Dowling locally) — a period_standard flag is appropriate at synthesis. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's split rationale is incorporated.

sources.ref: R. MacMullen, Christianity and Paganism in the Fourth to Eighth Centuries, Yale UP 1997
sources.independence_note: Strongest ally position; independent documentary base (sermons, canons, archaeology).

sources.ref: P. Brown, The Cult of the Saints, U Chicago P 1981
sources.independence_note: Independent of MacMullen; founds the modern critique of the pagan-infiltration model.

sources.ref: R. A. Markus, The End of Ancient Christianity, CUP 1990 (via Whelan, Early Medieval Europe 29, 2021)
sources.independence_note: Independent line reframing 'pagan survivals'; carried by current specialist literature.

sources.ref: D. Frankfurter, Christianizing Egypt, Princeton UP 2018
sources.independence_note: Independent regional evidence; explicitly criticizes the syncretism-as-merger category the claim asserts.

rulings.would_change_this: A documented church-wide act of the era relaxing standards (a council or imperial-ecclesial decree instituting accommodation), or a peer-reviewed demographic argument that a majority of existing Christians measurably abandoned prior norms, would lift the as-written ruling; a field shift vindicating MacMullen's strongest reading over Brown/Markus/Frankfurter would move the 'union' component to probable.

quantifier_audit: only changing the objects of their worship (exclusive 'only')

components.component: Pagan converts who joined the church continued pagan-style devotional practice.
components.rationale: Documented across regions: festivals, incubation at shrines, healing cult, ex-votos, amulets redirected to saints (MacMullen 1997; Frankfurter 2018 on Egypt: festivals, dance, and incubation re-steered 'toward Christian saints').

components.component: Their veneration of Jesus, Mary, and the saints was idolatry with relabeled objects — 'only changing the objects of their worship'.
components.rationale: The exclusive 'only' asserts that nothing changed but the labels. Brown (Cult of the Saints) demonstrated structural discontinuities: the cult was led by bishops and elites, not a vulgar substrate; it inverted pagan mortuary distancing (tombs brought inside the city and altar); it ran on intercession and patronage theology foreign to the cults it displaced. Frankfurter, the closest thing to a continuity ally, still holds the practices were incorporated into a new religious system rather than continuing under new names. The balance of specialist assessment is against relabeling-only.

components.component: The conversion-era generation (the narrative's 4th-century setting) directed worship to images of Jesus, Mary, and the saints.
components.rationale: Chronological telescoping. Kitzinger (DOP 8, 1954) finds the first literary hint of proskynesis before church images only in the early 6th century (Hypatius of Ephesus dossier) and dates the devotional efflorescence to the late 6th-7th centuries; developed Marian image devotion is later still (post-Ephesus 431 for the title cult, later for image practice). Saint- and relic-cult were 4th-century realities (Brown), but image-directed worship among the first post-Constantinian converts is roughly two to three centuries early.

rulings.weakened_version: Pagan converts carried devotional habits into the church, and the developing cult of saints — and, centuries later, of images — absorbed many functions previously served by pagan cult (healing, festival, local patronage).
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest position for the claim: MacMullen (1997) reads saints' shrines taking over healing, incubation, and festival functions as pagan religiosity surviving under Christian labels, and Frankfurter documents exactly such functional transfer in Egypt. Engagement: both authors' own accounts defeat the exclusive 'only' — MacMullen describes transformation-with-assimilation across four centuries, not instant relabeling by the conversion generation, and Frankfurter explicitly rejects the 'pagan survival' reading of the transferred gestures. Brown's structural argument (elite leadership; inversion of pagan corpse-avoidance; intercession theology) stands unrefuted as evidence that more than the objects changed. The functional-transfer core is therefore credited in the weakened ruling; the relabeling-only sentence is not. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's telescoping point is confirmed against Kitzinger and incorporated.

sources.ref: P. Brown, The Cult of the Saints, U Chicago P 1981
sources.independence_note: Foundational monograph directly on the substitution question; independent of MacMullen and Kitzinger.

sources.ref: E. Kitzinger, 'The Cult of Images in the Age before Iconoclasm', Dumbarton Oaks Papers 8 (1954), JSTOR 1291064
sources.independence_note: Art-historical line, independent of the social-history genealogy; carries the chronology.

sources.ref: R. MacMullen, Christianity and Paganism, Yale UP 1997
sources.independence_note: Ally position for functional transfer; engaged per §5.

sources.ref: D. Frankfurter, Christianizing Egypt, Princeton UP 2018
sources.independence_note: Independent regional documentation of gesture-transfer; rejects the relabeling category.

rulings.would_change_this: Datable 4th-century evidence of widespread adoration directed at images of Jesus, Mary, or the saints (literary or archaeological) would remove the telescoping and reopen the as-written ruling; a refutation of Brown's structural discontinuities in the specialist literature would move the frame component toward genuinely-open.

components.component: Paganism was publicly vanquished (suppressed as the empire's public religion) while pagan customs and elements entered the church.
components.rationale: Both halves are secure: Theodosian-era legislation ended public pagan cult, and the entry of pagan customs into church practice is documented (MacMullen 1997; the sibling claim GC-03-010 was already ruled supported in Phase 1).

components.component: Paganism 'became the conqueror'; 'her spirit controlled the church'.
components.rationale: Totalizing characterization ruled against the balance of specialist assessment. Even MacMullen, the strongest academic ally, describes assimilation into a triumphant Christianity that eliminated paganism as a system — and his evidence base consists largely of clergy condemning pagan survivals, i.e., the teaching church resisting rather than embodying the 'spirit of paganism'. The church's doctrinal core (canon, creeds, Trinitarian controversy, episcopate) developed on internal Christian-Jewish logic. Brown, Markus, and Frankfurter reject the conquest framing outright. The 'vanquished became the conqueror' paradox descends from 19th-century Protestant historiography (cf. Dowling locally), not from any current specialist position.

rulings.weakened_version: Though paganism was publicly defeated, much pagan practice and attitude survived within Christianity and deeply shaped popular Christian religion for centuries.
rulings.minority_engagement: MacMullen (Christianity and Paganism, Yale UP 1997) is the named strongest opposing position to this ruling: he argues the two systems interacted far longer and more deeply than older scholarship allowed, and that medieval Christianity absorbed pagan material wholesale — reviewers (Klingshirn, CHR; Frend, Theology) took the argument seriously. Engagement: MacMullen's thesis sustains the weakened sentence, and this ruling credits it there; it does not sustain 'controlled', because the control he documents runs the other way (church authorities legislating and preaching against the practices), and his sharp Christian/pagan dichotomy is precisely what Markus and Frankfurter showed to be unstable. The as-written frame therefore fails on the ally's own evidence. Period note: matches White's sources; period_standard flag appropriate at synthesis. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's rationale is incorporated.

sources.ref: R. MacMullen, Christianity and Paganism in the Fourth to Eighth Centuries, Yale UP 1997
sources.independence_note: Ally position engaged per §5; own documentary base.

sources.ref: P. Brown, The Cult of the Saints, U Chicago P 1981
sources.independence_note: Independent; dismantles the infiltration model underlying 'conqueror'.

sources.ref: R. A. Markus, The End of Ancient Christianity, CUP 1990; D. Frankfurter, Christianizing Egypt, Princeton UP 2018
sources.independence_note: Two further independent lines converging against the conquest characterization.

sources.ref: J. Dowling, History of Romanism (1845), local scholarship copy
sources.independence_note: Period anchor only: same 19th-century Protestant genealogy as White's text; not evidence for or against.

rulings.would_change_this: A specialist consensus shift endorsing the strong paganization reading — e.g., new monographs re-establishing MacMullen's sharpest formulation against the Brown/Markus/Frankfurter critique — would move the frame to genuinely-open; evidence that church teaching authority adopted rather than fought pagan cult norms would move it further.

components.component: The adoration of images and relics entered Christian worship gradually.
components.rationale: 'Gradually' is exactly what the scholarship shows: martyr-cult relics from the 2nd-3rd centuries, elite-led saint cult in the 4th (Brown), didactic and commemorative images from the 4th-5th, devotional image practice intensifying late 6th-7th (Kitzinger), conciliar settlement in 787.

components.component: Image and relic veneration was introduced as a substitute for idol worship (origin mechanism).
components.rationale: Relic veneration originates inside the martyr cult before any convert-accommodation problem existed: Martyrdom of Polycarp 18 (local anf01, ~line 4048; 2nd century on the traditional dating, after 200 on Moss/Ehrman's skeptical dating — pre-Constantinian either way). Brown shows the saint cult as an internally Christian, bishop-led development. Images entered first as decoration and 'books of the illiterate' (Gregory to Serenus), with adoration emerging later against official caution. An origin-as-substitute account is contradicted by the documented organic genealogy.

components.component: The purpose was 'to promote their nominal acceptance of Christianity' (motive of the introducers).
components.rationale: §3 as amended applies, but no automatic cap is needed because the record is genuinely mixed: an accommodationist intent IS documented in specific cases — Paulinus of Nola (Carmen 27, c. 400) states he installed paintings at Felix's shrine to occupy and instruct rustic ex-pagan crowds, and Gregory the Great (Reg. 11.56 to Mellitus, 601) prescribes keeping temples, installing relics, and replacing sacrificial feasts with martyr festivals to win the English 'to inward joys'. Generalized to the institution of image/relic ADORATION, however, the motive fails on documents: the documented rationales for images are didactic and commemorative, the same Gregory ordered that adoration of images be prevented (letters to Serenus of Marseilles), and the rival account (organic growth from martyr piety and, later, incarnational image-theology) has its own documentary support (Polycarp; the Hypatius and iconophile dossiers). Scholarly inference cannot carry the general purpose-clause above improbable against that record.

rulings.weakened_version: Church leaders sometimes deliberately provided Christianized substitutes for pagan cult — relics installed in converted temples, martyr festivals replacing sacrificial feasts, paintings to attract and instruct half-pagan rustics — to ease pagans' nominal entry into the church.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest position for the claim as written is the 19th-century Protestant substitution thesis (Dowling locally; Wylie), whose nearest modern heir is MacMullen's documentation of saint-shrines absorbing pagan cult functions. Engagement: the documented accommodation (Paulinus; Gregory's Mellitus letter — both primary, both engaged above) concerns festivals, temple re-use, and didactic images, and is credited in full in the weakened ruling; what it never documents is the introduction of image/relic adoration for conversion purposes — the hierarchy's one documented stance on adoration in the formative period (Gregory to Serenus) is prohibition. The claim's purpose-clause converts a real, local, documented strategy into the origin story of a whole devotional institution; that generalization is what fails. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's Nicaea-II-rationale point is incorporated (iconophile theology is late and is the practice's defense, not its origin motive).

sources.ref: Martyrdom of Polycarp 18, ANF vol. 1 (local scholarship/anf01..., ~line 4048), with dating debate per Moss/Ehrman
sources.independence_note: Pre-Constantinian primary counterexample to the substitution-origin; dating caveat noted, both datings pre-Constantinian.

sources.ref: Gregory the Great, Register 11.56 (letter to Mellitus, 601), via Bede HE 1.30; Oxford Cult of Saints database E06424
sources.independence_note: Documentary evidence FOR a substitution strategy (temples, festivals, relics) — cited against interest of the as-written ruling and credited in the weakened ruling.

sources.ref: Paulinus of Nola, Carmen 27 (paintings for the rustics at Felix's shrine)
sources.independence_note: Independent c. 400 documentary statement of accommodationist intent for images; didactic, not adorational.

sources.ref: P. Brown, The Cult of the Saints, U Chicago P 1981; E. Kitzinger, DOP 8 (1954)
sources.independence_note: Two independent academic lines carrying the organic-origin account and the image-cult chronology.

rulings.would_change_this: Discovery of period documents — conciliar acta, episcopal correspondence — instituting or defending the adoration (not the didactic use) of images or relics explicitly as a replacement for idol worship to win converts would move the motive component to probable or higher.
rulings.notes: Flagged by the dossier as the MOTIVE test case; the amended §3 rule was applied without the automatic cap because partial documentation exists on both sides.

components.component: Rome (the Latin church) developed, named, and taught the doctrine of purgatory.
components.rationale: Uncontested: purgatorium as noun and defined place is a Latin-church formation, crystallizing in the late 12th century (Le Goff) with conciliar definition at Lyon II (1274) and Florence; 'which Rome named' is, incidentally, exactly right on Le Goff's account.

components.component: Purgatory is 'an invention of paganism' (pagan origin/genealogy).
components.rationale: The documented genealogy is Jewish-Christian: prayer and offering for the dead (2 Macc 12:39-45), early Christian intercession for the dead and purgation ideas (Passio Perpetuae; Tertullian; Cyprian; Augustine's purgatorial fire), systematized in the 12th century (Le Goff, Birth of Purgatory). Post-Le Goff revision (Brown's 7th-century watershed; Moreira, Heaven's Purge, OUP 2010) moves the formation earlier but renders it more internally Christian, not less. Greco-Roman purification eschatology (Plato's Gorgias/Phaedo, Virgil Aeneid 6) contributed conceptual material (Bernstein, Formation of Hell, Cornell UP 1993), which is what survives of the claim — an antecedent contribution, not a pagan 'invention' that Rome merely renamed.

components.component: Rome 'employed [purgatory] to terrify the credulous and superstitious multitudes' (function/motive of its use).
components.rationale: Both readings are documented at comparable quality. For: fear-laden purgatory preaching, vision literature and exempla, and the indulgence economy exploiting fear of postmortem pain are documented (Le Goff; the pre-Reformation indulgence campaigns). Against: Duffy (Stripping of the Altars, Yale UP 1992) documents late-medieval purgatory piety as a system of hope, consolation, and solidarity between living and dead, in which fear was embedded in communal care rather than deployed as a terror instrument. Qualified specialists thus disagree on the doctrine's dominant function; the disagreement does not reduce to confession (Le Goff is a secular Annales historian; Duffy's Yale UP monograph carries independent peer validation). The derogatory 'credulous and superstitious' is White's register, not a rulable assertion.

rulings.weakened_version: The doctrine of purgatory absorbed pre-Christian (Greco-Roman) conceptions of purificatory afterlife punishment, and its medieval preaching and the indulgence economy often played on fear of postmortem torment.
rulings.minority_engagement: The pagan-derivation position is real and named: it is the standard thesis of White's milieu (Dowling 1845, local copy: 'the pagan doctrine concerning the purification of departed souls... which he called Purgatory'; Elliott and Hagenbach per the 1911 GC appendix note), and its defensible modern residue is Bernstein's demonstration (Cornell UP) that Plato and Virgil supplied purificatory-punishment schemes on which Christian imagination drew — Le Goff himself acknowledges the Greco-Roman material. Why it fails as written: the operative doctrine — suffrages for the dead, penitential debt, the church's power over the interim state — descends by documented steps from Jewish-Christian prayer for the dead through patristic purgation theology to 12th-century systematization; no current specialist derives it from a pagan institution, and the post-Le Goff literature (Moreira; Brown) that pushed the dating earlier strengthened the internal-Christian genealogy. 'Invention of paganism' inverts a documented lineage; 'employed to terrify' remains genuinely divided (Le Goff vs Duffy) and is not the failing component. Period_standard flag appropriate at synthesis. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's Le Goff-based rationale is incorporated and extended with the post-Le Goff revisions.
