evidence_review: STATE OF THE QUESTION. The dossier's shared question is continuity: whether the medieval papacy's documented claims remained the Roman Catholic Church's operative principles and teaching, each claim carrying a present-tense-1911 layer. (1) THE MEDIEVAL DOCUMENTARY CORE IS REAL AND UNDISPUTED. Gregory VII's Dictatus Papae (1075) asserts among its 27 propositions that the pope may depose emperors (prop. 12), may absolve subjects from their fealty to wicked rulers (prop. 27), and that the Roman church has never erred nor will err to all eternity (prop. 22) — the text White's own appendix cites through Mosheim/Gieseler/Baronius, and GC ¶53 quotes it accurately. Innocent III extracted the feudal oath of Peter II of Aragon (1204), described himself in his consecration sermon as 'set between God and man, lower than God but higher than man... who judges all and is judged by no one', and governed by plenitudo potestatis (C. Morris, The Papal Monarchy, Oxford; B. Tierney, The Crisis of Church and State 1050-1300). Boniface VIII's Unam Sanctam (1302) closes with the definition that 'it is absolutely necessary for the salvation of every human creature to be subject to the Roman Pontiff' (text at papalencyclicals.net); Tierney calls it 'probably the most famous' medieval church-state document; the hierocratic theorists (Giles of Rome, De ecclesiastica potestate 1301/02; James of Viterbo; Augustinus Triumphus; Hostiensis on plenitudo potestatis) built the maximal theory around these claims. (2) CONSTANCE AND 'NO FAITH WITH HERETICS'. The facts, from Lea's account (local file, vol. 2) and the conciliar acta: Hus came under Sigismund's unconditional imperial safe-conduct (18 Oct 1414), was imprisoned 28 November; the council's canonists assured Sigismund that 'it could not and might not be in any law that a heretic could enjoy a safe-conduct' (contemporary Constance burgher, in Lea), Sigismund repeating in the 7 June session that 'many say that we cannot, under the law, protect a heretic or one suspect of heresy'; and in session 19 (23 Sept 1415) the council formally decreed that no safe-conduct from any secular power can prejudice the Catholic faith or prevent a competent ecclesiastical judge from trying, judging and condemning a heretic OR SUSPECTED HERETIC, even one who came to judgment relying on the safe-conduct and would not have come without it, the promisor remaining 'in no way obligated' (Lea's rendering, lines 15925-15938; summarized in Tanner's edition of the decrees: 'notwithstanding safe conducts of emperors and kings and others, a competent judge can inquire into heresy'). Lea further documents that the council's self-exculpation (that the safe-conduct postdated the arrest) was 'a lie manufactured for the occasion', contradicted by Sigismund himself in public session. Modern conciliar scholarship converges on the legal substance while rejecting the polemical generalization: Fudge (The Trial of Jan Hus: Medieval Heresy and Criminal Procedure, Oxford UP 2013; via The Medieval Review 14.04.12) finds the trial followed canon-law precedent, safe-conducts being treated as 'irrelevant when it came to accusations of heresy', though the proceedings violated the presumption of innocence; Brandmüller (Das Konzil von Konstanz 1414-1418, 2 vols) likewise judges the trial 'procedurally largely fair' under prevailing law. On the maxim itself, the Catholic-apologetic denial (Pallavicino; Hefele; Catholic Encyclopedia 1908: 'it is not true that the council decreed that no faith should be kept with a heretic') targets the general-perfidy reading — the council issued no canon licensing promise-breaking with heretics generally — but understates the session-19 decree, which did release the promisor in the safe-conduct case. Early modern Catholic opinion was itself split: the inquisitorial canonist Simancas (De catholicis institutionibus) held that faith given to heretics ought not be kept, while the Jesuit Becanus (De fide haereticis servanda, 1607/08; modern academic edition and translation, 2019) argued the duty to keep faith across confessional lines — the position that became and remained mainstream; Trent's 1562 safe-conduct to Protestants was drafted precisely to neutralize the Constance precedent and was honored. No Catholic doctrinal standard of the 1911 era taught the maxim, and the church's accredited organs of that era explicitly denied it. (3) THE CIRCA-1911 STATE OF THE GREGORIAN PRINCIPLES. Two strands separate. Ecclesiological: papal monarchy inside the church stood stronger in 1911 than in the Middle Ages — Vatican I's Pastor Aeternus (1870) defined universal ordinary jurisdiction and papal infallibility (within stated limits: ex cathedra, faith and morals only), and Unam Sanctam's closing definition was still cited as de fide. Political: the deposing power and absolution of subjects were dead as operative claims — Vatican I deliberately defined nothing on temporal power; Bishop Fessler's minimizing reply to Schulte (The True and the False Infallibility) received official approbation from Pius IX; Newman's Letter to the Duke of Norfolk (1875), answering Gladstone's The Vatican Decrees in their Bearing on Civil Allegiance (1874; 150,000 copies sold — the classic statement of White's own thesis by a Protestant statesman), argued the medieval depositions rested on historically conditioned public law; national hierarchies had formally disclaimed the deposing power with Rome's acquiescence (Münster bishops 1774; the British and Irish Catholic oath declarations); no pope of the era asserted it, and 1911-era Catholic reference works treated temporal-jurisdiction ultramontanism as obsolete. Yet in-principle church-over-state doctrine demonstrably persisted: the Syllabus of Errors (1864) condemned the propositions that popes had 'exceeded the limits of their power' (prop. 23), that the church may not employ force or any temporal power (prop. 24), and that church and state ought to be separated (prop. 55); Leo XIII's Immortale Dei (1885) restated the Gelasian dyarchy — two powers, each supreme in its own order — while demanding state recognition of the true religion; Pius X's Vehementer Nos (1906) condemned the French separation law as 'a most pernicious error'; and the confessional-state 'thesis' persisted until Vatican II (J. C. Murray's Leo XIII series, Theological Studies 1952-55, Georgetown Woodstock archive, documents both the persistence and the internal transformation). Whether that persistence amounts to doctrinal continuity or development remains genuinely debated among qualified scholars (Murray and Rhonheimer for development; Thomas Pink for continuity of the coercion doctrine), but no qualified scholar affirms that the Gregorian deposing program functioned as the operative principles of Pius X's church. (4) THE POPE AS MEDIATOR IN MEDIEVAL LAY TEACHING. What laity were actually taught (Duffy, The Stripping of the Altars, Yale UP 1992/2005; the confession-manual literature, Journal of Medieval History 36/4, 2010): direct prayer universally required of every lay person (Pater noster, Ave, Creed), devotion centered on Christ's passion, saints as approachable intercessors, forgiveness through contrition and priestly absolution available from any confessor — a system in which the pope figured as remote juridical head, not as the taught channel of personal access to God; doctrinally Christ alone was the perfect mediator (Aquinas, ST III q. 26), priests and saints mediating ministerially. The vicariate and obedience components, by contrast, are documented: Vicarius Christi standard from Innocent III onward, 'in the place of God' a fair gloss of vicarius Dei usage, Unam Sanctam making subjection to the pope necessary for salvation. SOURCE GENEALOGY: Lea and Lenfant both rest on the Constance acta (the von der Hardt line) but are independent critical treatments; the Catholic Encyclopedia digests Hefele/Pallavicino (one apologetic genealogy, counted once); Fudge and Brandmüller are independent modern specialists on opposite confessional sides converging on the legal substance; the papal instruments (Dictatus Papae, Unam Sanctam, Syllabus, Pastor Aeternus, Immortale Dei, Vehementer Nos) are primary anchors independent of all narrative lines; Murray, Duffy, and Tierney are genealogically independent academic voices.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет три утверждения книги о неизменности папской доктрины: что принципы Григория VII и Иннокентия III остаются принципами Рима в 1911 году, что церковь провозглашает правило «с еретиками не следует держать слова» и что средневековых мирян учили видеть в папе единственного земного посредника на пути к Богу. Средневековое документальное ядро каждого утверждения реально: Dictatus Papae закрепил право низлагать императоров, булла Unam Sanctam объявила подчинение папе необходимым для спасения, а Констанцский собор 23 сентября 1415 года постановил, что охранная грамота не защищает еретика от церковного суда, и это стоило Гусу жизни. В авторских формулировках все три утверждения получили оценку «improbable»: к 1911 году церковь не заявляла право низложения как действующее, её официальные авторы прямо отрицали максиму о еретиках, а мирян учили приступать к Богу через Христа, таинства и святых, но не через папу лично. Ослабленные версии тех же утверждений оцениваются как «well-supported»: собор действительно издал такой декрет и никогда его не отменял, Рим не отрёкся от средневековых притязаний и в 1906 году всё ещё осуждал отделение церкви от государства, а подчинение папе средневековая доктрина объявляла условием спасения. Картину изменили бы документы начала XX века, в которых Рим утверждал бы низложение государей или максиму о еретиках как действующее учение.

quantifier_audit: The principles ... are still the principles (definite, totalizing identity of the whole medieval program with 1911 doctrine)

components.component: Gregory VII and Innocent III asserted the principles the chapter has in view: deposing emperors, absolving subjects from allegiance, supremacy over temporal rulers, ecclesial inerrancy (Dictatus Papae props 12, 22, 27; Innocent III's oath from Peter II of Aragon, 1204).
components.rationale: Primary texts exactly as GC's own appendix cites them (Mosheim tr.; Gieseler; Baronius) and as standard scholarship presents them (Tierney, Crisis of Church and State; Morris, The Papal Monarchy). ¶53's quotations of the Dictatus propositions and the Aragon oath are accurate.

components.component: The ecclesiological strand — papal supremacy over the whole church and infallibility/indefectibility — remained Rome's principle in 1911, indeed strengthened.
components.rationale: Pastor Aeternus (1870) defined universal ordinary jurisdiction and papal infallibility; Unam Sanctam's closing definition was still cited as de fide. In this strand the sentence is simply true, and the ruling records it in the book's favor.

components.component: The political strand foregrounded by ¶53 — the power to depose rulers and absolve subjects from allegiance — remained 'the principles' of the church circa 1911.
components.rationale: Vatican I deliberately defined nothing on temporal power; Fessler's officially approved reply to Schulte minimized exactly these claims; Leo XIII's Immortale Dei framed two powers each supreme in its own order and commanded civic obedience; no pope of the era asserted deposition against any state; national hierarchies formally disclaimed the deposing power with Rome's acquiescence (Münster 1774; British/Irish oath declarations); 1911-era Catholic reference works treated temporal-jurisdiction ultramontanism as obsolete. Non-repudiation and the Syllabus's defense of the medieval popes (prop. 23) sustain a weakened continuity claim, not the identity of the deposing program with 1911 operative doctrine.

components.component: Totalizing identity frame: Rome essentially unchanged, the medieval program still definitive ('the boast of Rome that she never changes' taken at face value in ¶54).
components.rationale: The semper eadem claim the frame leverages covered defined doctrine of faith and morals; the deposing power was never defined doctrine, which is precisely how 1911-era Catholic theology reconciled immutability rhetoric with abandonment of the political program. The frame collapses the distinction the evidence requires: real continuity in ecclesiology and church-state principle, real discontinuity in the operative political claims.

rulings.weakened_version: Rome in 1911 had not repudiated the claims of Gregory VII and Innocent III: its official teaching defended the medieval popes' acts as legitimate (Syllabus prop. 23), still condemned church-state separation (Immortale Dei 1885; Vehementer Nos 1906), and asserted papal supremacy within the church more strongly than ever (Pastor Aeternus 1870).
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case for the claim as written has three documented layers. (1) Gladstone (The Vatican Decrees in their Bearing on Civil Allegiance, 1874) argued substantially White's thesis: Rome never repudiated the deposing power, and Vatican I converted medieval claims into binding doctrine, compromising Catholics' civil allegiance. It fails on evidence, not confession: the authoritative Catholic responses it provoked — Fessler's officially approbated reply and Newman's Letter to the Duke of Norfolk (1875) — show the church's accredited interpreters denying that the medieval political claims bound anyone, and no subsequent papal act asserted them; the inference from non-repudiation to present operative principle conflates 'unrenounced' with 'in force'. (2) Ultramontanes of the Vatican I generation (Manning, W. G. Ward) held that the power of the keys includes temporal jurisdiction; but their maximal reading lost inside Catholic theology itself, and by 1911 even confessional reference works called it obsolete. (3) Modern continuity scholarship — the intransigentist literature on the 19th-century papacy's medieval-Christendom restoration ideal, and Thomas Pink's argument that the church's coercion doctrine was never repudiated before Vatican II — keeps a genuine continuity question open at the level of in-principle church-state doctrine. The ruling concedes exactly that strand: it is why the weakened version is well-supported and why the claim is improbable rather than discredited. What no qualified scholar affirms is the as-written identity: that the Gregorian deposing program functioned as the operative principles of Pius X's church.

rulings.would_change_this: A circa-1890s-1900s magisterial act or officially approved theological manual teaching the deposing power as operative doctrine would move the as-written ruling toward genuinely-open or higher; conversely, a documented pre-1911 formal papal renunciation of the medieval claims would cut the weakened version's non-repudiation core and widen the indictment of the book's rhetoric.
rulings.notes: Date-scoped per the charter note: the ruling concerns 1911, not later developments (Dignitatis Humanae 1965 is outside the claim's tense). The verifier's 'disputed' rationale is sustained in substance and sharpened by decomposition: its two halves (Syllabus continuity vs defunct deposing power) become the well-supported weakened version and the failing as-written component respectively. GC-35-111 ('had she but the power...') is a separate counterfactual claim not ruled here.

quantifier_audit: Faith ought not to be kept with heretics, nor persons suspected of heresy (universal norm, extending to the merely suspect)
she declares (present tense; the church as one continuing declaring subject)

components.component: The quoted words are drawn from the source GC's footnote 1006 names: Lenfant, History of the Council of Constance, I, 516 (ed. 1728).
components.rationale: Verbatim wording could not be independently confirmed this session (Google Books and archive.org fetches failed); the citation is precise (volume, page, edition), the 1911 revision verified quotations, and the substance matches the documented session-19 decree. Lenfant is a Huguenot historian of Constance — a conduit reporting the council, noted as such.

components.component: A conciliar declaration of this substance exists: Constance decreed (session 19, 23 Sept 1415) that no secular safe-conduct protects a heretic or suspected heretic from ecclesiastical trial, judgment and punishment, and that the promisor is released from his pledge; council canonists asserted that no law allows a heretic the benefit of a safe-conduct; the principle governed Hus's case.
components.rationale: The decree stands in the conciliar acta (Tanner's edition, summarized at papalencyclicals.net: 'notwithstanding safe conducts of emperors and kings and others, a competent judge can inquire into heresy'); Lea gives the full text and context, including the council's demonstrably false self-exculpation about the safe-conduct's date; Fudge (Oxford UP 2013) and Brandmüller — independent specialists on opposite confessional sides — confirm the canonical substance ('safe conducts were irrelevant when it came to accusations of heresy'). Note that 'nor persons suspected of heresy' tracks the decree's own extension to suspects: at the 1415 level the quotation is substantively accurate.

components.component: Present-tense attribution: the Roman Catholic Church circa 1911 declares the maxim as her standing doctrine ('she declares', within ¶27-28's 'she is unchanged' frame).
components.rationale: No defined Catholic doctrine ever taught the general maxim; mainstream Catholic theology from Becanus (De fide haereticis servanda, 1607/08) onward taught the duty of keeping faith with heretics, and that line prevailed; Trent's 1562 safe-conduct to Protestants was drafted against the Constance precedent and honored; the church's 1911-era organs (Catholic Encyclopedia 1908) explicitly denied the maxim — whatever the apologetic motive, that is documentary evidence of what the church then declared. The unrepealed session-19 decree is a dormant precedent, not a standing present-tense declaration.

rulings.weakened_version: The Roman church's own general council decreed (Constance, 23 September 1415) that no safe-conduct given to a heretic or suspected heretic binds against ecclesiastical judgment and that the giver of the pledge is released; the decree rationalized Hus's execution under an imperial safe-conduct and was never formally retracted.
rulings.minority_engagement: Both directions engaged. FOR the claim as written: the Protestant-polemical tradition from Lenfant through the 19th-century controversialists, powerfully seconded by Lea's independent verdict that the council operated on the canonical principle that 'the heretic had no rights' and covered itself with 'a lie manufactured for the occasion' — this establishes the medieval substance beyond dispute, but nothing in it establishes a standing 1911 declaration; Lea himself frames the affair as inquisitorial canon law of its period. AGAINST even the weakened version: the Catholic-apologetic denial (Pallavicino; Hefele; Catholic Encyclopedia 1908, 'it is not true that the council decreed that no faith should be kept with a heretic'), which argues the council merely held that a secular power cannot immunize against ecclesiastical jurisdiction. This denial is right against the general-perfidy caricature but understates the actual decree, which extended to suspects and expressly released the promisor — the reason the weakened version stands at well-supported over the apologetic minimum. The intra-Catholic split (Simancas affirming the maxim; Becanus refuting it) shows the maxim was a real position inside early modern Catholicism, never its defined doctrine. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's 'disputed' is resolved by date-scoping: the 1415 declaration is fact, the 1911 present tense is not.

sources.ref: H. C. Lea, A History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages, vol. 2 (1888) — local: scholarship/lea-inquisition-middle-ages-vol2-1888.txt, lines 15753-15960 (safe-conduct affair, canonists' maxim, session-19 decree, false self-exculpation)
sources.independence_note: Independent 19th-century archival scholarship, still a standard reference; anti-institutional lean noted; rests on the Constance acta independently of Lenfant's narrative.

sources.ref: Decrees of the Council of Constance, session 19 (23 Sept 1415), Tanner's edition via papalencyclicals.net
sources.independence_note: The conciliar acta themselves; the documentary anchor both polemic and apologetic must interpret.

sources.independence_note: Leading modern English-language specialist, non-Catholic, independent of both Lea and the apologetic line; converges with Brandmüller on the canonical substance.

sources.ref: W. Brandmüller, Das Konzil von Konstanz 1414-1418, engaged via Historisches Lexikon Bayerns and konziliengeschichte.org summaries
sources.independence_note: Catholic conciliar historian (confessional location noted); his 'procedurally largely fair' judgment presupposes the same canonical principle the decree states — convergence across confessional lines.

sources.ref: M. Becanus, On the Duty to Keep Faith with Heretics (De fide haereticis servanda, 1607/08; modern academic edition/translation, 2019, academia.edu record); J. Simancas, De catholicis institutionibus (contra)
sources.independence_note: Documents the early modern intra-Catholic debate: the maxim defended by an inquisitorial canonist, refuted by a Jesuit controversialist whose position became mainstream — direct evidence against 'standing doctrine'.

sources.ref: Catholic Encyclopedia (1908), 'Council of Constance' (newadvent.org)
sources.independence_note: Confessional apologetic digesting Hefele/Pallavicino (one genealogy); used here as primary documentary evidence of what the church's organs declared circa 1911, not as arbiter of the medieval facts.

rulings.would_change_this: An authoritative Roman document of the 1890s-1900s (Holy Office ruling, approved moral-theology manual, catechism) affirming that faith need not be kept with heretics would move the as-written ruling sharply up; a formal pre-1911 papal or conciliar repudiation of the session-19 decree would cut the weakened version's 'never retracted' clause. Verbatim confirmation of Lenfant I.516 would raise component 1 from probable to well-supported without changing the claim-level rulings.
rulings.notes: The extractor's claim_text already hedged ('per the cited source'), but the charter requires ruling on the printed sentence's force: '"..." she declares', present tense, inside ¶28's chameleon/serpent invariance frame. The dual ruling separates the accurate 1415 quotation from the inaccurate 1911 tense.

quantifier_audit: none could approach God except through him
They were taught (generalized medieval lay instruction)

components.component: Medieval laity were generally taught that the pope personally was their earthly mediator, through whom alone they could approach God.
components.rationale: Documented counterexamples defeat the exclusive: every lay person was required to learn and use direct prayer to God (Pater noster, Ave, Creed); devotion centered on Christ's passion, Mary, and the saints as approachable intercessors (Duffy, The Stripping of the Altars); forgiveness was taught through contrition and absolution from any priest (confession-manual literature, JMedHist 2010); doctrinally Christ alone was the perfect mediator (Aquinas, ST III q. 26). The papal-theory steelman (Innocent III 'set between God and man'; Unam Sanctam's subjection-for-salvation definition; the hierocrats) concerns jurisdiction and corporate salvation, not the taught path of personal access to God. Not discredited: the preceding GC sentence extends mediation to 'the priests and prelates to whom he delegated authority', and on that hierarchical reading the sentence compresses real documents; but the printed clause asserts the pope in person ('through him'), and no evidence shows laity taught that.

components.component: They were taught that the pope stood in the place of God (the vicariate).
components.rationale: Vicarius Christi was the standard papal title from Innocent III onward; his consecration sermon claims a place 'between God and man, lower than God but higher than man, who judges all and is judged by no one' (standard text in Tierney and Morris); 'in the place of God' is a fair English gloss of vicarius Dei/Christi as publicly taught and preached. A component ruled in the book's favor.

components.component: They were taught that the pope was to be implicitly obeyed.
components.rationale: Dictatus Papae's obedience propositions; Gregory VII's discipline of rulers; Unam Sanctam's closing definition making subjection to the Roman pontiff necessary for the salvation of every human creature; the excommunication and interdict system enforcing it. Also ruled in the book's favor.

rulings.weakened_version: Medieval teaching made salvation unavailable outside the Roman church headed by the pope — Unam Sanctam declared subjection to the Roman pontiff necessary for the salvation of every human creature — presented the pope as Christ's vicar standing between God and man, and required obedience to him.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest defense of the sentence as written is the hierocratic maximal theory: Giles of Rome's De ecclesiastica potestate and Augustinus Triumphus derived all power, spiritual and temporal, from the pope; Hostiensis' plenitudo potestatis and Unam Sanctam's definition give the pope a soteriologically necessary headship; Innocent III's own 'medius inter Deum et hominem' makes 'earthly mediator' almost a quotation. Add the delegation clause in the preceding GC sentence, and 'through him' can be read as 'through the hierarchy he heads'. The defense fails for the claim as printed on the evidence of what laity were actually taught: high papal theory was curial and academic discourse, not parish catechesis; the documented content of lay religious instruction (universal direct prayer, Christ-and-saints devotion, absolution from any confessor) is incompatible with 'taught that none could approach God except through him [the pope]'. The defense is conceded in full at the weakened level, where the soteriological headship reading is well-supported — the dual ruling records precisely this. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's split ('partly grounded, partly overstated') is sustained and sharpened: the vicariate and obedience components stand, the exclusive-personal-mediation component fails.

sources.ref: E. Duffy, The Stripping of the Altars: Traditional Religion in England 1400-1580 (Yale UP, 1992/2005)
sources.independence_note: Standard revisionist account of late-medieval lay religion; Catholic author writing in a university-press, peer-validated venue — weighed as academic with location noted; independent of the papal-theory literature.

sources.ref: B. Tierney, The Crisis of Church and State 1050-1300 (with documents); C. Morris, The Papal Monarchy (Oxford)
sources.independence_note: Standard scholarly editions/discussions of Dictatus Papae, Innocent III's consecration sermon, and Unam Sanctam; independent of Duffy's devotional-history line.

sources.ref: Innocent III, Sermo in consecratione ('medius inter Deum et hominem, citra Deum sed ultra hominem...'); Boniface VIII, Unam Sanctam (1302), papalencyclicals.net text
sources.independence_note: Primary anchors read through Tierney/Morris; they ground components 2-3 and the weakened version, not the exclusive of component 1.

sources.ref: Aquinas, Summa Theologiae III q. 26 (Christ the one perfect mediator; others mediate ministerially)
sources.independence_note: The doctrinal counterexample to the exclusive, from the church's own normative theology of the period.

sources.ref: 'Lay religion and pastoral care in thirteenth-century England: the evidence of a group of short confession manuals', Journal of Medieval History 36/4 (2010)
sources.independence_note: Peer-reviewed evidence on what pastoral instruction actually contained; independent of Duffy (different period/corpus).

rulings.would_change_this: Medieval catechetical or sermon evidence (primers, pastoral manuals, model sermons) teaching laity that access to God ran through the pope in person would move the as-written ruling toward genuinely-open; conversely, evidence that the vicarius Dei title was confined to curial documents and absent from preaching would lower component 2 from well-supported.
rulings.notes: Ch. 3 ¶17 context: the sentence sits between the delegation clause ('priests and prelates to whom he delegated authority') and the punishment claim (GC-03-062, ruled separately in Phase 1); the delegation clause is what makes the weakened hierarchical reading recognizably the same claim rather than a subject change.

evidence_review: STATE OF THE QUESTION. (1) The barbes and their training. Modern Waldensian scholarship rests on archival sources the 19th century barely used: inquisition registers, trial depositions, and the surviving barbe manuscripts (small portable vernacular volumes now in Trinity College Dublin, Cambridge, and Geneva; Todd, The Books of the Vaudois, 1865; de Lange's Brill source series). Gabriel Audisio (Preachers by Night: The Waldensian Barbes, 15th-16th Centuries, Brill 2007; The Waldensian Dissent, CUP 1999) reconstructs a real and organized formation: candidates trained over several years, memorizing large parts of the New Testament (four to five years on the Testament until known by heart), learning a manual trade or a craft such as surgeon or barber, then serving an itinerant apprenticeship in pairs, a younger barbe under an elder, ministering by night to scattered communities across France, Provence, Italy and Germany. The key primary document is the 1530 memorandum of Georges Morel of Freissinieres to Oecolampadius (printed in Gerdes, Hist. Evang. Renov. ii.402-418; discussed in Baird, Rise of the Huguenots, and by Audisio and Cameron), describing barbe selection, probation, celibacy, pair-travel and rotation. What this documentation does NOT contain: any university route. Euan Cameron (The Reformation of the Heretics, OUP 1984; Waldenses, Blackwell 2000) and Audisio describe a deliberately internal, clandestine, artisan-level culture; the movement's social base was peasants and craftsmen, and exposure of heretic youth at Catholic institutions would have contradicted the movement's entire survival strategy. The 'youth to the seminaries of Lombardy or the Sorbonne' motif enters via the romantic layer: Wylie 1870s (local file, lines 3644-3647), descending from the post-Reformation Vaudois pastor-historians (Perrin 1618, Gilles 1644, Leger 1669), whose apologetic retrojection modern scholarship has documented (Villani, 'The British invention of the Waldenses', in Remembering the Reformation, Routledge 2020; 'Waldensianism Before Waldo: The Myth of Apostolic Proto-Protestantism in Antebellum American Anti-Catholicism', Church History, Cambridge 2022). Clandestine book-carrying, by contrast, is the documented core: Wylie's own version has missionaries carrying Scripture portions 'concealed among their wares or about their persons' (lines 3709-3711); Lea reports from inquisitorial sources a chief apostle traveling with disguises 'now as a cobbler, then as a barber' (lines 3322-3325); the surviving barbe books are precisely the small portable volumes such practice implies. The specific 'garments so prepared' (tailored concealment) detail has no located attestation in primary or modern scholarly literature; its circulation today is confined to confessional retellings genealogically downstream of Wylie and GC itself. On traceability: the inquisitors complained of Waldensian evasion (Lea lines 3446-3449) and the network did survive three centuries, but the record also documents repeated successful tracing and mass prosecution (Strasbourg 1212 burnings, Antonio Secco's 1387-88 inquisition in the Alpine valleys, Cologne 1392, the Pomeranian inquisitions of 1392-94 with hundreds of depositions naming barbes and circuits; Lea lines 3405, 13274, 16049, 17252). (2) Soteriology. Every branch of scholarship agrees the Waldenses rejected purgatory (settled in the negative among them by the late 14th century), indulgences, and masses and suffrages for the dead, holding that God alone pardons sin (Nobla Leyczon; Lea lines 3398-3425; Britannica; Catholic Encyclopedia concedes the same). But specialists equally agree they were not proto-Protestants on justification: Lea already registers Dieckhoff, Herzog and Montet ('the early Waldenses were not Protestants in our modern sense', lines 3376-3382), and quotes their own maxim 'la fe es ociosa sensa las obras' (faith without works is idle) with works redefined as piety, repentance, charity, justice (lines 3422-3425); Cameron documents barbes hearing auricular confession and assigning penances in imitation of the church's rite; Audisio characterizes barbe religion as penitential moralism. The 1532 Chanforan junction is where Reformed justification entered; its exact status is itself debated (Cameron minimizes the 'Reformed synod' reading; Villani and the MDPI Religions 2023 survey 'From Heretical Beggars to Protestant Organizers' report positions from decisive event to constructed myth), but no side of that debate restores a medieval sola-fide Waldensian soteriology. (3) Reformation genealogy. The mainstream view (Cameron; the Church History 2022 article) reverses GC's causality: Wycliffe's dissent grew from Oxford scholastic theology, Luther's from Augustinian academic theology, and the Waldenses were received INTO the Reformation in the 1530s. The named counter-position is the Czech and Waldensian 'First Reformation' continuity school (Amedeo Molnar, Storia dei valdesi I, 1974; Giovanni Gonnet): German-Bohemian Waldensian networks before 1420, Friedrich Reiser's documented Waldensian-Hussite organization, influence on Chelcicky, and the Unitas Fratrum's 1467 recourse to the Waldensian bishop Stephen for consecration (independently reported by Lea, lines 3412-3415). This documents a real Bohemian channel; it does not connect Waldensians to Wycliffe or Luther, and mainstream Hussite historiography (Kaminsky) grounds Hussitism in Wyclif's theology and Czech reform preaching. (4) The Albigenses. The traditional academic picture (Barber, The Cathars; Lambert, Medieval Heresy; Hamilton) holds the Cathars/Albigenses to be dualists: two principles, docetic Christology, rejection of most of the Old Testament, an ascetic elite of 'perfects'. The primary record opposes them to the Waldenses: inquisitorial documents pair 'heresy [Catharism] and Waldensianism' as distinct categories and 'the Waldenses themselves regarded the Cathari as heretics to be combated intellectually' (Lea lines 3363-3369); the earliest Waldensian literature includes anti-Cathar polemic (the Liber Antiheresis tradition of Durand of Huesca), with only a marginal intermediate group (the Runcarii, Lea lines 3571-3575). A live revisionist current (Pegg, 'The Paradigm of Catharism'; Moore, The War on Heresy, 2012; the 2016 Sennis volume Cathars in Question with Biller's extended rebuttal; Shulevitz's History Compass 2019 survey) argues organized dualist Catharism may be an inquisitorial and 19th-century scholarly construct; on that reading Albigensian belief is largely unreconstructible. Neither branch makes the Albigenses scripture-centered evangelical 'brethren' of the Waldenses. (5) The medieval-soteriology baseline for the control claim. Formal doctrine taught salvation by grace through the sacraments; Pelagianism stood condemned, and Trent's decree on justification later anathematized reliance on works apart from grace, evidence of what Rome held officially. But a major academic line finds the operative late-medieval theology and pastoral system works-tilted: Oberman (The Harvest of Medieval Theology) judged Biel's via moderna doctrine of justification, built on 'facere quod in se est' (do what is in you and God will not deny grace), essentially Pelagian; Ozment (The Age of Reform; The Reformation in the Cities) documents the penitential system's burden and anxiety, with Luther's Anfechtungen as the era's exhibit. Against this, Duffy (The Stripping of the Altars, Yale 1992) and the revisionists present traditional religion as vigorous, grace-saturated and consoling. That disagreement is between qualified scholars on comparable evidence and does not reduce to confession. WHAT CHANGED SINCE THE 19TH CENTURY: the archival turn (Dieckhoff 1851 through Montet, then Gonnet/Molnar, then Cameron/Audisio/Biller) dismantled the romantic proto-Protestant Vaudois portrait that Wylie and Muston carried and GC inherited; the Cathar field consolidated the dualist picture and then, in the 2010s, opened the deconstruction debate; late-medieval religion acquired the Ozment-Duffy controversy in place of the older Protestant gloom narrative.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет портрет вальденсов и альбигойцев в книге: студентов с рукописями в перешитой одежде, протопротестантскую сотериологию, «семена Реформации» и альбигойцев как «братьев по вере». Современные исследователи (Камерон, Одизио, Биллер) подтверждают документированное ядро этих сцен: тайных странствующих проповедников-барбов, годы заучивания Писания наизусть, ремесленные прикрытия, отказ от индульгенций и чистилища, мученичество в Пьемонте. Однако конкретные детали книги (учёба в городских школах Франции и Италии, специально приготовленная одежда, полная неуловимость для инквизиции, вера в спасение без заслуг дел) восходят к романтической историографии XIX века, тогда как выучка барбов была внутренней и покаянной, а Реформация Уиклифа и Лютера возникла независимо от вальденсов. Именование альбигойцев «братьями» вальденсов не поддерживает ни традиционная наука, считающая катаров дуалистами, ни ревизионисты вроде Пегга и Мура, у которых верования альбигойцев вообще не поддаются реконструкции. В ослабленных формулировках почти все утверждения досье подтверждаются, а вопрос о том, было ли средневековое благочестие гнётом без утешения, остаётся честно открытым: спор Озмента и Даффи продолжается. Картину изменили бы находки вроде инквизиционных протоколов об учёбе вальденской молодёжи в университетах или корпуса альбигойских текстов с библейской, недуалистической верой.

components.component: Waldensian communities maintained organized instruction of their youth and future barbes in their alpine valleys ('their schools in the mountains')
components.rationale: Audisio reconstructs a genuine multi-year formation from the 1530 Morel memorandum and inquisition depositions: New Testament memorization over years, vernacular literacy (the surviving barbe books), training in a trade. 'Schools' as fixed alpine institutions overstates it (the 'college of the barbes' at Pra del Torno is a later Vaudois tradition); organized instruction as such is documented, hence probable rather than well-supported for the wording.

components.component: Some of the youth were sent from these schools to institutions of learning in the cities of France or Italy
components.rationale: No medieval attestation exists: no matriculation record, no inquisition deposition, no barbe manuscript. The genealogy runs GC <- Wylie ('it was not uncommon for the Waldensian youth to proceed to the seminaries in the great cities of Lombardy, or to the Sorbonne at Paris', local vol. 1 lines 3644-3647) <- the post-Reformation Vaudois pastor-historians (Perrin, Gilles, Leger), whose retrojection of a Protestant self-image is itself documented (Villani; Church History 2022). Modern archival scholarship (Audisio, Cameron, Biller) describes a deliberately clandestine, internal, artisan-level formation with no university route, in a movement of peasants and craftsmen for whom exposure at Catholic institutions would have negated the survival strategy the same paragraph celebrates.

rulings.weakened_version: Waldensian youth destined for the barbe ministry received extended organized training (years of Scripture memorization plus a trade) and traveled beyond their native valleys through the cities of France and Italy in the course of their formation and itinerant apprenticeship.
rulings.minority_engagement: The Phase-1 defender's steelman (answered per charter 7.4): the claim descends from the older Vaudois historians rather than being Wylie's invention, and some barbes commanded Latin (Morel's 1530 correspondence). Both points are true and both fail to carry the claim: Perrin, Gilles and Leger wrote post-1532 confessional apologetic whose proto-Protestant retrojections are the documented problem, not independent evidence (provenance failure, charter 4.2); Morel's Latin is one exceptional document about one delegate and attests individual learning, not an institutional university route. Present-day retellings that assert the university route (ESDA, Lineage Journey and similar heritage sites) are genealogically downstream of Wylie and of GC itself and count as one source line with zero independent weight (charter 4.4). No modern specialist defends the route; Audisio's and Cameron's reconstructions of the training positively exclude it.

sources.ref: G. Audisio, Preachers by Night: The Waldensian Barbes (15th-16th Centuries), Brill 2007 (via HAL-SHS notice, Project MUSE and AUSS reviews); The Waldensian Dissent, CUP 1999
sources.independence_note: Archival reconstruction from inquisition registers and barbe manuscripts; independent of the Wylie/Vaudois-historian tradition it corrects.

sources.ref: E. Cameron, Waldenses: Rejections of Holy Church in Medieval Europe, Blackwell 2000; The Reformation of the Heretics, OUP 1984
sources.independence_note: Independent modern specialist; same conclusion as Audisio from partly different archives.

sources.ref: G. Morel, 1530 memorandum to Oecolampadius (Gerdes ii.402-418), via Baird, History of the Rise of the Huguenots (CCEL) and Audisio's discussion
sources.independence_note: The key insider document on barbe formation; describes probation, pair-travel, rotation; contains no university element.

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism vol. 1, local file lines 3644-3652
sources.independence_note: The period source of the claim; genealogical parent of GC's paragraph, not corroboration.

rulings.would_change_this: A matriculation record, an inquisition deposition, or a barbe notebook attesting Waldensian youth studying at Paris or Lombard institutions would raise the ruling; a pre-Reformation Vaudois document describing such a practice would reopen it.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate: matches Wylie, the documented source tradition of 1888/1911.

quantifier_audit: greatest treasure (superlative, rhetorical; no universal quantifier)

components.component: Itinerant Waldenses carried Scripture manuscripts concealed on their persons or among their goods
components.rationale: The documented kernel: Audisio's barbes carried books on clandestine circuits; the surviving barbe corpus (Dublin, Cambridge, Geneva) consists of small portable vernacular volumes suited to exactly this; Lea reports from inquisitorial sources an apostle traveling under changing disguises to elude capture (local lines 3322-3325); Wylie's own period version has Scripture portions 'concealed among their wares or about their persons' (lines 3709-3711).

components.component: The garments of the student-missionaries were specially prepared ('so prepared') as concealment devices for the manuscripts
components.rationale: The specific tailored-garment device has no located attestation in any primary source or in modern scholarship (verifier, defender and this adjudication all failed to find one; the straggler search returned only confessional retellings downstream of Wylie/GC). It is also welded to the unattested student scenario of GC-04-069 ('their' = the youth at the city schools). A positive, specific device claim whose only source is an uncited 19th-century romantic narrative, against a documented but different concealment practice (among wares, about the person), fails the burden as written.

rulings.weakened_version: Waldensian itinerant preachers and colporteurs carried portions of Scripture hidden about their persons and among their goods as they traveled.
rulings.minority_engagement: The Phase-1 defender's steelman is incorporated rather than opposed: the portable barbe corpus and documented clandestine book-carrying make 'manuscripts concealed on the person' plausible in kind, and that is exactly the weakened ruling. What no published position defends is the sewn/prepared-garment device attached to student-missionaries; the only carriers of that detail (Wylie/Muston and their confessional descendants, including GC-derived retellings) form a single genealogical line (charter 4.4). Since no qualified scholarship affirms the detail, genuinely-open is unavailable and the as-written ruling turns on the unmet burden for a specific unattested embellishment.

sources.ref: G. Audisio, Preachers by Night, Brill 2007; The Waldensian Dissent, CUP 1999
sources.independence_note: Archival; documents clandestine itinerancy and book culture; contains no prepared-garment device.

sources.ref: Barbe manuscript corpus: J. H. Todd, The Books of the Vaudois (Trinity College Dublin MSS), 1865; de Lange's Brill 'Religious Minorities: The Waldenses' source brochure; American Waldensian Society archive notes
sources.independence_note: The physical books themselves: small portable vernacular volumes; independent material evidence for the concealment-friendly format.

sources.ref: H. C. Lea, History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages vol. 1, local file lines 3322-3325, 3446-3449
sources.independence_note: 1888 critical scholarship built on inquisitorial records; genealogically independent of the Wylie/Muston romantic line.

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism vol. 1, local file lines 3709-3715
sources.independence_note: Period source; note even Wylie's version lacks the tailored-garment detail for students, which GC heightens.

rulings.would_change_this: An inquisition deposition, trial inventory, or contemporary description attesting garment-sewn concealment of Scripture by Waldensian carriers (or a scholarly attestation by Biller/Audisio from the trial records) would raise the as-written ruling to probable or better.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate: the romantic staging matches the Wylie/Muston layer available in 1888/1911.

quantifier_audit: could not, by the closest inquiry

components.component: Waldensian students won converts in the city institutions of learning, whose principles frequently permeated entire schools
components.rationale: Wholly derivative of the unattested university-attendance premise (GC-04-069) and unattested itself: no inquisition record or modern study documents Waldensian school-evangelism. Even the period source is thinner than GC's rewrite: Wylie has the youths converting 'the rich merchants with whom they traded, and the landlords in whose houses they lodged' (local vol. 1 lines 3651-3662), not whole schools permeated; the escalation is GC's own.

components.component: The papal authorities could not, by the closest inquiry, trace the spreading heresy to its source
components.rationale: In kind, the clandestinity is real: inquisitors complained of Waldensian evasion (Lea lines 3446-3449) and the network survived three centuries (Audisio). But as an absolute of untraceability it is defeated by documented counterexamples: authorities repeatedly traced, prosecuted and burned Waldensian circuits and barbes (Strasbourg 1212; Antonio Secco's 1387-88 inquisition of the Alpine valleys; Cologne 1392; the Pomeranian inquisitions of 1392-94 with hundreds of depositions naming preachers and routes; Lea lines 3405, 13274, 16049, 17252). Charter 6.3: multiple documented counterexamples cap the absolute; applied to the unattested school scenario the component fails as written.

rulings.weakened_version: Waldensian evangelism was deliberately clandestine, and the authorities often failed for long periods to trace its itinerant network, which survived three centuries of inquisition.
rulings.minority_engagement: The Phase-1 defender's steelman (clandestine proselytism that authorities struggled to trace is well documented in general: Audisio's 'preachers by night', inquisitorial complaints about the sect's invisibility) is accepted and powers the weakened ruling. It cannot carry the claim as written for two reasons the defense itself flagged: the school-evangelism scene is wholly derivative of GC-04-069 and has no attestation outside the Wylie line; and the absolute 'could not, by the closest inquiry' is counter-documented by the successful inquisitions listed above. No published position affirms the as-written scene.

sources.ref: G. Audisio, Preachers by Night, Brill 2007
sources.independence_note: Documents the real clandestinity and the network's longevity; independent of the romantic tradition.

sources.ref: H. C. Lea, History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages vol. 1, local file lines 3405, 3446-3449, 13274, 16049, 17252
sources.independence_note: Inquisitorial-record evidence on both sides: evasion complaints AND repeated successful tracing; independent of Wylie.

sources.ref: E. Cameron, Waldenses, Blackwell 2000
sources.independence_note: Corroborates the prosecution record and the movement's survival strategy.

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism vol. 1, local file lines 3644-3663
sources.independence_note: Period source whose more modest wording GC heightens; genealogical parent, not corroboration.

rulings.would_change_this: Inquisition records documenting conversion networks in schools or universities traced (or persistently untraceable) to Waldensian students would move the as-written ruling in the corresponding direction.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate at the level of the underlying scenario (Wylie), though the 'entire school' escalation is GC's own.

components.component: Medieval penitential religion directed those anxious about sin toward works of satisfaction (penance, fasts, vigils, pilgrimages, indulgences) as the pathway to pardon
components.rationale: Uncontested across the debate: the penitential-indulgence system is documented in the pastoral sources; both Ozment and Duffy presuppose its works-apparatus and differ only on its experiential valence. Paragraph 28's own examples (convent asceticism, scourging, vigils, pilgrimage, penance) are the standard inventory.

components.component: This teaching is fairly characterized as 'taught to trust to their good works to save them', i.e. Rome's operative message made human works the ground of salvation-confidence
components.rationale: As a description of official doctrine the sentence flattens: grace was formally primary, Pelagianism stood condemned, and Trent later anathematized reliance on works apart from grace. But a major academic line holds the operative late-medieval theology and pastoral practice works-tilted: Oberman judged Biel's via moderna doctrine of justification, built on 'facere quod in se est', essentially Pelagian (The Harvest of Medieval Theology), and Ozment documents the penitential system's burden with Luther's Anfechtungen as exhibit. Against this stands Duffy's revision (The Stripping of the Altars): traditional religion as grace-saturated, consoling and vigorous. Qualified scholars, comparable evidence, disagreement not reducible to confession (Duffy's Catholic location and Oberman/Ozment's Protestant location noted per charter 5.3, weighed as academic on both sides): the definition of genuinely-open.

components.component: Many conscientious practitioners experienced the system as anxiety without relief ('ever looking to themselves... finding no relief')
components.rationale: The paragraph's own scope-terms are 'multitudes', 'thousands', 'many', not 'all', so no universal is asserted and no counterexample-cap applies (charter 6). Documented scrupulosity and works-anxiety exist (monastic rigorism, the consolation literature that presupposes the anxiety it treats, Luther as the era's most famous case; Ozment's corpus); Duffy contests the generality of gloom, not the existence of the anxious class.

rulings.weakened_version: Late medieval pastoral religion, and the via moderna strand of its theology, gave human works a load-bearing role in the economy of salvation, and many conscientious practitioners experienced the penitential system as a burden without relief.
rulings.minority_engagement: Both sides are documented rather than one dismissed. For the claim: Oberman (Biel's justification doctrine 'essentially Pelagian'; the facere-quod-in-se-est covenant made human effort the precondition of grace) and Ozment (the burden thesis). Against it: Duffy and the revisionists (traditional religion vibrant and consoling; the works-apparatus experienced within grace), plus the formal-doctrine objection (sacramental grace, condemnation of Pelagianism) that 'taught to trust good works' misstates what Rome officially taught. Neither side fails on evidential quality; the question is a live standoff about lived religion. The Phase-1 verifier reached exactly this analysis and the defender agreed 'disputed' was right; this adjudication confirms and converts it to genuinely-open with the documentation the label requires. Control-sample note: treated with full rigor per the dossier instruction; the extractor's needs_verification:false flag was wrong and the verifier's control catch was correct.

sources.ref: H. A. Oberman, The Harvest of Medieval Theology: Gabriel Biel and Late Medieval Nominalism (Harvard UP 1963; ACLS ebook)
sources.independence_note: The standard study of via moderna soteriology; supports the works-tilt reading of actually-taught theology.

sources.ref: S. Ozment, The Age of Reform 1250-1550 (Yale 1980); The Reformation in the Cities (Yale 1975)
sources.independence_note: The burden thesis; independent of Oberman's argument though convergent.

sources.ref: E. Duffy, The Stripping of the Altars: Traditional Religion in England 1400-1580 (Yale 1992)
sources.independence_note: The revisionist counter-position; genealogically independent of both, built on English parish sources.

sources.ref: Council of Trent, Decree on Justification (1547), read as evidence of formally taught doctrine
sources.independence_note: Shows official soteriology anathematized bare works-trust; cuts against the claim as a statement of formal doctrine.

rulings.would_change_this: A corpus-scale study of confession manuals, model sermons and indulgence preaching quantifying works-versus-grace messaging, or a field-shifting synthesis on lived penitential experience, would tip the frame component toward probable (if it vindicated Ozment/Oberman) or improbable (if it vindicated Duffy).
rulings.notes: Control-sample claim (extractor had flagged needs_verification: false); adjudicated at full rigor.

components.component: The Waldenses rejected the church's works-atonement machinery: indulgences, purgatory, masses and suffrages for the dead, holding that God alone pardons sin
components.rationale: Convergently attested: Lea from the sources (purgatory settled in the negative among them by the late 14th century; 'the whole machinery of indulgences and so-called pious works was thrown aside'; the Nobla Leyczon's denial that any pope since Silvester could pardon a single mortal sin, local lines 3398-3421), Cameron and Audisio for the 15th-16th century communities, Britannica, and the Catholic Encyclopedia conceding the same rejections.

components.component: They held the principle of works-atonement false as such, i.e. a grace-alone soteriology in which no good works atone (the sense the paragraph asserts: 'Christ as their only hope', 'Reliance upon human merit intercepts the view of Christ's infinite love')
components.rationale: Specialist scholarship is essentially unanimous that medieval Waldensian soteriology was penitential, not sola-fide: barbes heard auricular confession and assigned penances in imitation of the church's rite, works of satisfaction at the center of their pastoral practice (Cameron); their religion was a commandment-keeping moralism (Audisio); their own maxim ran 'la fe es ociosa sensa las obras', faith without works is idle, with works redefined as piety, repentance, charity, justice rather than pilgrimages and formal exercises (Lea lines 3422-3425); already Dieckhoff, Herzog and Montet established that 'the early Waldenses were not Protestants in our modern sense' (Lea lines 3376-3382). Reformed justification entered at the 1532 junction with the Reformation, whatever the exact status of Chanforan (itself debated: Cameron versus the traditional reading, per Villani and the Religions 2023 survey), and no side of that debate restores a medieval grace-alone Waldensian soteriology.

rulings.weakened_version: The Waldenses rejected the atoning machinery of indulgences, purgatory and suffrages for the dead, insisting that God alone pardons sin.
rulings.minority_engagement: The Phase-1 defender's downgrade argument (answered per charter 7.4): the sentence can bear a narrow reading, rejection of the works-that-atone machinery (purgatory, indulgences, suffrages), which is documented. Accepted, and that narrow reading is exactly the weakened version, ruled well-supported. But as printed the sentence sits inside paragraph 29's grace-alone frame ('point them to Christ as their only hope of salvation... Reliance upon human merit intercepts...'), and charter 2 requires ruling the claim with its actual framing and rhetorical force: in that sense it asserts the proto-Protestant portrait, which the specialist literature rejects on the barbe-manuscript and trial-record evidence. The older evangelical-Vaudois historiography (Wylie, Monastier, Comba's tradition) is the strongest published defense of the as-written sense; it fails on provenance, reading post-1532 Protestant confessions of faith back into the medieval movement, a retrojection modern scholarship has specifically documented (Cameron; Church History 2022). The mixed evidence the defender cited is real, but the mix (rejection of some works-machinery plus retained penance-satisfaction) is itself what makes the blanket as-written attribution improbable rather than open: no qualified position affirms a medieval Waldensian sola-fide.

sources.ref: E. Cameron, The Reformation of the Heretics, OUP 1984; 'Dissent and Heresy' chapter (Blackwell Companion, cited in Phase-1 with the confession/penance evidence); Waldenses, Blackwell 2000
sources.independence_note: The central modern specialist on Waldensian soteriology; archival.

sources.ref: H. C. Lea, History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages vol. 1, local file lines 3376-3382, 3398-3425
sources.independence_note: 1888 critical synthesis carrying Dieckhoff/Herzog/Montet; genealogically independent of Cameron and of the Wylie line; quotes the Waldensian works-maxim directly.

sources.ref: G. Audisio, The Waldensian Dissent, CUP 1999
sources.independence_note: Independent corroboration of the penitential-moralist characterization.

sources.ref: 'From Heretical Beggars to Protestant Organizers: The Reception of the Reformation by the Waldensians', Religions 14(1):4, MDPI 2023; S. Villani, 'The British invention of the Waldenses' (Routledge 2020)
sources.independence_note: Document the Chanforan debate and the historiographical construction of the proto-Protestant portrait; independent of Cameron's specific theses.

rulings.would_change_this: A pre-1532 barbe manuscript or trial deposition showing grace-alone teaching (justification without works of satisfaction) would raise the as-written ruling; conversely, fuller documentation of merit-teaching in the barbe corpus would pressure the weakened ruling's 'God alone pardons' element.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate: the sola-fide portrait matches Wylie and the broader 19th-century Protestant historiography White used (Phase-1 anachronistic before defense downgrade).

components.component: The Waldenses were scattered over many lands
components.rationale: Uncontested: communities and missions from Aragon to Bohemia, Pomerania and Calabria (Lea local line 3371; Cameron; Biller; Audisio).

components.component: They planted the seeds of the Reformation that began in the time of Wycliffe and grew broad and deep in the days of Luther (causal contribution to the Reformation's genesis)
components.rationale: The preponderance of modern scholarship reverses the causality: Wycliffe's dissent grew out of Oxford scholastic theology with no demonstrated Waldensian input; Luther's out of Augustinian academic theology; and the Waldenses received Reformation theology in the 1530s rather than seeding it (Cameron's absorption reading of the Chanforan junction; the proto-Protestant genealogy documented as a confessional and 19th-century construction in 'Waldensianism Before Waldo', Church History 2022, and Villani 2020). The one documented channel, Waldensian networks feeding the Bohemian reform stream, does not touch Wycliffe or Luther, and mainstream Hussite historiography (Kaminsky) grounds Hussitism in Wyclif's theology and Czech reform preaching.

components.component: The Reformation 'is to be carried forward to the close of time' by those willing to suffer for the word of God and the testimony of Jesus
components.rationale: Prophetic-eschatological framing (with the Revelation citation the sentence's source-ref attaches to); out of scope per charter 9. All in-scope findings are conditional on not evaluating this layer.

rulings.weakened_version: Waldensian dissent, scattered across many lands, contributed to the late-medieval dissenting milieu, notably in Bohemia where Waldensian and Hussite networks merged, and the movement ultimately joined the Reformation.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest opposing position is the continuity school the Phase-1 defender invoked (answered per charter 7.4): Amedeo Molnar and Giovanni Gonnet, and Czech 'First Reformation' historiography, group Waldensians with Wycliffites and Hussites as precursors of Protestantism, on real evidence: German-Bohemian Waldensian communities before 1420, Friedrich Reiser's documented Waldensian-Hussite organization, influence on Chelcicky, and the Unitas Fratrum's 1467 recourse to the Waldensian bishop Stephen for consecration (independently reported by Lea, local lines 3412-3415). Their institutional location (Molnar and Gonnet wrote from within the Waldensian-church historiographical tradition) is noted, not held against them (charter 5.3). This evidence is conceded and powers the weakened ruling of probable. It fails to sustain the claim as written because the sentence asserts seeding of 'the Reformation that began in the time of Wycliffe': the continuity school's channel runs through Bohemia after Hus, not to Wycliffe, and no scholarship connects Waldensian writings to Wycliffe's or Luther's theology. The defense's further point that 'seeds' is figurative softens but does not remove the causal-priority assertion the sentence makes; a figurative planting still claims the crop.

sources.ref: 'Waldensianism Before Waldo: The Myth of Apostolic Proto-Protestantism in Antebellum American Anti-Catholicism', Church History (Cambridge UP, 2022)
sources.independence_note: Documents the construction of the seeds-of-the-Reformation genealogy in the very tradition GC used; peer-reviewed.

sources.ref: E. Cameron, Waldenses, Blackwell 2000; The Reformation of the Heretics, OUP 1984
sources.independence_note: The absorption reading of the 1532 junction; independent of the Church History article.

sources.ref: A. Molnar, Storia dei valdesi I (1974); G. Gonnet; Czech 'First Reformation' historiography (with Reiser and Unitas Fratrum evidence; Lea local lines 3412-3415 independently reports the 1467 consecration)
sources.independence_note: The named minority; confessional location noted; its documented Bohemian channel is incorporated into the weakened ruling.

sources.ref: 'From Heretical Beggars to Protestant Organizers', Religions 14(1):4, MDPI 2023 ('in some aspects... could be seen as proto-Protestants, but they mostly did not raise the doctrinal objections characteristic of sixteenth-century Protestant leaders')
sources.independence_note: Recent peer-reviewed survey positioned between the camps; also documents the Chanforan-historiography debate.

rulings.would_change_this: Documented textual dependence of Wycliffe or Luther on Waldensian writings would raise the as-written ruling; a demonstration that the Waldensian-Hussite networks (Reiser, the 1467 consecration) are inquisitorial or later constructs would lower the weakened ruling.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate: the Wylie/Muston/D'Aubigne seed narrative was standard in White's sources (Phase-1 anachronistic before defense downgrade).

components.component: The Waldenses laid down their lives upon the mountains of Piedmont under persecution
components.rationale: Textbook record: the 1487-88 crusade against the Dauphine and Piedmont valleys, the persecutions of 1560-61, the 1655 'Piedmont Easter' massacre; Lea documents Alpine persecutions from 1387 onward (local lines 13274, 16049).

components.component: The Albigenses of France were likewise persecuted to death by the Roman church
components.rationale: The Albigensian crusade (1209-1229) and the Languedoc inquisition are among the best-documented repressions of the Middle Ages (Lea vol. 1; Barber; uncontested on any side of the Cathar debate).

components.component: The Albigenses were the Waldenses' 'brethren', bearing similar witness to the same biblical faith (doctrinal kinship)
components.rationale: Traditional scholarship (Barber, The Cathars; Lambert, Medieval Heresy; Hamilton) holds the Cathars/Albigenses to be dualists: two principles, docetic Christology, rejection of most of the Old Testament, a 'perfect' elite, doctrinally antithetical to the scripture-centered Waldenses. The primary record opposes the two groups: inquisitorial documents pair 'heresy [Catharism] and Waldensianism' as distinct categories, and 'the Waldenses themselves regarded the Cathari as heretics to be combated intellectually' (Lea local lines 3363-3369), with the earliest Waldensian literature including anti-Cathar polemic (the Liber Antiheresis tradition of Durand of Huesca) and only a marginal intermediate group (Runcarii, Lea lines 3571-3575). The revisionist current (Pegg; Moore, The War on Heresy; the Sennis volume Cathars in Question, 2016) destabilizes the organized-dualist model but supplies no positive evidence of evangelical kinship: on the skeptics' own account Albigensian belief is largely unreconstructible, and Biller's rebuttal in the same volume keeps the dualist reading live. Neither branch of current scholarship supports 'brethren'; shared persecution and loose medieval usage of 'Albigenses' (which sometimes swept in southern-French Waldenses, Lea lines 3363-3364) are what the 19th-century tradition mistook for kinship.

components.component: What both groups witnessed to is 'the truth' in the book's theological sense
components.rationale: Theological valuation; out of scope per charter 9. The in-scope kinship component is ruled only as a doctrinal-similarity question.

rulings.weakened_version: Like the Waldenses in Piedmont, the Albigenses of France were dissenters from the Roman church who were persecuted to death for their religion.
rulings.minority_engagement: Two opposing positions engaged. (1) The Phase-1 defender's Pegg/Moore case (answered per charter 7.4): the Cathar-deconstruction debate is real and live (Sennis ed., Cathars in Question, York Medieval Press 2016; Shulevitz, History Compass 2019), and it genuinely weakens confident assertions about what the Albigenses believed. The defender itself conceded the revisionists do not make the Albigenses proto-Protestant Bible Christians. Where this adjudication differs from the defense's 'disputed' framing: charter 1 makes genuinely-open available only where qualified positions disagree about the claim itself; here no qualified position affirms the kinship, so destabilizing the dualist counter-portrait leaves the positive 'brethren' assertion unevidenced rather than contested, which is improbable, not open. (2) The 19th-century Protestant line (Wylie, following Allix and Faber: Albigenses as evangelical witnesses; Wylie vol. 1 calls them 'congenial fellow-labourers' of the Waldenses, local lines 3721-3724): it fails on provenance, resting on the martyrological reading of hostile sources and on the terminological looseness of 'Albigenses'; no modern school retains it. The weakened shared-persecution reading the defender proposed is adopted and ruled well-supported.

sources.ref: M. Barber, The Cathars: Dualist Heretics in Languedoc in the High Middle Ages (Longman 2000; H-Net and IHR reviews); M. Lambert, Medieval Heresy
sources.independence_note: The standard dualist picture, built on the Languedoc sources; independent of the Waldensian literature.

sources.ref: A. Sennis (ed.), Cathars in Question (York Medieval Press 2016), via The Medieval Review 17.01.27 and EHR review; M. G. Pegg; R. I. Moore, The War on Heresy (2012); D. Shulevitz, 'Historiography of heresy', History Compass 17 (2019)
sources.independence_note: The revisionist current and its rebuttal (Biller) in one volume; genealogically independent of Barber's synthesis; establishes that NEITHER side supports evangelical kinship.

sources.ref: H. C. Lea, History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages vol. 1, local file lines 3359-3369, 3571-3575
sources.independence_note: Inquisitorial-record evidence of the two sects' distinction and mutual hostility; predates and is independent of both modern camps.

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism vol. 1, local file lines 3721-3724 ('congenial fellow-labourers... the Albigenses')
sources.independence_note: Period source of the 'brethren' framing; genealogical parent of GC's sentence.

rulings.would_change_this: A corpus of Albigensian writings showing scripture-centered, non-dualist faith, or a consensus vindication of the Pegg/Moore reading combined with positive evidence identifying Languedoc dissent with Waldensian-type evangelism, would reopen the kinship component.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate: matches Wylie's treatment of the Albigenses as evangelical witnesses (Phase-1 anachronistic before defense downgrade).

evidence_review: The dossier's shared question is the composition history of the biblical books, on which the state of the question has moved decisively since White's sources were written, along two separable evidential lines. LINE 1 — antiquity of human history (bears on GC-00-005 alone). The 'first 2,500 years of human history' frame presupposes the Ussher-type computation (creation 4004 BC, Moses/Exodus c. 1491 BC) printed in KJV margins from 1701. James Barr's Ethel M. Wood Lecture 'Biblical Chronology: Legend or Science?' (Univ. of London, 1987) establishes that the biblical chronology is a literary-theological construct, not a historical measurement. Independently, the archaeological record documents continuous human settlement long before 4004 BC: Pre-Pottery Neolithic Jericho by c. 9000 BC, Çatalhöyük c. 7400–6200 BC (UNESCO; radiocarbon plus dendrochronological calibration), literate urban civilization at Uruk by c. 3350–3200 BC (Met Museum, 'Origins of Writing'; Archaeology Magazine 2016), and Homo sapiens some 300,000 years old. No peer-reviewed archaeology defends a c. 4000 BC start of human history; the sole defense is young-earth creationism (Answers in Genesis, ICR), a confessional position whose case rests on scriptural premises plus rejection of the convergent chronometers, without standing in any independent venue. Note the asymmetry: the claim's OTHER component — no written Scripture existed before Moses' era — is true on every scholarly dating, since critical scholarship moves biblical composition LATER, not earlier. LINE 2 — date of biblical composition (bears on GC-00-007, GC-00-008, GC-01-097, and, separately, GC-17-066). The academic preponderance places the earliest biblical literary composition in the first millennium BC: epigraphically, an Old Hebrew script and standardized scribal infrastructure capable of long literary prose is attested only from the 9th century onward (Rollston, Writing and Literacy in the World of Ancient Israel, SBL 2010), and Schniedewind (How the Bible Became a Book, Cambridge UP 2004) argues biblical literature was written down largely in the 8th–6th centuries; literary-historically, the completed Torah is dated to the Persian period (Römer, 'How to Date Pentateuchal Texts', FAT 111, Mohr Siebeck 2016; the compositional process, though 'hotly contested' in its details, is contested within a 1st-millennium window). For Deuteronomy specifically, the 7th-century Josianic connection (de Wette 1805 onward) is reinforced by the close parallels between Deut 28:20–44 and Esarhaddon's Succession Treaty of 672 BC (Weinfeld, Biblica 46, 1965; Steymans, OBO 1995), strengthened by the 2009 Tell Tayinat find of an EST copy imposed on western vassals (Steymans, 'Deuteronomy 28 and Tell Tayinat', 2013) — the very chapter GC quotes is the most firmly 7th-century-anchored text in the Pentateuch. The named minority — Kitchen (On the Reliability of the Old Testament, Eerdmans 2003: 2nd-millennium Hittite treaty form, Egyptian background), Berman (JBL: Hittite CTH 133 closer to Deut 13 than EST), Zehnder's critique of direct EST dependence — is methodologically argued and was engaged: Levinson & Stackert's response shows the form-parallel argument cannot date texts by itself; the linguistic profile of pentateuchal prose is monarchic-period Classical Hebrew, not 2nd-millennium; and even scholars who doubt direct EST literary dependence (Crouch; Zehnder's sympathizers) retain a 1st-millennium date. Defense of actual Mosaic-era composition today is confined almost entirely to confessional venues (JETS, Themelios), so the disagreement fails the charter's genuinely-open test (§1: disagreement must not reduce to confessional alignment). Consequences for the arithmetic: the '1600 years Moses to John' span requires the Mosaic anchor (c. 1500 BC to c. AD 100); on critical dating the span of composition-as-books is roughly 900–1100 years (archaic poetry possibly 12th–10th c. — Cross/Freedman, Russell 2007, itself debated with proposals down to the 5th c. — to Revelation under Domitian c. 95 AD, which is mainstream on all sides). JOEL is the exception in the pool: its date is notoriously underdetermined — proposals run from the 9th to the 2nd century BC on purely internal evidence; the current majority favors the Persian period (Wolff, Hermeneia: 500–350; Crenshaw, Anchor Bible: 5th c.; Barton, OTL: c. 400), but pre-exilic dates are defended on comparable internal evidence by non-confessional critics (Kapelrud c. 600; Rudolph 597–587) and by evangelicals (Stuart, WBC: 9th–8th c.; the nations-mentioned argument and the absence of Persian loanwords cut early, the temple-community profile and prophetic citations cut late; Thompson's Festschrift survey 'The Date of Joel' documents the standoff). A '2,500 years before 1780' figure implies c. 720 BC (with rounding, c. 620–820 BC), inside the published range though not the majority position. WHAT CHANGED SINCE THE 19TH CENTURY: in White's milieu the Ussher margins and Mosaic authorship were standard, and 19th-century critics themselves commonly dated Joel early (9th–8th c.); the Deuteronomy 7th-century dating existed academically (de Wette) but had not penetrated her confessional sources. The 1600-year span was and remains the standard figure of conservative Bible handbooks.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет рамку композиции Библии в «Великой борьбе»: письменное откровение началось с Моисея, Библию писали 1600 лет от Моисея до Иоанна, Иоиль писал за 2500 лет до 1780 года, а предупреждение Второзакония прозвучало за четырнадцать веков до разрушения Иерусалима в 70 году нашей эры. Современная библеистика датирует древнейшие библейские книги первым тысячелетием до нашей эры, а ядро Второзакония связывает с седьмым веком, поэтому четыре утверждения из пяти в написанном виде получили оценки от «маловероятно» до «опровергнуто»; опровергнута только рамка «первые 2500 лет человеческой истории», требующая начала человечества около 4000 года до нашей эры. Датировка Иоиля остаётся честно открытым вопросом: специалисты предлагают даты от девятого до четвёртого века до нашей эры, и цифра «2500 лет» попадает внутрь этого диапазона. В ослабленных версиях (Библия складывалась около тысячелетия и дольше, предупреждение Второзакония на много веков старше 70 года) четыре утверждения из пяти хорошо подтверждены. Картину изменили бы датируемые рукописи или надписи второго тысячелетия до нашей эры с библейским текстом.

quantifier_audit: the first twenty-five hundred years of human history

components.component: Human history's first 2,500 years ran from its beginning to the era of Moses — i.e., humanity began c. 4000 BC (the Ussher-type arithmetic frame asserted as world history)
components.rationale: Decisively refuted by convergent, independent evidence: continuous archaeological record of human settlement from Pre-Pottery Neolithic Jericho (c. 9000 BC) and Çatalhöyük (c. 7400–6200 BC) through literate Uruk (c. 3350–3200 BC), calibrated by radiocarbon and dendrochronology; Homo sapiens c. 300,000 years old. Barr (1987) shows the 2,500-year Adam-to-Moses figure is a correct rendering of the Bible's internal genealogical arithmetic but not a measurement of human history. No peer-reviewed archaeology supports a c. 4000 BC start.

components.component: No biblical text existed in written form before the era traditionally assigned to Moses (c. 15th–13th c. BC); transmission of religious tradition before then was oral
components.rationale: True on every scholarly dating and strengthened by the critical position: conservative dating begins biblical writing with Moses; critical dating begins it centuries later (9th–6th c. BC). Either way, nothing of the Bible existed in writing before c. 1500 BC, and oral transmission preceding written Scripture is a commonplace across the field.

components.component: What God communicated in that period counts as 'revelation' (divine communication through chosen servants)
components.rationale: Theological category; out of scope per §9. The empirical rulings above are conditional on reading 'written revelation' as 'written Scripture', which the paragraph's own context (Moses to John, an inspired book) requires.

rulings.weakened_version: No biblical book yet existed during the long pre-Mosaic era — a span the Bible's own traditional chronology reckons at about 2,500 years — and religious knowledge in that era passed down orally.
rulings.minority_engagement: The only standing defense of a c. 4000 BC start of human history is young-earth creationism (Answers in Genesis, ICR), which argues that Ussher's method was scholarly by his era's standards and that uniformitarian dating methods rest on unprovable assumptions.
