The first point is true but irrelevant (period-competence does not make the chronology correct); the second fails on evidence, because the chronometers are multiple and independent (dendrochronology, ice cores, varves, calibrated radiocarbon, Egyptian/Mesopotamian king-lists) and converge on the same deep timescale, while YEC offers no positive counter-chronology accepted in any peer-reviewed venue. Confessional identity is not the ground of dismissal; the absence of independent evidential support is. No Phase-1 defense object exists (defender skipped disputed verdicts); the strongest defense — that White writes devotionally within her tradition's chronology — is incorporated as the weakened version, ruled well-supported.

sources.ref: James Barr, 'Biblical Chronology: Legend or Science?' (Ethel M. Wood Lecture, University of London, 1987), biblicalstudies.org.uk/pdf/emwl/chronology_barr.pdf
sources.independence_note: Hebrew-Bible specialist analyzing the Ussher-type computation from the biblical side; genealogically independent of the archaeological line.

sources.ref: UNESCO World Heritage entry 'Neolithic Site of Çatalhöyük' (occupation 7400–6200 BC) + PMC article on Pre-Pottery Neolithic Jericho (c. 9000 BC)
sources.independence_note: Archaeological/scientific line resting on excavation and radiometric data; independent of biblical-chronology scholarship.

sources.ref: Metropolitan Museum of Art, 'The Origins of Writing'; Archaeology Magazine, 'The World's Oldest Writing' (2016): proto-cuneiform at Uruk c. 3350–3200 BC
sources.independence_note: Digest of the Uruk epigraphic corpus; same genealogy as other Uruk summaries, counted once; used for the continuous-civilization context, not as the decisive counterexample.

sources.ref: NCSE, 'Seven Myths about Ussher'; Wikipedia 'Ussher chronology' / 'Young Earth creationism'
sources.independence_note: Background on the chronology's reception; genealogically dependent on the mainstream literature above.

sources.ref: Answers in Genesis, 'The World: Born in 4004 BC?'; ICR, 'James Ussher and His Chronology'
sources.independence_note: The minority defense engaged; confessional apologetic, cited as position only.

rulings.would_change_this: Peer-reviewed evidence collapsing the convergent chronometric methods together — e.g., a demonstrated systematic error inflating dendrochronological and ice-core chronologies by thousands of years — or archaeological redating placing Neolithic Jericho and Çatalhöyük after c. 4000 BC. For the weakened version: a datable pre-1500 BC manuscript of any biblical text would refute it in the other direction.
rulings.notes: Genre: devotional Introduction, not a historical chapter. period_standard candidate: the Ussher frame stood in KJV margins and throughout White's milieu; academic prehistory had already moved by 1888/1911, but her confessional sources had not. The framing_gap of 4 is the dossier's largest and is the real finding here.

components.component: The earliest biblical books were composed in written form in the time of Moses (c. 15th–13th c. BC)
components.rationale: Clear academic preponderance against: Old Hebrew script and a standardized scribal apparatus capable of long literary prose are attested only from the 9th c. BC (Rollston); biblical literature was written down largely in the 8th–6th c. (Schniedewind); the completed Torah dates to the Persian period (Römer). Even the neo-documentarians who date sources earliest place them in the monarchy, not the Late Bronze Age. The linguistic profile of pentateuchal prose is monarchic Classical Hebrew. The engaged minority (Kitchen) fails to establish 2nd-millennium composition as opposed to 2nd-millennium memory.

components.component: 'The written word' — the theological status of these texts as inspired revelation
components.rationale: Out of scope per §9; the empirical layer ruled above is when the biblical texts were composed, conditional on reading 'the written word' as 'the biblical text', which the extraction and context support.

rulings.weakened_version: Some material now in the Pentateuch — archaic poetry and early legal and narrative tradition — may descend from traditions reaching back toward the late second millennium BC, though the biblical books as written compositions date to the monarchic period and later.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest opposing position: K. A. Kitchen (On the Reliability of the Old Testament, Eerdmans 2003), an academically credentialed Egyptologist arguing that the Sinai/Deuteronomy covenant form matches late-2nd-millennium Hittite treaties (historical prologue plus blessings and curses) rather than 1st-millennium Assyrian ones, and that Egyptian customs and nomenclature in the Pentateuch fit 2nd-millennium realities. The argument is real but fails to carry composition-dating: (1) treaty-form parallels cannot date texts by themselves (Levinson & Stackert contra Berman), and the closest verbal parallels for Deut 28 run to the 7th-c. Esarhaddon treaty, not Hittite exemplars; (2) accurate 2nd-millennium background at most shows old tradition or memory, not Mosaic-era writing; (3) the epigraphic record shows no Hebrew literary infrastructure before the 9th c. (Rollston). Because actual Mosaic-era composition is today defended almost entirely within confessional venues, the disagreement reduces largely to confessional alignment and fails the genuinely-open test; hence improbable, not genuinely-open. No Phase-1 defense object exists (defender skipped disputed verdicts).

sources.ref: W. M. Schniedewind, How the Bible Became a Book (Cambridge UP, 2004): biblical literature written down largely 8th–6th c. BC
sources.independence_note: Social history of writing; engaged via published summaries.

sources.ref: C. A. Rollston, Writing and Literacy in the World of Ancient Israel (SBL, 2010) with published reviews (ProQuest; BAS)
sources.independence_note: Iron Age epigraphic corpus; same broad data class as Schniedewind but independent author and analysis (dates Old Hebrew script later than the consensus, strengthening the point).

sources.ref: T. Römer, 'How to Date Pentateuchal Texts — Some Case Studies', in The Formation of the Pentateuch (FAT 111, Mohr Siebeck, 2016)
sources.independence_note: European literary-historical criticism; a line genealogically distinct from the epigraphic one.

sources.ref: K. A. Kitchen, On the Reliability of the Old Testament (Eerdmans, 2003) with CBQ 69 (2007) review
sources.independence_note: The engaged minority; academically credentialed, confessionally located defense.

sources.ref: F. M. Cross & D. N. Freedman on archaic poetry; B. D. Russell, The Song of the Sea (2007): 12th–10th c. datings vs archaizing-style skeptics (range 13th–5th c.)
sources.independence_note: Grounds the weakened ruling; a debate internal to non-confessional scholarship, hence genuinely open.

sources.ref: 'Composition of the Torah' — Wikipedia
sources.independence_note: Digest of the literature above; not counted toward the 2-source floor.

rulings.would_change_this: A datable 2nd-millennium Hebrew (or proto-Hebrew) manuscript or inscription containing pentateuchal text, or a peer-reviewed linguistic demonstration that pentateuchal prose reflects 2nd-millennium Northwest Semitic rather than monarchic Classical Hebrew. For the weakened version: a consensus resolution of the archaic-poetry dating debate in either direction.
rulings.notes: The smallest framing gap in the dossier (1): even the steelman only reaches genuinely-open, because the claim's subject is the beginning of biblical WRITING, and no relaxation short of changing the subject makes Mosaic-era writing probable.

components.component: Moses was 'the historian of creation and the law' — i.e., Moses wrote Genesis and the pentateuchal law
components.rationale: Same evidential anchor as GC-00-007 (see evidence_review, Line 2): composition of Genesis and the law is dated to the monarchic-through-Persian periods by the clear preponderance of academic scholarship; Mosaic authorship is defended almost entirely in confessional venues.

components.component: Biblical composition concluded with the Johannine writings near the end of the 1st century AD
components.rationale: Revelation is mainstream-dated to Domitian's reign (c. 95 AD) and the Johannine corpus to c. 90–110; the endpoint holds on all sides. (Whether the author was John the apostle is a separate, contested attribution question, not load-bearing for the endpoint date; a minority extends 2 Peter into the early 2nd c., which would stretch, not shrink, the span.)

components.component: The total span of biblical composition was about 1,600 years
components.rationale: Pure arithmetic on the two anchors: c. 1500 BC to c. 100 AD gives 1,600 only if the Mosaic anchor holds. On critical dating the span of composition-as-books is roughly 900–1,100 years (10th/9th c. BC at the earliest, counting archaic poetry, to c. 100 AD). The figure inherits the improbability of the Mosaic anchor.

rulings.weakened_version: The Bible was composed over an immense span — roughly a millennium or more — by many writers across changing eras, concluding near the end of the first century AD.
rulings.minority_engagement: The 1,600-year figure is the standard span of conservative Bible handbooks (commonly '1,500–1,600 years, 40+ authors') and holds exactly if Kitchen's 2nd-millennium case for Mosaic composition holds; that case is engaged and found wanting at GC-00-007 (treaty-form parallels cannot date texts; epigraphic and linguistic evidence points to 1st-millennium composition). The claim is therefore a conditional frame whose condition fails on the preponderance of evidence, while the same rhetorical point ('one coherent book written across many centuries by many hands') survives fully at the millennium scale — which is why the weakened ruling is well-supported and uncontested. No Phase-1 defense object exists (defender skipped disputed verdicts).

sources.ref: Shared pool, evidence_review Line 2: Schniedewind (Cambridge UP 2004), Rollston (SBL 2010), Römer (Mohr Siebeck 2016) for the start anchor
sources.independence_note: Same anchor as GC-00-007; chain-inherited, counted as one failure point in synthesis.

sources.ref: 'Dating the Bible' — Wikipedia (earliest texts to Daniel 168–164 BC; NT 2nd half of 1st c.; Revelation under Domitian)
sources.independence_note: Digest of standard datings; used for the endpoint and span arithmetic, where it matches the academic literature.

sources.ref: Biblical Archaeology Society, 'When Was the Bible Written?'
sources.independence_note: Popular-scholarly digest, genealogically close to the academic mainstream; corroborates the millennium-scale span.

sources.ref: K. A. Kitchen, On the Reliability of the Old Testament (Eerdmans, 2003)
sources.independence_note: The minority under which the 1,600-year figure holds; engaged at GC-00-007.

rulings.would_change_this: The same evidence class as GC-00-007: a datable 2nd-millennium witness to pentateuchal text would restore the 1,600-year span; conversely, a consensus shift dating ALL biblical material after the exile (a live minimalist position) would shrink the span below a millennium and lower the weakened ruling.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate: the 1,600-year figure was the standard span in 19th-century conservative handbooks and remains so in confessional literature today. Favorable component ruling recorded (endpoint well-supported) per §7 symmetry.

components.component: The book of Joel was written about 2,500 years before 1780 — i.e., c. 720 BC (with rounding, c. 620–820 BC)
components.rationale: Joel's date is among the most disputed in the prophetic corpus, argued entirely from internal evidence: proposals run from the 9th to the 2nd c. BC. The current majority favors the Persian period (Wolff: 500–350; Crenshaw: 5th c.; Barton: c. 400), but pre-exilic dates are defended on comparable internal evidence by non-confessional critics (Kapelrud c. 600; Rudolph 597–587) and evangelicals (Stuart: 9th–8th c.); the disagreement does not reduce to confessional alignment, and specialists on both sides concede the evidence underdetermines the answer (no king mentioned, no Assyria/Babylon/Persia among the nations, no Persian loanwords — cutting early; functioning temple community, citations of earlier prophets, Greeks in Joel 3:6 — cutting late). The implied c. 720 BC sits inside the published range, in the minority pre-exilic window.

rulings.weakened_version: Joel was written well over two thousand years before 1780.
rulings.minority_engagement: Not strictly required for genuinely-open, but both sides are documented per §1: the post-exilic majority (Wolff, Crenshaw, Barton) rests on the book's temple-community profile and its citations of other prophets; the pre-exilic case (Thompson's survey; Kapelrud; Rudolph; Stuart) rests on the roster of nations and the linguistic absence of Persian traces. Neither side commands decisive evidence; head-counting favors post-exilic, but the charter forbids counting heads where the evidence base is thin and shared (§5.4). For the weakened version's marginal contestation: Treves's 2nd-c.-BC (Maccabean) dating would push the span to c. 1,940 years, but it is a fringe position without following; on all mainstream datings (9th c.–350 BC) the span is 2,130–2,615 years. No Phase-1 defense object exists (defender skipped disputed verdicts); the verifier's period note (19th-c. scholarship commonly dated Joel 9th–8th c.) is incorporated below.

sources.ref: J. A. Thompson, 'The Date of Joel' (Festschrift article; biblicalstudies.org.uk/pdf/myers/thompson.pdf) — surveys Kapelrud (c. 600), Rudolph (597–587), and the early datings
sources.independence_note: Survey line documenting the pre-exilic side from non-confessional scholarship.

sources.ref: H. W. Wolff, Joel and Amos (Hermeneia): composite work 500–350 BC; J. L. Crenshaw, Joel (Anchor Bible, 1995): 5th c., dating 'guesswork'
sources.independence_note: The post-exilic majority line; independent commentary traditions (German form-critical vs American), though both argue from the same internal data — the shared data pool is the reason the question stays open.

sources.ref: 'The Date of Joel' — exegesisandtheology.com (2023): the 9th-c. case (nations mentioned/not mentioned; Obadiah–Joel–Amos citation order)
sources.independence_note: Conservative statement of the early-date arguments; cited as position, the same arguments appear in Stuart's WBC commentary.

sources.ref: 'Book of Joel' — Wikipedia; academia.edu, 'The Date and Meaning of the Book of Joel'
sources.independence_note: Range documentation (9th–2nd c. proposals); digest of the literature above.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed resolution of the intertextual direction of dependence (does Amos quote Joel or Joel quote Amos?), a firm linguistic-typological placement of Joel's Hebrew, or an external anchor (e.g., an identifiable historical referent for the locust crisis) would collapse the range; a firm Persian-period result would move the as-written ruling to improbable, a firm 8th-c. result to well-supported.
rulings.notes: The dossier's one genuinely-open ruling, and genuinely so: openness is documented on both sides, not a budget artifact. period_standard: the '2,500 years' figure matched the 19th-century scholarly mainstream (9th–8th-c. datings were then common), so even a future improbable ruling would carry the period_standard tag.

components.component: The Deuteronomy 28:56–57 warning was given/recorded about fourteen centuries before AD 70 (c. 1330 BC, Mosaic era)
components.rationale: The 7th-century dating of Deuteronomy's core (Deut 12–26 + 28) is among the most robust results of Pentateuchal criticism: the Josianic connection (de Wette onward), the close parallels between Deut 28:20–44 and Esarhaddon's Succession Treaty of 672 BC (Weinfeld 1965; Steymans 1995), and the 2009 Tell Tayinat find showing the EST imposed on western vassals. On the preponderance, the warning was written roughly seven, not fourteen, centuries before AD 70 — and the very chapter GC quotes is the most firmly 7th-century-anchored text in the Pentateuch. The engaged minority (Kitchen, Berman, Zehnder) does not overturn this: see minority_engagement.

components.component: 'Again was fulfilled the warning prophecy' — the mother-cannibalism of the AD 70 siege as fulfillment of Deut 28
components.rationale: Prophecy-fulfillment framing; out of scope per §9. (The Josephus siege events themselves were verified separately in Phase 1 and are not part of this claim.)

rulings.weakened_version: The Deuteronomy 28 warning was recorded many centuries — on any scholarly dating at least about seven — before the AD 70 siege it is said to foreshadow.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest opposing position: the 2nd-millennium dating defended by Kitchen (Hittite treaty form) and Berman (JBL: the 15th-c. Hittite text CTH 133 parallels Deut 13 more closely than the EST), with Zehnder's peer-published critique of direct EST literary dependence. Actual arguments engaged: Berman's parallel concerns Deut 13, not the ch. 28 curse sequence, where the EST parallels (sequence of curses, Deut 28:20–44) remain the closest known; Levinson & Stackert's response ('The Limitations of Resonance') shows form-resonance cannot carry composition-dating against the convergent linguistic and historical evidence; and even scholars who accept parts of the dependence critique (Crouch, Israel and the Assyrians) retain a 7th-century setting. The recent JETS survey (François, 'Is It Time to Abandon the Hittite Treaty Structure?') documents that the 2nd-millennium position survives today almost entirely within confessional venues. PHASE-1 DEFENDER ANSWERED (§7.4), point by point: (a) the Psalms-consistency complaint criticizes Phase-1 uniformity, not the evidence on Deuteronomy; adjudication rules on this claim's own evidence base (§0.2) — if anything it suggests the Psalm datings were under-scrutinized, not that this one was over-scrutinized; (b) 'White dates the utterance per internal chronology' — as printed, 'the warning prophecy given fourteen centuries before' asserts as history WHEN the warning was given, and asserting the narrative's internal chronology as the actual date presupposes exactly the Mosaic-era origin that the preponderance rejects; (c) 'composition remains hotly contested' — contested among academics is the compositional process within a 1st-millennium window, not a 15th–14th-c. composition; (d) 'the rhetorical function survives any dating' — conceded, and incorporated as the weakened version, ruled well-supported. The downgrade from anachronistic to disputed is thus partially vindicated: the claim is not simply false-by-consensus-with-no-debate, but the debate it points to does not extend to the 14-century figure.

sources.ref: M. Weinfeld, 'Traces of Assyrian Treaty Formulae in Deuteronomy', Biblica 46/4 (1965): 417–427
sources.independence_note: Origin of the EST-parallel line.

sources.ref: H. U. Steymans, Deuteronomium 28 und die adê zur Thronfolgeregelung Asarhaddons (OBO, 1995); Steymans, 'Deuteronomy 28 and Tell Tayinat' (2013)
sources.independence_note: Same comparative-Assyriological genealogy as Weinfeld, but strengthened by new PRIMARY evidence (Tayinat EST copy, 2009) — counted with Weinfeld as one line plus a primary anchor.

sources.ref: B. M. Levinson & J. Stackert, 'The Limitations of Resonance: A Response to Joshua Berman' (SSRN; J. Ancient Judaism)
sources.independence_note: Methodological line, independent of the Steymans genealogy; also documents the minority's actual argument.

sources.ref: J. Berman, JBL papers via 'Deuteronomy and Hittite Treaties' (bibleinterp.arizona.edu, 2014)
sources.independence_note: The peer-published minority itself; confessionally located (Bar-Ilan/orthodox) but venue-validated.

sources.ref: B. François, 'Is It Time to Abandon the Hittite Treaty Structure of Deuteronomy?', JETS 68.4
sources.independence_note: Confessional-venue survey conceding the state of the debate from the minority side; genealogically independent of the critical line.

sources.ref: De Wette's Josianic connection (2 Kings 22) as carried in standard reference literature ('Book of Deuteronomy' overviews)
sources.independence_note: The older, independent anchor of the 7th-c. date, predating and independent of the treaty-parallel line.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed demonstration that the Deut 28 curse sequence predates the Neo-Assyrian treaty tradition (e.g., via a datable earlier Northwest Semitic exemplar beyond Sefire), or any 2nd-millennium textual witness to Deuteronomy. Conversely, a consensus collapse of the Josianic connection toward a still-later (exilic) date would deepen the as-written ruling.
rulings.notes: Originally anachronistic, defense-downgraded to disputed. period_standard candidate: Mosaic dating was the standard of White's milieu and named sources, although de Wette's 7th-c. dating had been in academic circulation since 1805 — the Phase-1 anachronistic instinct was sound and the synthesis tag should reflect it.

evidence_review: GC 35:46 welds two assertions: (1) Rome long and bloodily persecuted the Waldenses, and (2) 'some of whom were observers of the Sabbath' (seventh day). Assertion (1) is uncontested across the whole historiography, from H. C. Lea (1888, local text) to Gabriel Audisio (The Waldensian Dissent, Cambridge UP 1999) and Euan Cameron (Waldenses, Blackwell 2000): inquisitorial campaigns from the 1230s, Innocent VIII's crusade bull of 1487, Merindol 1545, Piedmont 1655. Assertion (2) is the hedged residue of a 19th-century identification whose broader forms (GC-04-046, GC-26-032: 'Waldenses kept the Sabbath through the Dark Ages') were confirmed anachronistic in Phase 1 and are not re-opened here. White's conduit is J. N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath (2nd ed. 1873, local file, pp. 407-418): Andrews builds on Robert Robinson's Ecclesiastical Researches, on Goldast's 17th-century gloss ('Insabbatati... non quod circumciderentur... sed quod in Sabbato judaizarent'), on Ussher's report that 'many' so read the name, and on Robinson's speculation that the Passagini were 'that portion of the Waldenses who lived in the passes of the mountains'; the GC sentence itself carries no source reference in the 1911 apparatus (footnote 1015 covers only the Abyssinian edict; see source/gc_footnotes_1911.txt line 2031). All three legs of the identification have moved against it since the 19th century. Leg 1, etymology: the names sabatati/insabbatati/zabatati/xabatenses/ensabatatz are explained by 12th-13th-century witnesses from the preachers' distinctive cut-open sandals, the badge of apostolic poverty; Lea vol. 1 (local, lines 3232-3235) already rendered Insabbatati 'the Shoed'; the current specialist study is Adam L. Hoose, 'The Sabatati: The Significance of Early Waldensian Shoes, c. 1184-1260,' Speculum 91/2 (2016), which treats the footwear as a contested symbol of Waldensian claims to apostolic authority. The sabbath reading descends from glosses five centuries late (Goldast on Frederick II's 1220 constitution; the inquisition editor Pena), and inferring practice from a hostile nickname is circular. Even confessional Adventist reference scholarship concedes this leg: the SDA source-book note explains that post-Reformation translators rendered 'dies dominicalis' (Lord's day) of the Latin sources as 'Sabbath', and Bacchiocchi accepted the sandal etymology. Leg 2, conflation: the documented seventh-day observers of Latin Christendom in the Waldensian era are the Passagini of Lombardy (Mosaic law incl. circumcision and Saturday; Summa contra haereticos ascribed to Praepositinus; Bonacursus), condemned in Ad abolendam (1184) as a group listed separately from the Poor of Lyons; Lea (local, lines 3561-3564) classes them as 'Judaizing Christians' distinct from the Waldenses. Robinson's guess that Passagini were mountain-pass Waldenses is the root of the conflation Andrews transmitted into GC. Moneta of Cremona's chapter 'De Sabbato, et de die Dominico' (Adversus Catharos et Valdenses, c. 1241) shows the Sabbath argued in exactly this Lombard polemical space, but reading it as proof of Waldensian Saturday rest is an inference no independent specialist draws. Leg 3, direct attestation: the one concrete document is the line published by Doellinger (Beitraege zur Sektengeschichte des Mittelalters II, 1890, p. 662) from a 15th-century Bohemian error catalog: the group keeps no feasts of Mary and the apostles 'nisi diem dominicam' (except the Lord's day), while 'Nonnulli vero cum Judaeis sabbatum celebrant' (but some celebrate the sabbath with the Jews). At face value this attests a judaizing margin ('nonnulli') within or beside a Sunday-keeping festival-rejecting community, in a region whose judaizing currents later surfaced openly in the Moravian Sabbatarians of the 1520s; its heading and group identification could not be independently verified within budget. Against assertion (2) stands the entire direct record of Waldensian practice: inquisition depositions and manuals (Bernard Gui's Practica, the Passau Anonymous catalog for the Austrian-Bohemian communities, the Alpine and Languedoc records underlying Cameron and Audisio) describe Sunday-observing communities that rejected the church's festival calendar, never Saturday rest; B. W. Ball's standard history (The Seventh-Day Men, Clarendon 1994 / James Clarke 2009) finds no traceable seventh-day observance before the Lollard era (first 'modern' sabbatarian c. 1402) and treats organized sabbatarianism as a 17th-century English phenomenon. The published minority case is P. Gerard Damsteegt, 'Decoding Ancient Waldensian Names: New Discoveries,' AUSS 54/2 (2016) 237-258 (popularized in Ministry 2017 and Adventist Review): insabbatati covered both festival-rejecting Sunday keepers and a second class who also kept the seventh day; his evidence is Goldast/Pena, Moneta, the Council of Tarragona (1242) usage, Bernard Gui's 'Ensavates' naming, and Doellinger II 662. The etymological and Moneta legs fail on dating, circularity, and the Passagini alternative; the Doellinger line supports at most a late Bohemian margin. The evidence-weight picture: independent academic scholarship (Hoose in Speculum; Ball at Clarendon; Lea; the Cameron/Audisio/Biller consensus) versus a confessional peer-reviewed position (Damsteegt in AUSS) plus sabbatarian apologetic sites (cogwriter, friendsofsabbath) that all descend from the same Andrews-Goldast-Doellinger chain and count genealogically as one line.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяло одну оговорку из главы 35: среди гонимых Римом вальденсов «некоторые соблюдали субботу». Сами гонения подтверждены полностью, но субботство вальденсов современная медиевистика отвергает: их прозвище insabbatati происходит от особых сандалий проповедников, а богатые протоколы инквизиции описывают общину, чтившую воскресенье и отвергавшую церковные праздники. Аргументы в пользу утверждения восходят к глоссам семнадцатого века и к смешению вальденсов с ломбардской сектой пассагиев, действительно соблюдавшей субботу; слабость этих аргументов признают и адвентистские справочники. Единственный прямой документ, враждебный каталог заблуждений пятнадцатого века из Богемии со словами «некоторые празднуют субботу с иудеями», оставляет открытым лишь сильно ослабленный вариант о поздней богемской периферии движения. Как написано, утверждение получило оценку «маловероятно»; ослабленная версия остаётся открытым вопросом, пока специалисты не изучат богемский документ.

components.component: Rome conducted a long and bloody persecution of the Waldenses
components.rationale: Uncontested across all historiography: inquisitorial campaigns from the 1230s, Innocent VIII's 1487 crusade bull, Merindol 1545, Piedmont 1655. Lea (local), Audisio (Cambridge UP 1999) and Cameron (Blackwell 2000) converge; the Phase-1 verifier likewise treated this half as fact.

components.component: Some of the persecuted medieval Waldenses observed the seventh-day Sabbath
components.rationale: The existential hedge lowers the burden (charter section 6 in reverse: instances would suffice), but the instances are not there. The insabbatati etymology is settled by specialist scholarship as footwear (Hoose, Speculum 2016; Lea; contemporary 12th-13th-century witnesses); the documented medieval seventh-day keepers in the relevant time and place were the Passagini, a distinct sect whose conflation with the Waldenses (Robinson via Andrews) is the genealogical root of the GC claim; the abundant direct record of Waldensian practice (Bernard Gui's Practica, the Passau Anonymous, Alpine and Languedoc records) shows Sunday observance with festival rejection and never Saturday rest; Ball's standard history finds no traceable seventh-day observance before c. 1402. The one direct attestation (Doellinger II 662, 15th-century Bohemian error catalog, 'Nonnulli vero cum Judaeis sabbatum celebrant') is late, hostile in genre, attributes the practice to an unnamed margin of a community it otherwise describes as classic Sunday-keeping festival rejecters, and has no independent specialist endorsement. Clear preponderance against, without a decisive refutation of the existential.

components.component: The persecution is 'a striking illustration of Rome's policy toward those who disagree with her'
components.rationale: The Waldensian case is a standard historiographical exemplar of the medieval church's coercive policy toward dissent (Ad abolendam 1184 onward); as a frame for the persecution component it is accurate. Its rhetorical function in the chapter (implying persecution FOR sabbath-keeping) belongs to the failing fact component, not to this frame.

rulings.weakened_version: A few late-medieval dissenters within the Bohemian Waldensian milieu observed the seventh-day Sabbath as a marginal judaizing deviation, not as a Waldensian practice.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest published defense is P. Gerard Damsteegt, 'Decoding Ancient Waldensian Names: New Discoveries,' AUSS 54/2 (2016) 237-258: the label insabbatati covered both Waldenses who rejected Catholic feast-days ('sabbata') while keeping Sunday, and a second class who also kept the seventh day; his evidence is Goldast's and Pena's early-modern glosses, Moneta of Cremona's chapter 'De Sabbato, et de die Dominico' (c. 1241), the Council of Tarragona (1242) usage of 'insabbatati' for Waldenses, Bernard Gui's 'Ensavates' naming, and the Doellinger II 662 line. This fails for the claim as written on evidential quality, not on the author's confession: (1) the sabbath glosses date from the 17th century, five hundred years after the name arose, while 12th-13th-century witnesses explain it from the sandals, and the specialist study (Hoose, Speculum 2016) settles the etymology; inferring practice from a polemical nickname is circular; (2) Moneta's sabbath chapter belongs to Lombard anti-heretical polemic in the exact region and generation of the Passagini judaizers, and Moneta frames it as defending the church against the charge of transgressing the Sabbath commandment, so attributing Saturday rest to Waldenses from it is an unforced inference no independent specialist draws; (3) name usage at Tarragona and in Gui establishes what Waldenses were called, not what they practiced; (4) Doellinger II 662 attests at most a 15th-century Bohemian margin ('nonnulli') inside a hostile error catalog whose preceding clause describes classic Sunday-keeping festival rejection. Conceded: point (4) is a genuine primary document that mainstream Waldensian scholarship has not engaged, and it keeps the weakened Bohemian-margin version genuinely open. The Phase-1 verifier's framing (Goldast/Pena vs Comba/Spini/Audisio) is incorporated and sharpened: since 2016 the etymology has a dedicated Speculum study, and the defense case reduces to the single unexamined Bohemian document. No Phase-1 defense object exists for this claim (disputed claims skipped the defense stage), so there is no defender argument to answer beyond the verifier's own steelman.

sources.ref: Adam L. Hoose, 'The Sabatati: The Significance of Early Waldensian Shoes, c. 1184-1260,' Speculum 91/2 (2016); accessed via academia.edu abstract and Troy University faculty page
sources.independence_note: Flagship university-press medieval-studies journal; works directly from 12th-13th-century primary sources; independent of the Andrews-Robinson-Goldast chain and of Lea.

sources.ref: H. C. Lea, A History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages, vol. 1 (1888), local file lines 3232-3235 (Insabbatati = 'the Shoed', from the apostolic sandal) and 3561-3564 (Passagii = 'Judaizing Christians', a sect distinct from the Waldenses)
sources.independence_note: Classic academic treatment from primary editions; genealogically independent of Andrews's conduit chain and predates the confessional debate.

sources.ref: B. W. Ball, The Seventh-Day Men: Sabbatarians and Sabbatarianism in England and Wales, 1600-1800 (Clarendon 1994; 2nd ed. James Clarke 2009); via publisher description and the Church History review already cited in Phase 1
sources.independence_note: Standard monograph on documented sabbatarianism; traces antecedents through Lollards, first 'modern' sabbatarian c. 1402; independent of both Lea and Hoose.

sources.ref: P. Gerard Damsteegt, 'Decoding Ancient Waldensian Names: New Discoveries,' AUSS 54/2 (2016) 237-258; PDF blocked (403), argument reconstructed from search digests, Ministry (2017) and Adventist Review popularizations
sources.independence_note: Peer-reviewed within a confessional (Adventist seminary) venue; the modern form of the Andrews position; the apologetic sites citing it (cogwriter, friendsofsabbath) descend from it and count as the same genealogical line.

sources.ref: J. J. I. von Doellinger, Beitraege zur Sektengeschichte des Mittelalters II (1890), p. 662: 15th-century Bohemian error catalog, '...nisi diem dominicam. Nonnulli vero cum Judaeis sabbatum celebrant'
sources.independence_note: Scholarly document collection by an independent (Old Catholic) church historian; the page itself could not be read within budget (archive.org fetch truncated), so wording and group attribution rest on Damsteegt's citation chain; flagged as partially verified.

sources.ref: J. N. Andrews, History of the Sabbath, 2nd ed. (1873), local file pp. 407-418 (lines 18740-19270): the Robinson-Goldast-Ussher etymology argument and the Passagini-as-Waldenses conflation
sources.independence_note: The 19th-century conduit behind the GC sentence; documents the claim's genealogy from inside; period position, not evidence for the practice.

sources.ref: SDA reference note on 'The Sabbath among the Waldenses' (via narkive archive): Latin sources say 'dies dominicalis' (Lord's day); post-Reformation translation practice rendered it 'Sabbath'
sources.independence_note: Confessional concession against interest from the claim's own tradition; same conclusion as the independent scholarship but genealogically distinct from it.

sources.ref: Pasagians: Summa contra haereticos ascribed to Praepositinus of Cremona and Bonacursus, via Wikipedia digest and Lea
sources.independence_note: Encyclopedic digest used only to corroborate the Passagini's distinctness, which Lea (academic, local) independently establishes.

sources.ref: GC 1911 apparatus: source/gc_footnotes_1911.txt line 2031 (footnote 1015 = Geddes, Church History of Ethiopia) — the Waldensian-Sabbath clause itself carries no reference
sources.independence_note: Local source text; establishes that the 1911 edition offers no source for the clause.

rulings.would_change_this: A scholarly edition or specialist study of the Doellinger II 662 catalog identifying its group as genuinely Waldensian, or any medieval trial record (Patschovsky's Bohemian corpus, the Fournier register, the Alpine registers) showing Waldensian communities at Saturday rest, would move the as-written ruling toward the claim; conversely, a specialist demonstration that the Bohemian 'nonnulli' were non-Waldensian judaizers would push the weakened ruling to improbable and the as-written toward discredited.
rulings.notes: Quantifier audit is empty because the claim contains no universal or exclusive terms; the operative quantifier is the existential hedge 'some of whom', which reverses the section 6 burden (instances suffice, but must be well-provenanced Waldensian instances). Evidence-mates GC-04-046 and GC-26-032 (population-level 'Waldenses kept the Sabbath') remain confirmed anachronistic from Phase 1 and were cited only as context. The weakened ruling of genuinely-open rests on positive documentation on both sides: Damsteegt's published reading of a real 15th-century document FOR, the Cameron/Audisio/Ball record and the Hoose etymology AGAINST; it is not a budget euphemism, since the budget sufficed to locate and weigh the decisive document class.

evidence_review: STATE OF THE QUESTION. (1) Provenance of GC's figures. Chapter 17's Lisbon paragraphs are explicit quotations: footnote 481/482 = Encyclopaedia Americana (1831), art. 'Lisbon' note; footnote 480 = Lyell, Principles of Geology p. 495. The wording 'In the course of about six minutes, sixty thousand persons perished' and the marble-quay passage appear verbatim in the Lyell/Encyclopaedia Americana tradition, which descends from the 18th-century account literature (John Michell's 1760 Philosophical Transactions memoir already carried the 60,000 figure). This is one genealogical transmission line, not independent corroboration (§4.4). The 90,000 sentence is White's own summarizing voice, hedged attributively ('It has been estimated'); estimates of 90,000-100,000 did circulate in period letters and 19th-century compendia. (2) Modern casualty scholarship. Alvaro S. Pereira ('The Opportunity of a Disaster', Journal of Economic History 69/2, 2009) estimates 30,000-40,000 deaths in Lisbon (population ~200,000) and 40,000-50,000 total for Portugal, Spain and Morocco from earthquake, tsunami and fire combined; he also notes contemporary reports are vague and partly conflated with the 18-19 November Morocco earthquakes. D. K. Chester ('The 1755 Lisbon earthquake', Progress in Physical Geography 25/3, 2001) records the published range for Lisbon alone as 10,000-100,000 and highlights the Pombaline parish-inquiry archive (Torre do Tombo) as the value-free evidence base. A. C. Araujo (Storicamente, U. Coimbra) surveys contemporary estimates: Pombal gave 6,000-8,000, Father Antony of the Sacrament ~18,000, the apostolic nuncio ~40,000; the city of ~250,000 'would have lost 10% of its population' (~25,000). Modern preponderance therefore sits at roughly 20,000-40,000 for Lisbon; no modern specialist working from the archival record defends 60,000 for the city, let alone 90,000 for the day. (3) Timing. Contemporary reports put the shaking at three shocks over ~9-10 minutes, with violent ground motion lasting 3.5-6 minutes; Moreira de Mendonca (1758) says the earth trembled 'from six to seven minutes'. So 'about six minutes' is a faithful period datum for the DURATION of shaking; the period formula's flaw is welding the whole day's toll (shaking + tsunami ~40 minutes later + three or more days of fire) into that window. (4) Cais de Pedra. The new stone quay at the Terreiro do Paco waterfront was real, recently built at great expense, and was destroyed during the catastrophe; modern geophysics treats its disappearance as ground-failure — liquefaction and lateral spreading of unstable alluvial mud, possibly with an associated waterfront landslide explaining reported whirlpools and vanished boats (2003 'Lisbon 1755: A Case of Triggered Onshore Rupture?' discussion; the 2021 Quaternary International ESI-07 paper documents severe liquefaction and ~5 m fissures). Period letters (the Braddock/Davy account tradition) attest that refugees crowded the quay and perished, and modern narrative histories repeat that hundreds died there. The dramatic layer — the quay swallowed whole 'with all the people on it', 'not one of the dead bodies ever floated', water afterwards unfathomably deep — is the embellished version: no abyss exists at the site, the mechanism was subsidence into shallow mud plus tsunami inundation, and disaster historians note that accounts and images of 1755 grow more exaggerated with distance and time. The core event stands; the total-engulfment absolutes do not. (5) Net. All three claims reproduce period-standard scholarship accurately (White's transmission is faithful; her sources are what aged), and each has a weakened form that modern scholarship sustains.
narrative_ru: Глава 17 описывает лиссабонское землетрясение 1755 года цифрами из справочников девятнадцатого века: 60 тысяч погибших за шесть минут, 90 тысяч за день и мраморная набережная, ушедшая под воду со всеми людьми. Современные исследования (Перейра, Честер, Араужо) насчитывают в Лиссабоне 30-40 тысяч жертв, а по всему региону 40-50 тысяч, поэтому оба числа книги завышены примерно вдвое. Рассказ о набережной Каиш-де-Педра имеет реальное ядро: причал действительно осел из-за разжижения грунта и укрывшиеся на нём люди погибли, но слой «все до единого, ни одно тело не всплыло» историки относят к преувеличениям очевидцев. В ослабленных формулировках («десятки тысяч погибших», «набережная разрушилась, погибло много людей») все три утверждения подтверждаются. Уайт при этом точно цитирует свои источники (Лайель и Encyclopaedia Americana 1831 года); устарели сами источники, а не её пересказ. Картину изменило бы демографическое исследование приходских книг, которое подтвердило бы потери, близкие к 90 тысячам.

quantifier_audit: in the course of about six minutes (hedged window applied to the whole toll)

components.component: About 60,000 people perished at Lisbon in the 1755 disaster
components.rationale: Modern archival-demographic scholarship converges far lower: Pereira (JEH 2009) 30,000-40,000 for Lisbon; Araujo ~10% of a population of ~250,000, with contemporary estimates topping out at the nuncio's 40,000; Chester (2001) records 10,000-100,000 only as the historical range of published figures. 60,000 for the city has no modern archival defender; it is the Michell-Lyell transmission figure.

components.component: Those deaths occurred within the ~6-minute course of the shaking
components.rationale: The day's toll accumulated across the shaking, the tsunami ~40 minutes later, and fires burning for days. Even period-internally, the formula compresses the whole toll into the first shocks. No modern reconstruction assigns anything like the full toll to the shaking window alone.

components.component: The violent shaking lasted about six minutes
components.rationale: Contemporary reports give 3.5-6 minutes of violent motion in three shocks over ~9-10 minutes; Moreira de Mendonca (1758) says six to seven minutes. As a duration datum, 'about six minutes' is period-accurate and consistent with modern reconstruction.

rulings.weakened_version: Within a few minutes of violent shaking, collapsing buildings killed tens of thousands in Lisbon, part of a disaster that claimed on the order of 30,000-40,000 lives in the city.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case for the claim is the traditional historiography itself: Michell (1760) and two centuries of repetition, including Lyell and the compendia White cites, carried 60,000 as the standard figure, grounded in the density of the city and the crowded All Saints' Day churches. It fails per charter §5.4 (head-counting vs weighing): the repetitions are one genealogical chain from impressionistic period letters, while the specialists who examined the Pombaline parish-inquiry archive and demographic data (Pereira 2009; the Portuguese scholarship surveyed by Araujo and Chester) converge on 20,000-40,000 for Lisbon. Even the highest contemporary official estimate (the nuncio's 40,000) falls short of the claim. No Phase-1 defense object exists (disputed claims skipped the stage); the adjacent successful defenses of GC-17-036/040 established that White quotes Lyell-tradition material faithfully, which this ruling incorporates: the failure lies in the period figure, not in her transmission of it.

sources.ref: A. S. Pereira, 'The Opportunity of a Disaster: The Economic Impact of the 1755 Lisbon Earthquake', Journal of Economic History 69/2 (2009); Lisbon 30,000-40,000, total 40,000-50,000
sources.independence_note: Modern economic-history reconstruction from Portuguese archival data; independent of the Michell-Lyell transmission chain.

sources.ref: D. K. Chester, 'The 1755 Lisbon earthquake', Progress in Physical Geography 25/3 (2001) 363-383
sources.independence_note: Geography/seismology state-of-question survey using the Torre do Tombo parish-inquiry archive; independent of Pereira in discipline and method.

sources.ref: A. C. Araujo, 'The 1755 Lisbon earthquake: the catastrophe and the reconstruction', Storicamente (storicamente.org/araujo_1755_lisbon_earthquake); contemporary estimates 6,000-40,000, city lost ~10% of ~250,000
sources.independence_note: Coimbra historian working from contemporary Portuguese testimony; third independent line.

sources.ref: Lyell, Principles of Geology (1830) p. 495 / Encyclopaedia Americana (1831) art. Lisbon note / Michell, Phil. Trans. (1760) — the '60,000 in six minutes' formula (GC footnotes 480-481)
sources.independence_note: One genealogical line; establishes the claim's pedigree and White's faithful transmission, not its accuracy.

sources.ref: Wikipedia '1755 Lisbon earthquake' (duration 3.5-6 minutes; toll digest 30,000-40,000)
sources.independence_note: Digests Pereira for tolls — genealogically dependent on source 1; used only for the duration datum and orientation.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed demographic study of Lisbon parish burial registers and the Pombaline inquiry yielding city deaths near 60,000, or archival evidence that contemporary official counts systematically undercounted by a factor above 1.5.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate: the figure is verbatim from the sources GC footnotes (Lyell / Encyclopaedia Americana 1831); Phase-1 vocabulary would have called this near-anachronistic. The claim sits inside quotation marks in GC — White asserts it by adoption, as credible history.

components.component: Estimates of ~90,000 deaths for the day circulated (attributive layer: 'It has been estimated')
components.rationale: True as a report of estimates: period letters and 19th-century compendia carried figures of 90,000-100,000; Chester records the published range for Lisbon reaching 100,000. The sentence honestly flags itself as an estimate.

components.component: About 90,000 people actually lost their lives on 1 November 1755 (the operative assertion the paragraph deploys)
components.rationale: Roughly double the top of modern scholarship: Pereira's TOTAL for Portugal, Spain and Morocco across earthquake, tsunami and days of fire is 40,000-50,000, and the same-day toll is lower still (fire deaths accrued over days; part of the Morocco toll belongs to the separate 18-19 November earthquakes). No modern archival estimate approaches 90,000 for the day.

rulings.weakened_version: Contemporary estimates of the death toll ran as high as 90,000, though tens of thousands — on modern reckoning 30,000-40,000 in Lisbon and up to ~50,000 overall — lost their lives in the catastrophe.
rulings.minority_engagement: The best defense is the sentence's own hedge: 'It has been estimated' is literally true, since 90,000-100,000 figures did circulate, and White's era compendia treated them as sober history. The hedge is credited in component 1 and in the weakened ruling; it does not rescue the sentence as written because its rhetorical function in the paragraph is assertive — the figure serves as the climactic measure of the disaster ('on that fatal day'), not as historiographical caution. On substance, no qualified modern position defends ~90,000 same-day deaths: the highest contemporary official estimate was 40,000 (the nuncio, per Araujo), and Pereira's maximal modern total including Morocco and the fires is 50,000. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's own rationale (90,000 as a high period estimate) is incorporated.

sources.ref: A. S. Pereira, JEH 69/2 (2009): total Portugal+Spain+Morocco 40,000-50,000; notes conflation with the 18-19 November Morocco earthquakes
sources.independence_note: Modern archival reconstruction; independent of the period transmission chain.

sources.ref: A. C. Araujo, Storicamente: contemporary estimates 6,000-8,000 (Pombal), ~18,000 (Father Antony of the Sacrament), ~40,000 (apostolic nuncio); city lost ~10% of ~250,000
sources.independence_note: Independent line from Portuguese contemporary testimony; shows even period officialdom never reached 90,000.

sources.ref: D. K. Chester, Progress in Physical Geography 25/3 (2001): published range 10,000-100,000 for Lisbon; parish-inquiry evidence base
sources.independence_note: Third discipline (physical geography); documents both the legacy range and the archival corrective.

sources.ref: Period estimate tradition (letters and 19th-century compendia carrying 90,000-100,000), unfootnoted in GC but standard in White's reference literature
sources.independence_note: Establishes the attributive layer's truth; genealogically one chain with the 60,000 formula.

rulings.would_change_this: A modern peer-reviewed demographic reconstruction from burial registers and the Pombaline inquiry supporting on the order of 90,000 same-day deaths across the affected region.
rulings.notes: This sentence is White's own voice, outside the quotation marks and unfootnoted. period_standard candidate: 90,000 was a live figure in 19th-century reference works.

quantifier_audit: with ALL the people on it
NOT ONE of the dead bodies EVER floated to the surface

components.component: A new quay of stone/marble, recently built at great expense, subsided/was destroyed at Lisbon during the catastrophe
components.rationale: The Cais de Pedra at the Terreiro do Paco waterfront was real and disappeared during the disaster; modern geophysics explains it as liquefaction/lateral spreading of unstable alluvial ground, consistent with the severe liquefaction and ~5 m fissures documented for 1755 (2021 Quaternary International ESI-07 study; 2003 'triggered onshore rupture' discussion).

components.component: A crowd that had taken refuge on the quay perished when it went down / was overwhelmed
components.rationale: Attested by contemporary letters (the Braddock/Davy account tradition) and repeated in modern narrative histories ('hundreds of people who had sought refuge'); survivors demonstrably fled to the open waterfront. Testimony-based and not independently countable, but no qualified position denies mass casualties at the quayside.

components.component: The quay sank with ALL the people on it and NOT ONE body EVER floated to the surface (total-engulfment layer)
components.rationale: The absolute layer belongs to the embellished version of the story (period letters even claimed unfathomable depths where the quay stood). No abyss exists at the site: the mechanism was subsidence into shallow tidal mud plus tsunami inundation, and the ground there was later rebuilt as the Praca do Comercio waterfront. Disaster historians note that 1755 accounts and images grow more exaggerated with distance and time; 'not one body ever floated' is unverifiable in principle by any eyewitness and carries the burden of its own absolutes (§6).

rulings.weakened_version: The newly built stone quay subsided into the Tagus during the catastrophe, and many of the people who had taken refuge on it perished in the waters.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case for the literal account: contemporary eyewitness letters assert the quay went down with people and boats and that nothing resurfaced, and modern seismologists take the sinking itself seriously — the 2003 geophysics discussion treats the 'widely debated sinking of the Lisbon quay' as probably real liquefaction-driven ground failure, possibly a waterfront landslide explaining reported whirlpools. That position is engaged and largely CONCEDED for the core event (components 1-2 ruled well-supported/probable). It does not sustain the absolutes: no eyewitness could verify that not one body ever surfaced; the associated unfathomable-depth detail was falsified by the site's actual shallow bathymetry and later rebuilding; and the account genre ('extraordinary events RELATED to have occurred', as GC's own quoted source hedges) marks it as second-hand marvel literature. White again transmits her cited source (Encyclopaedia Americana 1831) faithfully — the embellishment is the period source's, not hers. No Phase-1 defense object exists; the verifier's 'embellished legend vs real ground failure' framing is incorporated and sharpened into the component split.

sources.ref: 'Lisbon 1755: A Case of Triggered Onshore Rupture?' (2003, geophysics literature): quay sinking and whirlpools as ground liquefaction / waterfront landslide
sources.independence_note: Seismological line; supports the core event, undercuts the bottomless-abyss version.

sources.ref: 'The AD 1755 Lisbon Earthquake-Tsunami: seismic source modelling from ESI-07 environmental data', Quaternary International (2021): documented liquefaction, ~5 m fissures, tsunami inundation ~250-350 m at Terreiro do Paco
sources.independence_note: Independent environmental-effects study; same physical picture from a different team and dataset.

sources.ref: Modern Lisbon urban-history reconstructions (lisbonquake.com project; getLISBON): Cais de Pedra 'probably sunk in the unstable mud, with the multitude of people who had taken refuge'
sources.independence_note: Popular digests of Portuguese scholarship; corroborate the crowd-deaths core, not the absolutes.

sources.ref: Braddock/Davy contemporary account tradition via Encyclopaedia Americana (1831) art. Lisbon note (GC footnote 481); T. D. Kendrick, The Lisbon Earthquake (1956) as the standard historical treatment of the account literature
sources.independence_note: The claim's own genealogy: period marvel-literature layer; one transmission line into the 19th-century compendia.

rulings.would_change_this: Harbor archaeology or geotechnical coring at the Terreiro do Paco waterfront documenting a deep collapse structure with quay masonry and human remains would support the literal account; conversely, published body-recovery records from the Tagus shore would finish off the 'not one body' absolute.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate. GC's quoted source itself hedges the story ('related to have occurred'); the as-written ruling targets the substantive account the paragraph deploys, and the attributive reading is credited in the minority engagement.

evidence_review: STATE OF THE QUESTION. All four claims are slices of one narrative: Josephus, Bellum Judaicum 6.236-266. Josephus (an eyewitness of the siege, though not of the war council; a Flavian client whose War was endorsed by Titus himself) reports: (a) a council of war at which some commanders urged destroying the temple under the laws of war, others conditional sparing, and Titus declared that 'we ought not to revenge ourselves on things that are inanimate' and decided to preserve it as an ornament of Roman rule (BJ 6.236-243, confirmed verbatim in the fetched Whiston text); (b) a soldier who 'without staying for any orders... hurried on by a certain divine fury' threw fire into a golden window (6.252); (c) Titus rushing from his tent, signalling with his right hand and shouting to quench the fire, unheard or ignored amid the din, soldiers hurling more brands, heaps of bodies around the altar and blood running down its steps (6.254-259); (d) Titus entering the holy place with his commanders, finding it 'far superior to what the relations of foreigners contained', making a renewed attempt and ordering the centurion Liberalius to beat refractory soldiers with staves, in vain (6.260-266). Crucially, the same Josephan book also has Titus ordering the temple GATES set on fire when sparing efforts cost him soldiers (BJ 6.228), and after the fall he razed city and temple; so even the friendliest source contains no absolute preservation order. THE COUNTER-TRADITION: Sulpicius Severus, Chronica 2.30.6-7 (c. 400 CE) reports the opposite council outcome: 'others, and Titus himself, judged that the temple should above all be destroyed', to abolish the religion of Jews and Christians. Since Jacob Bernays (1861) this passage has been widely held to derive from the lost book of Tacitus's Histories; the derivation is accepted by editors of the critical editions, though H. Montefiore (Historia 11, 1962) challenged Bernays's stylistic case and proposed a non-Tacitean classical source (Antonius Julianus), to which Momigliano replied that this substitutes Tacitus's probable source for Tacitus, 'no gain and greater obscurity'. MODERN BALANCE: Leoni's own peer-reviewed survey (JJS 58.1, 2007) concedes that 'the vast majority of scholars have followed Bernays in giving more credence to the account of... Sulpicius Severus', i.e. deliberate destruction with Josephus whitewashing his patron; named exemplars of that line include T. D. Barnes ('The Sack of the Temple in Josephus and Tacitus') and J. Rives ('Flavian Religious Policy and the Destruction of the Jerusalem Temple'), both in Edmondson/Mason/Rives (eds.), Flavius Josephus and Flavian Rome (OUP 2005); the older literature includes Weiler, Yavetz and Alon. Contemporary Flavian imagery (Valerius Flaccus's proem picturing Titus 'scattering firebrands' at Jerusalem, cited in Mason's discussion) shows the regime celebrated rather than regretted the destruction. ON THE OTHER SIDE: T. Leoni (JJS 2007) defends Josephus's narrative as 'unequivocally clear, consistent and substantially trustworthy'; T. Rajak (Josephus: The Historian and His Society, 2nd ed. 2002) deems either council outcome plausible but gives greater weight to Josephus as the eyewitness; M. Goodman considers the accidental-fire account plausible given the difficulty of controlling fire; S. Mason (A History of the Jewish War, CUP 2016) is deflationary toward both retrospective framings ('no reason to imagine that Titus had a policy concerning the city or temple'). This is a documented disagreement among qualified specialists arguing from the same primary evidence with comparable methods, not reducible to confessional alignment: eyewitness proximity and internal coherence (Leoni, Rajak) versus patronage bias, the internal gates-order tension, Flavian celebration imagery, and the probable Tacitean counter-tradition (Barnes, Rives). Under §5.4 this is weighing, not head-counting: the majority lean is real, but the minority holds the best-placed witness. VERDICT STRUCTURE FOR THE DOSSIER: components asserting WHAT JOSEPHUS'S SCENE CONTAINS and what is common to all traditions (the assault, the fire spread by soldiers, the massacre of those sheltering in the temple, Titus's entry into the sanctuary) are well-attested; components asserting TITUS'S PRESERVATIVE INTENT AND ORDERS as unqualified fact sit exactly on the open Josephus-vs-Tacitus crux and are genuinely open; the one component asserting an absolute order 'that not one stone be touched' given from Olivet is attested in no ancient source in that form, is contradicted within Josephus's own account (gates fired at Titus's order, BJ 6.228), and is a 19th-century dramatized inversion of Matthew 24:2 in the Milman layer that White used. NINETEENTH-CENTURY CONDUIT: the 1911 appendix credits Milman's History of the Jews, book 16; paragraphs 41-43 are Milman verbatim (Phase-1 finding), and paragraphs 39-40 are a paraphrase of the same layer; Milman follows Josephus's dramatization, so the pro-Titus framing was period-standard historiography, predating the post-Bernays turn's penetration into popular histories.
narrative_ru: Досье разбирает сцену гибели Иерусалимского храма в 70 году: хотел ли Тит спасти святилище, как пишет очевидец Иосиф Флавий, или сам приказал его сжечь, как утверждает традиция Сульпиция Севера, восходящая, вероятно, к утраченной книге Тацита. Учёные спорят об этом с 1861 года: большинство после Бернайса не доверяет проимператорской версии Иосифа, однако её защищают и серьёзные специалисты, включая Раджак и Леони. Поэтому три из четырёх утверждений получили вердикт «действительно открыто»: наука сегодня не может решить, пытался ли Тит остановить пожар, хотя резня укрывшихся в храме и сам штурм сомнению не подлежат. Четвёртое утверждение, будто Тит с Елеонской горы приказал «не трогать ни одного камня» храма, в буквальном виде маловероятно: такого приказа нет даже у Иосифа, у которого Тит сам велел поджечь храмовые ворота. В ослабленной формулировке и это утверждение возвращается в разряд открытых, поскольку опирается на свидетельство очевидца. Картину изменила бы находка утраченной части «Историй» Тацита или новое независимое свидетельство о военном совете Тита.

quantifier_audit: not one stone of it be touched

components.component: Titus was filled with horror at the heaps of corpses in the valleys (and, per the printed quote, would willingly have ended the scene and spared Jerusalem the full measure of her doom)
components.rationale: Josephus reports the scene as an eyewitness (BJ 5.519: Titus groaned on his rounds and called God to witness the carnage was not his doing). No rival report exists, but the scene belongs precisely to the exculpatory Titus-portrait that the Bernays line (Barnes, Rives) treats as Flavian whitewash and that Leoni and Rajak defend. It stands or falls with the contested portrait; qualified specialists divide on comparable evidence (see evidence_review).

components.component: Titus wished to spare Jerusalem and the temple (intent attribution)
components.rationale: Both rival motive accounts have contested documentary bases of comparable kind: Josephus's contemporary eyewitness testimony (endorsed by the interested party) for preservation, versus the probably-Tacitean Sulpicius tradition plus Flavian celebration imagery (Valerius Flaccus) for deliberate destruction. Per §3 the motive component is not capped below genuinely-open, since neither side's motive account rests on undisputed documents; per §5.4 the split among qualified specialists (Barnes/Rives vs Leoni/Rajak/Goodman/Mason) is genuine.

components.component: From the crest of Olivet Titus gave command that not one stone of the temple be touched
components.rationale: No ancient source reports such an order in this form or setting. Josephus's actual content is weaker and later: a war-council decision not to burn the holy house (BJ 6.236-243) and orders to quench fires; and the same book contains a well-provenanced counterexample to the absolute, Titus's own order to set the temple gates on fire (BJ 6.228), plus the post-fall razing. The 'not one stone' phrasing is a dramatized inversion of Matthew 24:2 in the Milman layer White paraphrased, engineered for the chapter's providential irony ('One greater than he had declared that not one stone was to be left upon another'). One well-provenanced counterexample within the friendliest source caps the as-written absolute at improbable (§6.3).

rulings.weakened_version: Titus was appalled by the carnage of the siege and initially resolved that the temple should, if possible, be spared.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest defenses of the claim: (1) T. Leoni (JJS 58.1, 2007) argues Josephus's whole burning narrative is 'unequivocally clear, consistent and substantially trustworthy', and T. Rajak (2002) gives greater weight to Josephus as the eyewitness; if they are right, Titus did decide to spare the temple. Answer: even granting Leoni in full, Josephus contains no Olivet command 'that not one stone be touched' and does contain Titus's order to burn the temple gates (BJ 6.228), so the claim as written overstates the maximally friendly source; the Leoni/Rajak defense therefore rescues only the weakened version, which is ruled genuinely-open. (2) The dramatization defense: the sentence merely condenses Milman's period-standard retelling. Conceded as provenance (see notes), but under §2 the as-written ruling addresses the sentence's factual assertion, and no ancient evidence attests the absolute order. No Phase-1 defense object exists for this claim (disputed skipped the defense stage).

sources.ref: Josephus, Bellum Judaicum 6.228, 6.236-243 (Whiston trans., penelope.uchicago.edu, full text fetched; gates order also in the Loyola Wars VI text)
sources.independence_note: The claim's own underlying source, read in scholarly context; independent of the modern debate literature.

sources.ref: T. D. Barnes, 'The Sack of the Temple in Josephus and Tacitus', in Edmondson/Mason/Rives (eds.), Flavius Josephus and Flavian Rome (OUP 2005)
sources.independence_note: Bernays-line case against the Josephan exculpation; same OUP volume as Rives but an independent argument.

sources.ref: T. Leoni, "'Against Caesar's wishes': Flavius Josephus as a Source for the Burning of the Temple", Journal of Jewish Studies 58.1 (2007) 39-51
sources.independence_note: Peer-reviewed defense of Josephus; also documents the majority lean ('vast majority... followed Bernays'); genealogically independent of Barnes.

sources.ref: H. H. Milman, History of the Jews, book 16 (credited in the GC 1911 appendix for this section)
sources.independence_note: White's 19th-century conduit; follows Josephus's dramatization, not an independent witness.

rulings.would_change_this: Recovery of the lost portion of Tacitus's Histories book 5, or any ancient text attesting an early absolute preservation order by Titus, would reopen the as-written ruling; a verifiable ancient source for the Olivet command specifically would move it toward genuinely-open.
rulings.notes: Period-standard layer: the dramatization follows Milman (1911 appendix credit); Milman's exact wording could not be checked (page bot-blocked live and via archive), so whether the Olivet 'not one stone' staging is Milman's or White's heightening remains unverified. This affects only the period_standard attribution, not the ruling. Candidate for period_standard: true in synthesis if Milman's text is confirmed to carry the same dramatization.

components.component: Titus at last decided to take the temple by storm
components.rationale: The Roman assault on the temple complex in August 70 CE is undisputed across all traditions (Josephus BJ 6; the counter-tradition disputes intent toward the building, not the assault).

components.component: He determined that, if possible, the temple should be saved from destruction
components.rationale: The exact crux of the Josephus-vs-Sulpicius/Tacitus debate: Josephus's council narrative (BJ 6.236-243) asserts it; Sulpicius Severus, Chronica 2.30.6-7 (probably from Tacitus per Bernays, contra Montefiore) asserts the opposite council outcome. Leoni's survey concedes the post-Bernays majority favors deliberate destruction, but Rajak, Leoni, Goodman and (deflationarily) Mason keep the eyewitness account live on comparable evidential grounds. Per §3, neither motive account rests on undisputed documents, so no cap below genuinely-open applies; per §5.4 this is a weighed standoff, not head-counting.

components.component: His preservation commands were disregarded by the soldiers
components.rationale: Conditional on such commands having been given, which is the same contested nexus: Josephus reports orders to quench and their disregard (BJ 6.254-266); on the Bernays reading these orders are the invention, on Leoni's reading they are the history.

rulings.weakened_version: Titus decided to storm the temple while preferring, if possible, to preserve the building, and the fire escaped anyone's full control.
rulings.minority_engagement: Not required for genuinely-open; both sides are documented in evidence_review (Barnes/Rives/Bernays majority vs Leoni/Rajak/Goodman/Mason). The Phase-1 verifier's rationale (unresolved Josephus-vs-Tacitus dispute) is confirmed by independent research, not inherited as authority. No Phase-1 defense object exists.

sources.ref: Josephus, Bellum Judaicum 6.236-266 (Whiston trans., penelope.uchicago.edu, fetched)
sources.independence_note: Eyewitness primary source; pro-Flavian bias documented in the scholarship.

sources.ref: Sulpicius Severus, Chronica 2.30.6-7, with Bernays's Tacitean derivation; Montefiore, 'Sulpicius Severus and Titus' Council of War', Historia 11 (1962); Momigliano's rejoinder
sources.independence_note: The rival ancient tradition; genealogically independent of Josephus.

sources.ref: T. D. Barnes (OUP 2005) and J. Rives (OUP 2005), in Flavius Josephus and Flavian Rome
sources.independence_note: Named carriers of the post-Bernays majority; same volume, independent arguments.

sources.ref: T. Leoni (JJS 2007); T. Rajak, Josephus: The Historian and His Society (2nd ed. 2002); S. Mason, A History of the Jewish War (CUP 2016)
sources.independence_note: The qualified defense/deflationary side; three independent authors and venues.

rulings.would_change_this: A new fragment securing (or breaking) the Tacitean derivation of Sulpicius's council report, or any independent contemporary evidence of the war council's decision, would collapse the standoff in one direction.
rulings.notes: Tie at the claim minimum between the motive component (intent to preserve) and the fact component (orders disregarded); §3 priority fact > motive sets failing_component_kind.

components.component: Titus rushed to the burning temple and commanded the soldiers to quench the flames, but was unheeded
components.rationale: The heart of the contested exculpatory scene (BJ 6.254-259: Titus shouting and signalling with his right hand, unheard amid the din). The Bernays line treats precisely this tableau as Josephus's whitewash of his patron; Leoni defends it as consistent eyewitness reporting, and Rajak weighs Josephus's proximity heavily. Qualified specialists divide on comparable evidence (see evidence_review).

components.component: Soldiers hurled more blazing brands into the chambers adjoining the temple
components.rationale: Common ground of both traditions: Josephus reports soldiers spreading the fire against orders; the Sulpicius/Tacitus tradition has them burning it under orders. That Roman soldiers spread the fire is not in dispute; only the authorization is.

components.component: Soldiers slaughtered in great numbers those who had found shelter there
components.rationale: Undisputed across the traditions and the modern literature: Josephus describes heaps of bodies around the altar and blood running down its steps (BJ 6.259ff), and no ancient or modern account disputes the mass killing at the temple's fall. The Phase-1 verifier already noted the massacre as undisputed.

rulings.weakened_version: When the fire broke out, Titus made some effort to check it, but the soldiers' violence prevailed and great numbers of those sheltering in the temple were slaughtered.
rulings.minority_engagement: Not required for genuinely-open; both sides documented in evidence_review. Note the asymmetry inside the claim: the massacre and fire-spreading components are well-supported, and only the quench-order component holds the claim at genuinely-open; the weakened version cannot rise above it because the order either was or was not given, and no quantifier relaxation touches that. No Phase-1 defense object exists.

sources.ref: Josephus, Bellum Judaicum 6.249-266 (Whiston trans., penelope.uchicago.edu, fetched)
sources.independence_note: Eyewitness source of the scene; bias documented.

rulings.would_change_this: Same evidence class as GC-01-104: a secured Tacitus fragment or independent contemporary testimony on Titus's conduct at the fire. For the massacre components, only a wholesale archaeological-demographic revision of the siege's end could move them, which nothing currently suggests.
rulings.notes: All three components are of kind fact, so no cross-kind tie arises; the minimum is set by the quench-order component.

quantifier_audit: impossible (found it impossible to check the rage of the soldiery)

components.component: Titus entered the temple with his officers and surveyed the interior of the sacred edifice
components.rationale: Josephus states it plainly (BJ 6.260: he saw the holy place, 'far superior to what the relations of foreigners contained'), and a conqueror inspecting the sanctuary is plausible on either tradition; the late rabbinic tradition (Gittin 56b), hostile to Titus, likewise has him entering the sanctuary, though in legendary form. Single primary witness plus a converging hostile legend supports probable rather than well-supported.

components.component: The splendor filled them with wonder
components.rationale: Josephus's own dramatized register ('far superior to what the relations of foreigners contained'); as a characterization of the visit it is consistent with the attested magnificence of the Herodian temple and carries no counter-report, but rests on the one witness.

components.component: Titus found it impossible to check the rage of the soldiery
components.rationale: Presupposes that he was trying to check them, which is the contested exculpatory crux; on the Sulpicius/Tacitus reading there was nothing to check because the destruction was ordered. Same standoff as the dossier's core question.

components.component: He made a last effort to save the holy place, exhorting the soldiers to stay the conflagration
components.rationale: The climax of Josephus's exculpatory scene (BJ 6.262-266, including the order to the centurion Liberalius to beat refractory soldiers): defended as substantially trustworthy by Leoni and weighed favorably by Rajak, treated as Flavian fiction by the Bernays line (Barnes, Rives). Comparable evidential quality on both sides (see evidence_review).

rulings.weakened_version: Titus entered and surveyed the temple's splendid interior shortly before its destruction and made an effort to check the fire before the sanctuary burned.
rulings.minority_engagement: Not required for genuinely-open; both sides documented in evidence_review. The sentence is verbatim Milman (Phase-1 finding, 1911 appendix credit), and Milman transmits Josephus's dramatization wholesale; the entry-and-splendor layer is the best-attested part (probable), the rescue-attempt layer the contested part. No Phase-1 defense object exists.

sources.independence_note: Defense side; independent venues.

sources.independence_note: Bernays-line side; same volume, independent arguments.
