rulings.would_change_this: Same class as the dossier core: recovery of Tacitus's account or new contemporary testimony on Titus at the fire. For the entry component alone, a peer-reviewed study discrediting Josephus's sanctuary-entry report (or corroborating it independently) would move that component.
rulings.notes: Tie at the claim minimum between the last-effort component (fact) and the impossible-to-check frame; §3 priority fact > frame sets failing_component_kind.

evidence_review: State of the question. (1) Mechanics: since G. E. M. de Ste. Croix ('Why Were the Early Christians Persecuted?', Past & Present 26, 1963; repr. Christian Persecution, Martyrdom, and Orthodoxy, OUP 2006), the consensus holds that pre-Decian persecution was sporadic, local, and accusatory rather than inquisitorial: Trajan's rescript to Pliny (Ep. 10.97, read locally in the Melmoth edition) rules 'Do not go out of your way to look for them' (conquirendi non sunt) and bans anonymous accusations; Pliny's own letter (10.96) shows a reactive tribunal process. James Corke-Webster ('By Whom Were Early Christians Persecuted?', Past & Present 261, 2023, open access) pushes further, locating agency in local accusers and governors even in the empire-wide phases. Against this baseline stand three documented episodes of active state pursuit: the Decian order of 250 (Dionysius of Alexandria hunted by a frumentarius who searched 'roads, rivers, and fields', Eusebius HE 6.40, local text), Valerian's edicts of 257-258 (clergy executed, Christians banned from entering the coemeteria; Sixtus II seized by soldiers inside a catacomb during worship on 6 August 258, Cyprian Ep. 80), and the Diocletianic edicts of 303-304 (churches razed, scriptures burned, 'all the rulers of the churches in every place' imprisoned, HE 8.2/8.6, local text). A pre-Decian local manhunt is also documented: Polycarp's pursuers 'pushed the search with vigor' and tortured a servant to learn his hiding place (HE 4.15.11, local text). Between these phases lie long stretches of de facto toleration, including the Long Peace of 260-303. (2) Scale: the primary evidence itself spreads from Origen c. 248 (Contra Celsum 3.8: martyrs are 'some, on special occasions, and these individuals who can be easily numbered', verified verbatim) to Eusebius's eyewitness Thebaid report (HE 8.9.3: ten to a hundred killed in a day 'for a long series of years'). Modern estimates: Rodney Stark under 1,000 total (criticized as 'impossibly low' by T. D. Barnes); W. H. C. Frend 3,000-3,500 dead in the Great Persecution alone; Min Seok Shin (The Great Persecution: A Historical Re-examination, reviewed BMCR 2020.08.21) over 23,500 under Diocletian. Candida Moss (The Myth of Persecution, 2013) argues the 'Age of Martyrs' is a later construction; her named critics (Paul L. Maier, Michael Heiser) counter that she understates real, well-attested persecution; even Moss concedes the Decian-Valerianic and Diocletianic episodes. Net: totals of at least several thousand are the floor of serious specialist estimates; the legendary vast-multitude picture is rejected. (3) Catacombs: specialist archaeology (Vincenzo Fiocchi Nicolai, Pontifical Institute of Christian Archaeology; Leonard V. Rutgers, Utrecht; two genealogically independent lines) holds the catacombs were legally registered communal cemeteries and memorial sites, known to the authorities, unfit for habitation; the hiding-place picture is a romantic legend. The documented kernel: Valerian's ban on entering the cemeteries and Sixtus II's arrest there presuppose clandestine assembly during proscription, and even debunking scholarship concedes catacombs were 'only infrequently places of hiding during persecution' (SBTS journal piece cited by the Phase-1 defender). (4) Motives: the documented motive record for early persecution is Nero's scapegoating (Tacitus, Ann. 15.44), punishment of contumacia and rejection of the gods (Pliny-Trajan), and pax deorum anxiety (Ste. Croix). Pliny 10.96 does attest pagan concern over 'almost deserted' temples and a collapsed market for sacrificial victims; for the Great Persecution there is direct documentary evidence of a cult-restoration motive (Galerius's 311 edict; Maximinus Daia's rescripts to Tyre and to Lycia-Pamphylia answering petitions to expel Christians; Porphyry and Hierocles as ideologues; Diocletian's traditionalism). What has no documentary basis is a unified 'Paganism' acting from foresight of Christianity's eventual triumph across the whole era. (5) Religious freedom: Robert Louis Wilken (Liberty in the Things of God, Yale UP 2019) documents that Tertullian coined libertas religionis (Apol. 24; Ad Scapulam 2) and Lactantius argued religion cannot be coerced, unsettling the pure-anachronism objection; but neither was condemned for it, and Lactantius's argument fed the 313 Milan settlement. After establishment, the documented mechanism runs the other way: the Edict of Thessalonica (380) and Codex Theodosianus 16.5 proscribe some 65 named heresies with penalties up to death, dissenting assembly is criminalized, and Augustine's anti-Donatist theory justifies coercion against dissenters' pleas for freedom of conscience; no late-antique or early-medieval text is known that brands toleration doctrine itself 'heresy'. Formal condemnations of the toleration principle come only much later (Exsurge Domine art. 33 in 1520; Mirari Vos 1832; Syllabus 1864), outside this chapter's era.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяло пять утверждений главы 2 о размахе и механике гонений на ранних христиан. Современная наука видит спорадические местные преследования с несколькими короткими общеимперскими кампаниями третьего и начала четвёртого века, поэтому образ непрерывной охоты «где бы они ни искали убежища» опровергается рескриптом Траяна, прямо запретившим разыскивать христиан, а объяснение гонений тем, что язычество «предвидело торжество евангелия», остаётся недокументированным. Утверждение о «великом множестве» мучеников, напротив, скорее устояло: серьёзные современные оценки дают от нескольких тысяч казнённых и выше, хотя ревизионистская линия Кандиды Мосс оспаривает традиционный размах. Катакомбы служили кладбищами и местами тайных собраний в периоды запретов, но «домом» для скрывающихся, вопреки романтической легенде девятнадцатого века, не были. В ослабленных формулировках, привязанных к реально задокументированным кампаниям гонений, все пять утверждений получают оценку от вероятного до хорошо подтверждённого; картину изменили бы новые документальные находки, например доимперский эдикт о повсеместном розыске христиан.

components.component: Early Christians were martyred for their faith under Roman persecution
components.rationale: Undisputed across the whole spectrum, from Tacitus (Ann. 15.44) and Pliny 10.96 through the martyr acta to Moss herself, who denies the scale narrative, not the fact of martyrdoms.

components.component: The martyrs numbered 'great numbers'
components.rationale: The primary spread runs from Origen c. 248 ('individuals who can be easily numbered', Contra Celsum 3.8, verified verbatim; written before the Decian, Valerianic, and Diocletianic persecutions, so it caps only the pre-250 period) to Eusebius's eyewitness Thebaid account (HE 8.9.3: ten to a hundred a day 'for a long series of years'). Modern estimates: Frend 3,000-3,500 for the Great Persecution alone; Shin over 23,500 under Diocletian; only Stark goes below 1,000, and T. D. Barnes called that figure impossibly low. Per charter 5.4 this is weighed, not head-counted: the specialists who worked through the documentary base (Frend, Shin, Barnes) converge on totals of thousands and more, which satisfies a natural reading of 'great numbers'; the maximalist legendary picture is simultaneously rejected, which keeps this at probable rather than well-supported.

rulings.weakened_version: Thousands of Christians were executed in the Roman persecutions, above all under Decius, Valerian, and Diocletian, though far fewer than later legend claimed.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest published minority against 'great numbers' is the Stark-Moss line: Stark (The Rise of Christianity) put total martyrs under 1,000, and Moss (The Myth of Persecution, 2013) argues the 'Age of Martyrs' was constructed retrospectively, largely by Eusebius. The argument's real force is against continuity and intentionality, not against the aggregate: Moss concedes genuine persecution in 250-260 and 303-313, and Stark's sub-1,000 figure was rejected as 'impossibly low' by T. D. Barnes, a leading specialist on the tetrarchic period, while Moss's scale claims drew academic pushback from Paul L. Maier and others. Origen's 'easily numbered' predates the three biggest persecutions and cannot govern them. The minority keeps the upper bound honest but does not defeat a loose 'great numbers'. No Phase-1 defense entry existed for this claim (disputed skipped the defense stage).

sources.ref: Origen, Contra Celsum 3.8 (ANF IV text via newadvent.org, wording verified by fetch)
sources.independence_note: Pre-Decian primary witness, independent of Eusebius's apologetic tradition.

sources.ref: Eusebius, HE 8.9.3, local scholarship/npnf2-01-eusebius-church-history-life-constantine.txt line 42083 (eyewitness Thebaid executions)
sources.independence_note: Eyewitness stratum of Eusebius, read in the annotated NPNF edition; apologetic aims noted by the edition itself.

sources.ref: W. H. C. Frend, Martyrdom and Persecution in the Early Church (estimate 3,000-3,500 for the Great Persecution; transmitted via encyclopedic digest)
sources.independence_note: Independent of the Shin and Stark estimates; classic maximalist-leaning specialist.

sources.ref: Min Seok Shin, The Great Persecution: A Historical Re-examination (Brepols), via BMCR 2020.08.21 review (23,500+ under Diocletian)
sources.independence_note: Recent monograph, methodologically independent of Frend; known via independent academic review.

sources.ref: T. D. Barnes contra Stark ('impossibly low'), transmitted via roger-pearse.com
sources.independence_note: Academic author, blog transmission; genealogically independent of Frend/Shin.

sources.ref: Candida Moss, The Myth of Persecution (2013) + critics Maier (CRI review) and Heiser
sources.independence_note: Moss builds on Ste. Croix's sporadic-persecution picture; counted in that genealogy for mechanics, separate for the scale polemic.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed prosopographical census of documented martyrdoms that pushed defensible totals decisively below about a thousand would move the as-written ruling to genuinely-open; new documentary finds of unrecorded mass executions would push it to well-supported.
rulings.notes: Direction: moved toward the claim relative to Phase-1 'disputed'.

components.component: Christians buried their dead in the Roman catacombs
components.rationale: Undisputed consensus of catacomb archaeology; the catacombs were communal Christian cemeteries.

components.component: When suspected and proscribed, Christians 'found a home' (refuge, dwelling) in the catacombs
components.rationale: Specialist archaeology (Fiocchi Nicolai; Rutgers) holds the catacombs were legally registered cemeteries whose locations the authorities knew, without ventilation, water, or habitation traces; Rutgers: researchers have long debunked the hiding-place myth. The chapter's own context confirms the dwelling reading ('making their home in the dark but friendly bosom of the earth', paragraph 5), which is what fails. The documented kernel is narrower: clandestine assembly during proscription (Valerian's 257 ban on entering the coemeteria; Sixtus II seized by soldiers during worship in a catacomb, 258) and infrequent short-term concealment conceded even by debunking scholarship. Sixtus's arrest cuts against the refuge picture: the authorities walked in and took him.

rulings.weakened_version: Christians buried their dead in the catacombs and, during acute persecution, also gathered there clandestinely for worship and occasionally for short-term concealment.
rulings.minority_engagement: The Phase-1 defender (verdict_downgraded to disputed) argued that 'found a home' is conditioned on episodes of proscription and that Valerian's 257 edict, by banning entry to the burial places, presupposes Christians gathered there during proscription; it also cited the SBTS journal concession that catacombs were 'only infrequently places of hiding during persecution'. Answer: incorporated for the weakened version, which is why it earns probable; but the edict and Sixtus II's arrest document assembly under ban, not dwelling, and the arrest itself shows the galleries gave no protection from the authorities. The defender's own text conceded that the strong 'home' reading 'remains rejected by consensus'. Against the as-written dwelling assertion, two genealogically independent specialist lines (Fiocchi Nicolai, Pontifical Institute of Christian Archaeology; Rutgers, Utrecht) converge with the legal registration of cemeteries; the as-written ruling is therefore improbable, not genuinely-open.

sources.ref: Vincenzo Fiocchi Nicolai (rector, Pontifical Institute of Christian Archaeology), quoted rejecting refuge claims (web digest of specialist interviews)
sources.independence_note: Catholic institutional archaeology; independent of Protestant and secular lines.

sources.ref: Leonard V. Rutgers on the debunked hiding-place myth (web digest; cf. his Subterranean Rome)
sources.independence_note: Utrecht-based specialist, genealogically independent of Fiocchi Nicolai.

sources.ref: 'Christian Refugees in Catacombs of Rome: Fact or Fiction' (academia.edu paper, used by Phase-1)
sources.independence_note: Same conclusion as the two specialist lines; not independently peer-validated, weighed as supporting position.

sources.ref: Cyprian, Ep. 80 + Valerian's 257 rescript (Sixtus II arrested in a catacomb, 6 August 258; via New Advent Catholic Encyclopedia transmission)
sources.independence_note: Documentary anchor for the assembly-under-proscription kernel; independent of the archaeological argument.

sources.ref: 'The Maltreatment of Early Christians: Refinement and Response' (SBTS journal; concedes infrequent hiding)
sources.independence_note: Confessional academic venue; cited as the defender's strongest concession source.

rulings.would_change_this: Archaeological evidence of habitation in pre-Constantinian catacomb levels (hearths, domestic refuse, water storage) or a contemporary documentary attestation of sustained hiding would raise the refuge clause toward probable.
rulings.notes: Original Phase-1 verdict anachronistic; period_standard candidate: the refuge legend was standard in 19th-century historiography and devotional literature (Wylie-type sources), as Phase-1 documented.

components.component: 'Paganism' acted as a unified agent waging a coordinated defensive war on Christianity across the era
components.rationale: Greco-Roman paganism had no central agency or coordinating institution; the consensus from Ste. Croix (1963) to Corke-Webster (Past & Present, 2023) locates persecution in local accusers, governors, and, only from 250, imperial edicts. The personified single-agent picture is a 19th-century providentialist frame.

components.component: Pagan forces were mobilized to destroy Christianity (as the origin of the persecutions from Nero onward)
components.rationale: True only for the late empire-wide campaigns: Valerian 257-258 and above all the Diocletianic edicts of 303-304 aiming at eradication (churches razed, scriptures burned, all clergy imprisoned; Eusebius HE 8.2/8.6, local text). For the first two centuries, which this sentence introduces (paragraph 3: 'beginning under Nero'), persecution was sporadic, reactive, and locally triggered; Trajan's rescript forbade seeking Christians out. As an account of the persecutions' origin the mobilization picture fails; as an account of 250-313 it succeeds.

components.component: The persecutors' motive was foresight that the gospel's triumph would sweep away temples and altars
components.rationale: Charter 3 motive rule: the rival mainstream account of motive has direct documentary support (Tacitus on Nero's scapegoating; Pliny-Trajan on punishing contumacia and rejection of the gods; the accusatory process itself), while 'foresight of Christian triumph' as the operative cause of the persecutions overall is undocumented, so the cap is improbable. Partial documentary support exists for a cult-defense motive in specific settings: Pliny 10.96 notes temples 'almost deserted' and a collapsed market for sacrificial victims; Galerius's 311 edict states the aim of returning Christians to ancestral religion; Maximinus Daia's rescripts answer civic petitions to expel Christians for the gods' favor; Porphyry and Hierocles supplied the ideology. These lift the weakened version, not the as-written universal cause.

rulings.weakened_version: Pagan authorities and elites, who saw Christianity's spread as a threat to the traditional cults, at times mounted deliberate campaigns to destroy it, most systematically under Valerian and in the Great Persecution.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case for the claim is the anti-minimalist line (Frend; Shin; the critics of Moss) plus the documentary record of pagan ideological mobilization in 250-313: Porphyry's Against the Christians, Hierocles, Maximinus Daia's pagan-church project and rescripts, Diocletian's restorationism, and Pliny's temple-desertion remark as early evidence that Christian growth measurably hurt cult economics. This is engaged and partly incorporated: it documents real, motivated anti-Christian campaigns and carries the weakened version to probable. It does not restore the as-written claim, because Frend himself, with the rest of the field, holds the first two centuries sporadic and local, and no document attributes the persecutions as a whole to foresight of Christianity's triumph. No Phase-1 defense entry existed for this claim.

sources.ref: G. E. M. de Ste. Croix, 'Why Were the Early Christians Persecuted?', Past & Present 26 (1963); repr. OUP 2006
sources.independence_note: Foundational statement of the sporadic-local consensus; Moss's mechanics descend from it (one genealogical line).

sources.ref: James Corke-Webster, 'By Whom Were Early Christians Persecuted?', Past & Present 261 (2023), open access
sources.independence_note: Recent, methodologically distinct reinforcement of local-agency mechanics.

sources.ref: Pliny, Ep. 10.96-97, local scholarship/pliny-letters-melmoth-gutenberg.txt lines 8838-8906
sources.independence_note: Primary documentary base for both the reactive procedure and the temple-desertion remark; independent of modern syntheses.

sources.ref: Eusebius HE 8.2/8.6 (Diocletianic edicts) and HE 9.7 context (Maximinus's Tyre rescript), local NPNF text + Judaism-and-Rome / encyclopedic digests of Maximinus's rescripts
sources.independence_note: Documentary anchors for the late cult-defense motive; transmitted through Christian apologetic historiography, noted.

sources.ref: Diocletianic Persecution and Maximinus Daza digests (Wikipedia) for Porphyry/Hierocles and the petitions
sources.independence_note: Digest of academic literature (Barnes, Mitchell); used for orientation, not as arbiter.

rulings.would_change_this: A documented pre-Decian imperial or provincial program ordering suppression of Christianity as such (an edict, senatus consultum, or governor's mandate) would raise the fact and frame components; a document attributing persecution to anticipated Christian victory would raise the motive component.
rulings.notes: Tie at the claim minimum across fact, frame, and motive; fixed priority fact > frame > motive applied per charter 3. The motive component is the rhetorical heart of the sentence; tied_kinds preserves this for impact re-scoring.

components.component: During active persecutions, Christian fugitives were at times hunted down in their refuges
components.rationale: Documented across the era's persecution episodes: Polycarp's pursuers 'pushed the search with vigor' and tortured a servant to learn his hiding place, c. 155 (Eusebius HE 4.15.11, local text); Dionysius of Alexandria hunted by a frumentarius who searched 'roads, rivers, and fields' under Decius (HE 6.40, local text); the second Diocletianic edict ordered 'all the rulers of the churches in every place' seized (HE 8.2/8.6, local text); Eusebius's Martyrs of Palestine records searches and arrests.

components.component: This happened 'wherever' they sought refuge, era-wide and without remission
components.rationale: Defeated by decisive, undisputed primary evidence and multiple independent counterexamples. Trajan's rescript (Ep. 10.97, local text: 'Do not go out of your way to look for them'; anonymous accusations inadmissible) made non-pursuit official policy for the pre-Decian era, reaffirmed by Hadrian's rescript to Minucius Fundanus (HE 4.9). Refuge frequently succeeded even during the fiercest campaigns: Cyprian directed his church by letter from hiding through the Decian persecution; Dionysius, once rescued, lived unmolested in the Libyan desert; flight was so routinely viable that Tertullian wrote De Fuga in Persecutione to argue Christians should not use it. The Long Peace of 260-303 adds four decades with no hunting at all. Per charter 6.3, multiple independent counterexamples plus one decisive one (Trajan) yield discredited for the universal.

rulings.weakened_version: During active persecutions, Christian fugitives were at times relentlessly pursued: searched for, betrayed, and seized even in hiding.
rulings.minority_engagement: No modern scholar defends universal, continuous pursuit. The strongest available opposing position is the anti-minimalist camp (Maier and others contra Moss; Shin on the Great Persecution's severity; Barnes contra Stark), which rightly insists that active hunting did occur in the Decian, Valerianic, and Diocletianic campaigns and even in local pre-Decian episodes like Polycarp's. That case is fully incorporated in the first component and the weakened ruling. It cannot save 'wherever': every scholar in that camp accepts Trajan's rescript and the sporadic pattern as the pre-250 framework. The continuous-manhunt picture is the Foxe/Wylie martyrological tradition that White's era treated as standard; it is superseded on this specific point. No Phase-1 defense entry existed for this claim.

sources.ref: Pliny-Trajan correspondence, Ep. 10.96-97, local scholarship/pliny-letters-melmoth-gutenberg.txt lines 8896-8906
sources.independence_note: Decisive primary counter-evidence; wholly independent of Christian transmission.

sources.ref: Eusebius HE 4.15 (Polycarp), 6.40 (Dionysius/frumentarius), 8.2/8.6 (Diocletianic edicts), local NPNF annotated text
sources.independence_note: Christian transmission, apologetic aims flagged by the edition; supplies both the hunting episodes and the successful-evasion counterexamples.

sources.ref: G. E. M. de Ste. Croix, 'Why Were the Early Christians Persecuted?' (Past & Present 1963 / OUP 2006)
sources.independence_note: Carries the sporadic-reactive consensus; genealogical parent of the modern mechanics literature.

sources.ref: James Corke-Webster, 'By Whom Were Early Christians Persecuted?', Past & Present 261 (2023)
sources.independence_note: Independent recent confirmation that pursuit was episodic and locally driven.

sources.ref: Cyprian's Decian-era concealment (his own epistolary corpus; textbook-level datum)
sources.independence_note: North African documentary line, independent of Eusebius and of Pliny.

rulings.would_change_this: Discovery of pre-Decian imperial orders mandating empire-wide searches for Christians, or documentary evidence that the Long Peace involved covert systematic pursuit, would overturn the counterexample structure; nothing realistic restores the universal 'wherever'.
rulings.notes: Largest framing gap in the dossier (4): the rhetoric fails while the weakened substance is fully documented, partly from White's own era-accessible sources. Period_standard candidate for synthesis tagging.

components.component: A doctrine of religious freedom existed with identifiable upholders in the era described
components.rationale: The pure-anachronism objection (Phase-1 verifier) is weakened by Wilken (Liberty in the Things of God, Yale UP 2019): Tertullian coined libertas religionis (Apol. 24; Ad Scapulam 2) and Lactantius argued religion cannot be coerced, arguments that fed the 313 Milan settlement. The concept is ancient, though after establishment it survived mainly in dissenters' situational pleas rather than as a standing doctrine with a party of 'upholders'.

components.component: That doctrine was itself 'termed heresy' and its upholders 'hated and proscribed' in this period
components.rationale: No documented late-antique or early-medieval instance labels toleration doctrine 'heresy'. Its known ancient advocates were not condemned for it: Tertullian's later heterodoxy was Montanist, and Lactantius tutored Constantine's son. The documented mechanism inverts the claim: the Edict of Thessalonica (380) and Codex Theodosianus 16.5 proscribed some 65 named heresies with penalties up to death, criminalized dissenting assembly, and Augustine's anti-Donatist theory justified coercion against dissenters' appeals to freedom of conscience; those dissenters were proscribed for their doctrines and assemblies, their liberty pleas rejected but not anathematized as such. Formal condemnations of the toleration principle itself appear only centuries later (Exsurge Domine art. 33 in 1520; Mirari Vos 1832; Syllabus 1864), outside the era this chapter narrates.

rulings.weakened_version: Under the established imperial church, those who defended freedom of worship for dissenters met hostility, and dissent itself was branded heresy and legally proscribed.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest defense of the sentence combines Wilken (religious liberty is a genuinely ancient Christian doctrine, so no anachronism) with the observation that the Roman church did eventually condemn precisely this doctrine in formal documents: Exsurge Domine (1520) condemned the proposition that burning heretics is against the will of the Spirit, and Mirari Vos (1832) called liberty of conscience a 'deliramentum'. Answer: the first half is conceded and raises the frame component to probable; the second half describes events 700 to 1300 years after the era paragraph 17 narrates (the pre-separation apostasy of the early church), and within that era the documented pattern is proscription of dissent as heresy, not condemnation of toleration doctrine as heresy. The compressed long-run reading is exactly what the weakened version captures, hence its well-supported ruling. No Phase-1 defense entry existed for this claim; the Phase-1 verifier's anachronism rationale is partly answered by Wilken and partly sustained.

sources.ref: Robert Louis Wilken, Liberty in the Things of God: The Christian Origins of Religious Freedom (Yale UP 2019), via Reading Religion / Themelios / Law&Liberty reviews
sources.independence_note: University-press monograph known through multiple independent reviews; revisionist against the Enlightenment-origins account.

evidence_review: The dossier's question: can the sentence 'The mangled forms of millions of martyrs cried to God for vengeance upon that apostate power' (GC ch. 3 par. 31) stand as a quantitative assertion that millions of persons were killed as religious martyrs under papal-sanctioned persecution? (1) GENEALOGY OF THE FIGURE. The sentence sits in a paragraph built on Revelation 17:6 ('Babylon the great... drunken with the blood of the saints'), and its 19th-century source tradition is locally documented: John Dowling, History of Romanism (1845), bk VIII ch. 1 sec. 1 (printed pp. 541-542; local file lines 32180-32217) opens with the same Rev 17:6 quotation, asserts that 'more than Fifty Millions of the human family have been slaughtered for the crime of heresy by popish persecutors' since 606, and describes popery reigning as 'Despot of the World' -- language GC par. 32 echoes ('Popery had become the world's despot'). Dowling's supporting footnote quotes Scott's Church History: 'A million of poor Waldenses perished in France; nine hundred thousand orthodox Christians were slain in less than thirty years after the institution of the order of the Jesuits... The Inquisition destroyed... one hundred and fifty thousand within thirty years' (local lines 32250-32260). None of these component figures has archival basis; the Waldensian population never approached one million. White's 'millions' is thus period-standard martyrology arithmetic -- and restrained relative to her tradition's 50 million. (2) THE ARCHIVAL RECORD OF JUDICIAL KILLING. Modern scholarship rebuilt the numbers from trial records. Spain: Contreras & Henningsen's relaciones de causas database (1986) gives 44,674 trials 1540-1700 with 1,604 death sentences, 826 executed in persona; the full-history range runs from Monter/Kamen (~2,750-3,000 executions) through Garcia Carcel (~4,200-5,250) and Dedieu/Perez (<10,000) to Karlikowski's synthesis of ~7,300 in persona 1480-1820 (local Ereticopedia excerpts). Lea's 'Statistics of Victims' chapter (History of the Inquisition of Spain, vol. 4, 1907, p. 617) had already rejected Llorente's extrapolation method a century before. Portugal: ~1,200-2,000 executed in persona 1536-1821 (records show 1,808 in persona plus 633 in effigy). Italy: Andrea Del Col estimates ~1,250 death sentences out of 51,000-75,000 cases after 1542. Medieval inquisition: Bernard Gui's complete Toulouse register shows ~42-45 executions out of 636 culpae (~7%), and the documented medieval inquisitorial total is low thousands across three centuries. William Monter's corpus study ('Heresy executions in Reformation Europe, 1520-1565', in Grell & Scribner eds., Tolerance and Intolerance in the European Reformation, CUP 1996) puts ALL legally sanctioned heresy executions in Latin Christendom during the worst 45 years at ~3,000, Protestant-run tribunals included. Marian England: ~284 burned. Judicial religious executions under papally sanctioned authority across the whole 13th-18th century span therefore total roughly 15,000-25,000. (3) MASSACRES AND CRUSADE MORTALITY. St. Bartholomew's Day 1572: modern estimates 5,000-25,000 (Paris ~2,000-4,000, provinces ~3,000-5,000; the Huguenot figure of 70,000 is polemical). Merindol/Cabrieres 1545: hundreds to a few thousand Waldensians; Piedmont Easter 1655: ~1,700. Albigensian Crusade 1209-1229: mortality is warfare and genuinely unquantifiable; the famous 20,000 at Beziers comes from the legates' letter and likely exceeds the town's population; Laurence Marvin (The Occitan War, CUP 2008) concludes the war 'does not stand out as particularly barbarous' by medieval standards; Mark Gregory Pegg (Cambridge World History of Genocide vol. 1, ch. 18, 2023) argues the crusade was genocidal in character but supplies no million-scale death toll; the 200,000-1,000,000 range circulating in encyclopedic and popular sources has no archival basis and no current specialist defender. (4) AGGREGATION. In the claim's own terms -- Christians killed as heretics/martyrs under papally sanctioned persecution, the ones who 'cried for vengeance upon that apostate power' -- an honest maximalist tally (all judicial executions + all religious massacres + crusade civilian mass killings at generous values) lands in the low-to-mid hundreds of thousands at the outermost, one full order of magnitude short of 'millions' (>=2,000,000). The only routes to 'millions' are (a) the discredited polemical figures (1M Waldenses, 50M total) or (b) counting general war mortality of the confessional wars (French Wars of Religion 2-4M dead including famine and disease on both sides; Thirty Years' War 4.5-8M), which are combat, famine, and epidemic deaths of both confessions, not martyrdoms inflicted by the papacy. (5) DEFENSES OF HIGH COUNTS. Besides the 19th-century tradition, the only modern defenses located are counter-apologetic compilations (e.g., David Plaisted, 'Estimates of the Number Killed by the Papacy in the Middle Ages and Later', self-published web essay) that recycle the same 19th-century compendia; no academic historian defends 'millions of martyrs'.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяло фразу третьей главы о «миллионах мучеников», взывающих об отмщении к отступнической власти. Архивные подсчёты казней за ересь дают другой порядок величины: трибуналы инквизиции в Испании, Португалии и Италии казнили в сумме порядка десяти или пятнадцати тысяч человек за все века работы, а все казни за ересь в Европе 1520–1565 годов Уильям Монтер оценивает примерно в три тысячи. Даже с добавлением Варфоломеевской ночи, погромов вальденсов и жертв Альбигойского похода честный итог остаётся в пределах сотен тысяч и до «миллионов» не дотягивает минимум на порядок. Формулировка как написано получила вердикт improbable, ослабленная версия о десятках тысяч казнённых при папской санкции хорошо подтверждена. Сама цифра восходит к протестантской мартирологии девятнадцатого века: Джон Даулинг в 1845 году писал о пятидесяти миллионах, на этом фоне слово «миллионы» звучало даже сдержанно. Картину изменило бы рецензируемое демографическое исследование, документирующее около двух миллионов погибших именно за веру, а не в религиозных войнах в целом.

components.component: The cumulative number of persons put to death as religious martyrs under papal-sanctioned persecution reaches into the millions (>=2,000,000).
components.rationale: Documented judicial religious executions under papally sanctioned authority across the 13th-18th centuries total ~15,000-25,000 (Contreras-Henningsen database for Spain; Del Col for Italy; Portuguese tribunal records; Gui's register and the medieval record; Monter's ~3,000 for all Latin Christendom 1520-1565). Adding religious massacres (St. Bartholomew 5,000-25,000; Waldensian massacres in the thousands) and Albigensian civilian mass killings at generous values, an honest martyr-count stays in the low-to-mid hundreds of thousands -- a full order of magnitude below 'millions'. The two routes to 'millions' both fail: the polemical component figures (1M Waldenses via Scott's Church History, quoted by Dowling; 50M total) have no archival basis and no current specialist defender, and general war mortality of the confessional wars (2-4M in France, 4.5-8M in the Thirty Years' War) consists of combat, famine, and epidemic deaths of both confessions, not martyrdoms 'under that apostate power'. Per SS6 the population-level burden lies on the quantifier and the documented order-of-magnitude gap grounds an accusatory ruling; the ruling stops at improbable rather than discredited because cumulative persecution mortality over a millennium admits no single decisive counter-document (massacre and crusade deaths are not archivally countable the way trial records are) and the weakened version survives strongly (SS1).

components.component: The papacy is 'that apostate power' -- the Babylon of Revelation 17 drunken with the blood of the saints.
components.rationale: Prophetic identification of a symbol (Babylon = papacy), out of scope per SS9. The fact-component ruling is conditional in the sense that 'under the apostate power' is read historically as 'under papal-sanctioned authority', without evaluating the prophetic identification itself.

components.component: The martyrs' mangled forms 'cried to God for vengeance' upon that power.
components.rationale: Theological-apocalyptic imagery (divine audition of martyr blood, echoing Rev 6:10); supernatural framing is out of scope per SS9 and receives no ruling.

rulings.weakened_version: Tens of thousands of religious dissenters were put to death under papally sanctioned persecution between the thirteenth and eighteenth centuries, and massacre-inclusive tallies plausibly reach into the hundreds of thousands.
rulings.minority_engagement: Three defenses engaged. (1) The Phase-1 defender (adversarial/gc_ch03.defended.json, verdict_downgraded to disputed): argued the verifier had only apologetic websites, that 'that apostate power' spans more than Inquisition tribunals, that the Albigensian Crusade alone reaches 'up to ~1 million' (citing the Cambridge World History of Genocide) and the Crusades broadly 1-3M, so a maximalist tally into low millions is 'not demographically impossible'; White wrote 'millions', not Dowling's 50M. Answer: the sourcing objection was correct and this adjudication replaces the apologetic citations with archival scholarship (Contreras-Henningsen, Monter, Lea, Del Col), which yields the same order of magnitude; the scope widening is accepted -- and still fails, because Pegg's Cambridge WHG chapter makes a qualitative genocide argument without endorsing a million-scale toll, specialist military history (Marvin, CUP 2008) finds the Occitan War not exceptionally lethal and the Beziers 20,000 inflated, and 'the Crusades broadly' counts deaths crusaders inflicted on Muslims, Jews, and Greeks plus crusader combat losses -- not martyrs crying for vengeance on Rome in the sentence's own terms. The defender's own concession ('much crusade/war death was of combatants and general population, not martyrs in the strict sense... even the maximalist reading strains the word martyrs') marks exactly where the defense ends: it justified reopening the verdict, not sustaining 'millions'. (2) The 19th-century high-count tradition (Dowling 1845, quoting Scott's Church History): its component figures (1M Waldenses; 900,000 in thirty years; Inquisition 150,000 in thirty years) are pinned verbatim in the local file and have been superseded by trial-record scholarship from Lea (1907) to Henningsen-Contreras (1986); no figure survives archival checking. (3) Modern high-count compilations (Plaisted's web essay and similar counter-apologetic pieces): these aggregate the same 19th-century compendia and are genealogically dependent on them (SS4.4), engage no trial archives, and are citable as positions only. Confessional location plays no role in any of these dismissals; each fails on provenance and superseded data.

sources.independence_note: Encyclopedic digests of the specialist literature (Holt, Diefendorf); used for the massacre-vs-war-mortality distinction, not for any load-bearing figure.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed demographic or archival study documenting persecution deaths in the claim's own sense -- judicial executions plus religious massacres, excluding general war mortality -- at or above ~2 million under papally sanctioned authority; concretely, either archival evidence multiplying the known tribunal execution counts by two orders of magnitude, or a specialist consensus establishing Albigensian-era civilian mass-killing mortality near one million. Either would move the as-written ruling toward genuinely-open or better.
rulings.notes: period_standard candidate for synthesis tagging: the sentence matches (and understates) the martyrology arithmetic of White's period sources -- Dowling's 50 million tradition -- which is why the Phase-1 verifier ruled it anachronistic before the defense downgraded to disputed. The sentence's register is apocalyptic-rhetorical (a paragraph about Satan in Inquisition councils and Babylon of Rev 17), not statistical; the extractor's separation of the numeric component is nonetheless correct, and the dual ruling carries the genre point: the rhetoric fails as arithmetic while the weakened historical substance (large-scale papally sanctioned religious killing) is well-supported.

evidence_review: The dossier turns on one number: how many followers John Wesley had at his death on 2 March 1791. The documentary base is unusually good, because Methodism counted itself. The Conference Minutes for 1791 record 294 preachers and 71,668 society members in Great Britain, 19 missionaries and 5,300 members on mission stations, and 198 preachers with 43,265 members in America — roughly 120,000 enrolled members, or ~135,000–150,000 on the commonly cited totals that add Ireland and use fuller American returns (the '135,000 members and 541 itinerant preachers' summary reproduced across standard histories). Britannica's rounding is 79,000 in England and 40,000 in America. Modern biographical scholarship works from these rolls: Richard Heitzenrater (Duke; Wesley and the People Called Methodists) puts the movement at Wesley's death at 'nearly 80,000 adherents' in Britain, and Henry Rack (Reasonable Enthusiast) uses the same Minutes series. Membership, however, was a strict category — class tickets, quarterly renewal — and every side of the literature agrees the penumbra of regular hearers was much larger. The scholarly attempts to size that penumbra are estimates, not counts: Clive Field's peer-reviewed reconstruction (Journal of Ecclesiastical History 63, 2012, 'Counting Religion in England and Wales: The Long Eighteenth Century') puts 'Arminian Methodist' affiliation at 3.2% of the England-and-Wales population in 1800 (~285,000 people) — nine years and substantial growth after Wesley's death, implying a British affiliation community of roughly 200,000–230,000 in 1791; adding Ireland and America on comparable ratios yields a transatlantic constituency plausibly around 350,000–450,000. The Currie/Gilbert/Horsley tradition (Churches and Churchgoers, 1977) and American scholarship use members-to-community multipliers of about 3, sometimes stretched to 4–5 for hearers; only at the extreme of that band (135,000–150,000 × 4–5) does the figure clear 500,000. The half-million-at-Wesley's-death claim itself lives in 19th-century homiletic historiography (the Victorian hearers-multiplier tradition; cf. the same tradition's 'by 1820 ... total under direct Methodist influence not far below one million'), not in modern scholarship: no current specialist defends ≥500,000 for 1791. The decisive semantic point is White's own adjective 'avowed': the only historically documented form of avowed adherence — society enrollment — totaled ~120,000–150,000; unenrolled hearers, the population the multiplier counts, were precisely those who had not avowed adherence. What changed since the 19th century: denominational eulogy figures gave way to critical use of the Minutes series and to population-share estimation (Field), which caps even the loose reading below the book's threshold for 1791.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет одну цифру из главы 14: к концу жизни Уэсли его «открытых приверженцев» насчитывалось более полумиллиона. Документированные списки методистских обществ на 1791 год дают около 120–150 тысяч членов в Британии и Америке, а академические оценки более широкого круга регулярных слушателей (Филд, стандартные биографии Рэка и Хайценрейтера) выводят на 350–450 тысяч. Полмиллиона достигается только при самом растянутом множителе и при подмене «открыто заявивших о себе» на неоформленных слушателей, поэтому формулировка книги получила вердикт «маловероятно». Ослабленная версия, где приверженцы вместе со слушателями исчисляются сотнями тысяч, признана вероятной; сама цифра при этом повторяет ходовое утверждение методистской литературы девятнадцатого века. Картину изменил бы рецензируемый подсчёт или современный событиям документ, фиксирующий не менее полумиллиона заявленных методистов на 1791 год.

quantifier_audit: more than half a million souls (numeric lower bound asserted as fact)
avowed (scope-fixing qualifier: openly professed adherence, which raises the burden — it excludes the unenrolled hearers on whom any half-million reading depends)

components.component: Wesley's life lasted more than fourscore (80) years
components.rationale: [common_knowledge] Born June 1703, died 2 March 1791, aged 87. Textbook-level; safe-direction fast-path.

components.component: Above half a century of that life was spent in itinerant ministry
components.rationale: [common_knowledge] Wesley's field/itinerant ministry ran from 1739 to 1791 — about 52 years. Standard in every biography (Rack, Heitzenrater); safe-direction fast-path.

components.component: At his death Wesley's avowed adherents numbered more than half a million
components.rationale: Documented avowed adherence — Methodist society membership, the only form of open profession the sources record — totaled roughly 120,000–150,000 transatlantic per the 1791 Conference Minutes (71,668 GB + 5,300 missions + 43,265 America, plus Ireland). The half-million threshold is reachable only by counting unenrolled hearers, who by definition had not avowed adherence; and even for that loose community, the best academic estimate (Field, JEH 2012: Arminian Methodist affiliation 3.2% of England-and-Wales population in 1800 ≈ 285,000, after nine further years of growth) implies a 1791 transatlantic constituency of roughly 350,000–450,000. Only the extreme 4–5× multiplier band of the Victorian hearers tradition clears 500,000, and no modern specialist defends that figure for 1791. Clear preponderance against the sentence as printed; the multiplier defense is engaged below and fails on scope.

rulings.weakened_version: At the close of Wesley's long life his followers — about 135,000 enrolled society members in Britain and America, with the wider circle of adherents and regular hearers plausibly numbering several hundred thousand — formed one of the largest religious movements of the age.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case for the sentence as written is the adherents-not-members defense, anticipated by the Phase-1 verifier (no defender object exists for this claim: disputed skipped the defense stage) and rooted in 19th-century Methodist historiography: society membership was deliberately strict, the penumbra of regular hearers was several times larger, and Victorian writers (the Stevens-era eulogy tradition) used hearers-to-members multipliers of about five, under which 135,000–150,000 members yield 675,000+ 'adherents'. The argument is real and explains where the figure comes from, but it fails twice on evidence. First, on scope: White's sentence says 'avowed adherents', and the only documented act of avowal was society enrollment (~120,000–150,000); the multiplier population — unenrolled attendants — is precisely the set that had made no avowal, so the defense holds only by silently deleting the sentence's own qualifier. Second, on magnitude: the peer-reviewed estimate of the loose affiliation community (Field, JEH 2012: ~285,000 in England and Wales in 1800, nine growth-years after Wesley's death) back-projects to a 1791 transatlantic constituency of roughly 350,000–450,000, short of the stated threshold; the 4–5× band survives only in the Victorian homiletic tradition and in later-period American data, not in any current specialist's figure for 1791. The defense is confessionally located (denominational eulogy literature) but is dismissed here on scope and superseded data, not on confession.

sources.ref: Minutes of the Methodist Conferences, 1791 returns (294 preachers / 71,668 members GB; 19 missionaries / 5,300 mission stations; 198 preachers / 43,265 America), as reproduced in standard references incl. Wikipedia 'John Wesley' and Britannica 'John Wesley' (79,000 England + 40,000 America rounding)
sources.independence_note: Single documentary root (the connexional Minutes series) reproduced across encyclopedic and academic literature; counted once. It is the count Methodism made of itself, contemporaneous and against interest for a deflationary use.

sources.ref: C. D. Field, 'Counting Religion in England and Wales: The Long Eighteenth Century, c.1680–c.1840', Journal of Ecclesiastical History 63/4 (2012); summary tables via the author's brin.ac.uk page (Arminian Methodists = 3.2% of E+W population in 1800)

Heitzenrater (Duke), figure of 'nearly 80,000 adherents' in Britain at Wesley's death, reported from his 2016 Chautauqua lecture (chqdaily.com); standard biography: Wesley and the People Called Methodists (2nd ed. 2013); H. D. Rack, Reasonable Enthusiast (3rd ed. 2002) works from the same Minutes series
