sources.ref: Victorian hearers-multiplier tradition (Methodist eulogy historiography; cf. victorianweb.org citing the '1820: ~200,000 members, total under direct Methodist influence not far below one million' estimate) — the pedigree of the half-million figure GC reproduces
sources.independence_note: Cited as a position and as pedigree evidence only, never as arbiter; genealogically the likely upstream of White's sentence.

sources.ref: R. Currie, A. Gilbert, L. Horsley, Churches and Churchgoers: Patterns of Church Growth in the British Isles since 1700 (1977), members-vs-community framework (via brin.ac.uk and JEH citations)
sources.independence_note: Framework consulted via secondary description, not the volume itself; supports the legitimacy of a ~3× community multiplier, i.e. it partly helps the defense — noted for symmetry.

rulings.would_change_this: A contemporaneous document class actually enumerating professed Methodist followers beyond society rolls (for example, circuit-level returns of regular hearers from 1790–1791) totaling at or above 500,000 transatlantic, or a peer-reviewed quantitative study defending a ≥500,000 constituency of professed adherents at Wesley's death, would move the as-written ruling toward genuinely-open or probable. Conversely, a modern specialist study capping the 1791 community below ~250,000 would push the weakened ruling down.
rulings.notes: The first two components are decorative biography and pass on the safe-direction fast-path; the claim fails solely on the statistic. The figure matched the period-standard homiletic literature White drew on (exact upstream text not located within budget) — flag for period_standard: true in synthesis, bridging the Phase-1 anachronistic vocabulary.

evidence_review: STATE OF THE QUESTION: did a royal order extend the St. Bartholomew's Day massacre (24 August 1572) from Paris to the provinces? The foundational modern study is Philip Benedict, 'The Saint Bartholomew's Massacres in the Provinces', Historical Journal 21/2 (1978), still cited as the best treatment. Benedict's documented picture: massacres occurred in roughly twelve provincial cities (Meaux, La Charite, Bourges, Orleans, Saumur, Angers, Lyon, Troyes, Rouen, Bordeaux, Toulouse, Gaillac, with smaller incidents elsewhere), killing an estimated 3,000-5,000; they were driven by militant local Catholics and municipal factions acting amid rumor that killing was 'the king's will', not by a documented royal order. The surviving royal correspondence points the opposite way: Charles IX's letters of 24 and 28 August ordered governors to maintain the Peace of Saint-Germain and protect peaceable Protestants, and a circular of 30 August revoked whatever verbal instructions had circulated in his name. Early verbal orders to Lyon and Bordeaux are judged by the scholarship to have concerned arrests and security, not killing, and were countermanded. The 26 August lit de justice took royal responsibility only for the execution of Coligny and his alleged co-conspirators, not for a general massacre. The strongest documented court-level thread is a set of apparently genuine letters from the duke of Anjou (the king's brother) urging killings in the king's name; at Nantes the mayor withheld such a letter until the king's contrary orders arrived. Counterexamples to any universal extension are numerous and undisputed: the Huguenot strongholds La Rochelle, Nimes and Montauban were untouched; Nantes, Rennes and Aix protected their Protestants; several governors refused (the famous d'Orthez 'no hangman' reply at Bayonne belongs to this tradition, whatever its authenticity). Later scholarship converges on Benedict: Mack Holt (The French Wars of Religion, Cambridge UP) attributes the spread to hardline nobles misrepresenting the king's wishes; Arlette Jouanna (The Saint Bartholomew's Day Massacre: The Mysteries of a Crime of State, Manchester UP 2013) further exonerates Charles IX for the provincial 'copycat' massacres; Penny Roberts (Troyes) and Gautier Mingous (Lyon, French History 2020) document the local dynamics city by city. WHAT CHANGED SINCE THE 19TH CENTURY: the royal-extension-order reading was standard in White's source layer. Wylie, History of Protestantism (1870s), vol. 2 (local file, ~lines 61136-61144), reads 'In pursuance of orders sent from the court, they were extended to all provinces and cities where Protestants were found', citing the Jesuit Maimbourg (1682); Dowling, History of Romanism (1845; local file ~lines 34479-34485) has special messengers 'ordering a general massacre', though even Dowling limits the carnage to 'nearly all the provinces'. White's sentence tracks Wylie nearly verbatim (including 'seven days', 'two months' and Sully's 70,000 nearby). The premeditation school survived into the late 19th century (Henri Bordier, La Saint-Barthelemy et la critique moderne, 1879) against Ranke, Henri Martin, Henry White, Loiseleur, La Ferriere and Vacandard; by 1911 the consensus had turned against premeditation (1911 Britannica), and Benedict 1978 settled the provinces question specifically. No current specialist defends a royal order extending the massacre.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяло утверждение «Великой борьбы», что Варфоломеевская резня «по особому приказу короля» распространилась на все провинции и города, где жили протестанты. Сохранившиеся письма Карла IX от 24 и 28 августа 1572 года предписывали губернаторам хранить мир и защищать протестантов, а резня вспыхнула примерно в двенадцати городах усилиями местных католических активистов, поверивших слухам о воле короля (Бенедикт, Холт, Жуанна). Крупнейшие гугенотские города, включая Ла-Рошель, Ним и Монтобан, остались нетронутыми, и ряд губернаторов прямо отказался исполнять мнимый приказ. Формулировка книги отклонена как написано, поскольку документам противоречат и «особый приказ короля», и охват «всех провинций»; ослабленная версия, что резня перекинулась на провинции и многие исполнители считали себя орудием королевской воли, прочно подтверждена. Уайт почти дословно следует Уайли, передававшему стандартное для девятнадцатого века прочтение; картину изменил бы подлинный королевский приказ об истреблении протестантов в провинциях, если бы такой нашёлся в архивах.

quantifier_audit: all the provinces and towns where Protestants were found

components.component: The massacre was not confined to Paris but spread to provincial cities and towns in the following weeks
components.rationale: Undisputed: massacres in roughly twelve provincial cities between late August and early October 1572, with 3,000-5,000 provincial victims (Benedict 1978; Holt; Jouanna). Every modern account carries this.

components.component: The extension to the provinces occurred 'by special order of the king' (a royal order commanding the killing beyond Paris)
components.rationale: No such order exists in the documentary record. Charles IX's surviving letters of 24 and 28 August ordered governors to maintain the Peace of Saint-Germain and protect Protestants; the 30 August circular revoked verbal instructions circulating in his name; the early verbal orders to Lyon and Bordeaux are judged to have concerned arrests, not massacre, and were countermanded; the 26 August lit de justice claimed responsibility only for Coligny's execution as punishment of an alleged conspiracy. The provincial killings correlate with cities that had militant Catholic networks and prior confessional trauma, which fits local initiative under rumor of royal will, not a uniform command (Benedict 1978, the consensus study; Jouanna 2013; Holt). Not 'discredited': a documented court-level thread survives (apparently genuine letters of the duke of Anjou urging killings in the king's name; ambiguous early verbal signals), so the sentence is defeated by clear preponderance rather than by a single decisive refutation. This is a fact component (existence of an order), not a motive cap case; in any case the competing account rests on surviving royal letters, i.e. documents.

components.component: The massacre reached ALL the provinces and towns where Protestants were found
components.rationale: Multiple independent, undisputed counterexamples defeat the universal (charter section 6.3): the major Huguenot cities La Rochelle, Nimes and Montauban saw no massacre and became centers of resistance in the Fourth War; Nantes, Rennes and Aix protected their Protestants; several governors and municipalities refused to kill (a fact even Wylie records, with the d'Orthez reply). Benedict counts about a dozen affected cities against the many dozens of towns with Protestant communities. On the alternative reading that 'all' describes the scope of the alleged order rather than the outcome, the component collapses into the royal-order component, which itself fails; on either reading the sentence as printed does not survive.

rulings.weakened_version: The massacre was not confined to Paris: over the following weeks it spread to about a dozen provincial cities, with many perpetrators and some local authorities believing or claiming that they acted on the king's will.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case FOR a royal extension order combines: (1) the older premeditation school, most fully Henri Bordier (La Saint-Barthelemy et la critique moderne, 1879), building on contemporary Huguenot accounts, Capilupi's celebratory pamphlet, and perpetrators' claims of verbal orders; (2) White's own source layer, Wylie (1870s, citing the Jesuit Maimbourg, 1682) and Dowling (1845), which carried the royal-messenger reading as standard, and whose genealogy is notably not purely Protestant since Maimbourg was a Catholic historian; (3) the genuine residue moderns acknowledge: Charles IX's early verbal instructions to Lyon and Bordeaux, the 26 August lit de justice taking responsibility for Coligny's death, and the apparently genuine letters of the duke of Anjou urging killings in the king's name (the Nantes case). This case fails on evidence for the claim AS WRITTEN: the authenticated royal correspondence of 24 and 28 August commands protection of Protestants, the 30 August circular disavows verbal orders sent in the king's name, the verbal instructions are judged by Benedict and successors to have ordered arrests rather than killing, the Anjou letters are the brother's initiative rather than a 'special order of the king', and the claims of royal kill-orders come from perpetrators' ex post facto self-justifications and hostile pamphlets, not authenticated documents. The premeditation consensus had already collapsed by the late 19th century (Ranke, Henri Martin, Henry White, Loiseleur, La Ferriere, Vacandard; Lord Acton's final position), and Benedict 1978 settled the provinces question; no current specialist defends a royal extension order. The residue is fully incorporated in the weakened ruling. Phase-1 answer: no defense object exists (disputed skipped the defense stage); the verifier's rationale is consistent with this adjudication, which sharpens it by separating the decisively false universal quantifier from the preponderance-defeated royal-order component.

sources.ref: Philip Benedict, 'The Saint Bartholomew's Massacres in the Provinces', The Historical Journal 21/2 (1978), Cambridge Core (abstract and summaries engaged)
sources.independence_note: Foundational archival study of the provincial massacres; the upstream source of the modern consensus, examined directly rather than through digests.

sources.ref: Arlette Jouanna, The Saint Bartholomew's Day Massacre: The Mysteries of a Crime of State (Manchester UP, 2013; French orig. 2007), engaged via H-France Forum review PDF and IHR Reviews in History no. 1463
sources.independence_note: Independent archival synthesis post-Benedict; genealogically distinct examination of the royal correspondence and the lit de justice; further exonerates Charles IX for the provincial copycat massacres.

sources.ref: Mack P. Holt, The French Wars of Religion, 1562-1629 (Cambridge UP), position on Anjou and hardline nobles misrepresenting the king's wishes
sources.independence_note: Standard survey; downstream of Benedict on the provinces but an independent monographic treatment; source of the Anjou-letters point.

sources.ref: Wikipedia, 'St. Bartholomew's Day massacre in the provinces' (synthesizing Benedict, Holt, Knecht, Penny Roberts on Troyes, Mingous on Lyon)
sources.independence_note: Used as a map to the academic literature and for the city-by-city detail (protected cities, refusing governors); not an arbiter; genealogically downstream of Benedict.

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, vol. 2 (local file scholarship/wylie-history-of-protestantism-vol2.txt, ~lines 61136-61195), citing Maimbourg livr. vi
sources.independence_note: White's near-verbatim source for the sentence; establishes the period-standard genealogy (Wylie -> Maimbourg 1682), not the modern facts.

sources.ref: J. Dowling, The History of Romanism (1845; local file scholarship/dowling-history-of-romanism-1845.txt, ~lines 34479-34497)
sources.independence_note: Second, earlier period source with the royal-messenger reading ('nearly all the provinces'); independent of Wylie but drawing on the same 17th-century compilations (Maimbourg, Mezeray).

sources.ref: 1911 Encyclopaedia Britannica, 'St Bartholomew, Massacre of' (Wikisource), on the Bordier vs Ranke/Martin/Loiseleur/Vacandard premeditation debate
sources.independence_note: Documents the historiographical state at the time of GC 1911: the premeditation consensus had already turned against the royal-order reading.

rulings.would_change_this: An authenticated document from Charles IX or his council, datable to late August 1572, ordering the killing of Protestants in the provinces (as opposed to the surviving letters ordering their protection) would reverse the royal-order component; documented massacres in the major Huguenot towns (La Rochelle, Nimes, Montauban) or in most Protestant-populated towns would rehabilitate the universal extent. Conversely, a peer-reviewed demonstration that the Anjou letters are forgeries would push the royal-order component toward discredited.
rulings.notes: period_standard: true (candidate for synthesis tagging) - the sentence tracks Wylie nearly verbatim ('extended to all provinces and cities where Protestants were found', 'orders sent from the court'), and the royal-order reading was standard across White's period sources, Protestant (Wylie, Dowling) and Catholic (Maimbourg) alike. All three components are fact-kind, so no cross-kind tie arises at the minimum; the minimum is set solely by the universal-extent component.

evidence_review: State of the question on the Massacre of the Innocents (Matthew 2:16-18). ATTESTATION: the event is reported only in Matthew; Luke's infancy narrative, Mark, John, and Paul know nothing of it. The two ancient texts sometimes offered as corroboration fail the genealogical-independence test: Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho 78 (c. 160, read locally in ANF vol. 1) retells the Matthean story with the same Jeremiah 31:15 prooftext and is dependent on Matthean tradition; Macrobius, Saturnalia 2.4.11 (c. 430) has Augustus quip that it is 'better to be Herod's pig than his son' apropos of boys under two killed 'in Syria' together with Herod's own son, a late, garbled notice that scholars since Lardner have read as a conflation of the gospel story with Herod's execution of his son Antipater (4 BC), plausibly contaminated by Christian tradition; it is discounted as independent evidence. THE CRITICAL MAJORITY: Raymond Brown, The Birth of the Messiah (1977, rev. 1993, Anchor Bible Reference Library), the standard treatment, judges the episode unverifiable and likely non-historical in its present form: Josephus, who catalogs Herod's atrocities in detail (the executions of Mariamne, of Alexander and Aristobulus in 7 BC, of Antipater in 4 BC, the hippodrome order at his death), is silent, and Brown argues Josephus's demonstrated appetite for paranoia-revealing atrocities makes the silence weighty; the narrative closely tracks the first-century haggadic expansion of the Moses/Pharaoh infanticide story (Pharaoh forewarned of a liberator's birth, cf. Josephus Ant. 2.205-209), with Matthew casting Herod as Pharaoh and Jesus as the new Moses, capped by a fulfillment citation of Jeremiah 31:15. Ulrich Luz (Hermeneia) reads Matthew 2 as haggadic rather than historical reportage; most recent academic biographies of Herod deny the event (a concession made in print by defender Paul Maier in 1998); Vermes, Sanders, and Ehrman state the legendary reading as the mainstream one. THE DEFENSE: R. T. France, 'Herod and the Children of Bethlehem,' Novum Testamentum 21 (1979), the strongest peer-reviewed defense, argues that Bethlehem's population (roughly 1,000) implies perhaps ten to twenty boys under two, an atrocity of a scale below the notice threshold of surviving sources; that the argument from silence is methodologically weak given Josephus's selectivity; and that the deed fits Herod's documented final-years paranoia. Paul L. Maier (ancient historian, Western Michigan University; 'Herod and the Infants of Bethlehem,' in Chronos, Kairos, Christos II, 1998) adds that Josephus wrote for a Greco-Roman audience largely indifferent to infanticide. Everett Ferguson and Craig Keener endorse the small-scale point; David Instone-Brewer has further argued that Assumption of Moses 6.2-6 ('he shall slay the old and the young') may allude to indiscriminate killings by Herod, an argument that has not won acceptance because the passage is a vague merism for Herod's general cruelty. Even skeptics concede the deed is consistent in kind with Herod's documented record (Brown grants this explicitly). WHAT CHANGED SINCE THE 19TH CENTURY: White's milieu treated the episode as straightforward history via gospel harmonies; redaction and genre criticism of the infancy narratives (culminating in Brown) moved the mainstream to 'legendary or unverifiable,' while a peer-reviewed minority (France 1979; Maier 1998) keeps plausibility alive; recent academic work is largely reception-historical (Sung J. Cho, Matthew's Account of the Massacre of the Innocents in Light of Its Reception History, T&T Clark 2021). NET: both sides argue from the same tiny evidence base (Matthew 2, Josephus's corpus, Bethlehem demography, the Moses haggadah); neither positive confirmation nor positive disproof exists or is likely to; the disagreement is over the relative weight of Josephus's silence versus character-consistency, and it does not reduce cleanly to confessional alignment (Brown, a Catholic priest, anchors the skeptical side; France published his defense in a leading peer-reviewed journal).
narrative_ru: Досье проверяло единственное утверждение: приказ Ирода Великого об избиении младенцев в Вифлееме как историческое событие. Рассказ сохранился только в Евангелии от Матфея; Иосиф Флавий, подробно описавший жестокости Ирода, о нём молчит, и большинство критических исследователей (Браун, Луц, Вермеш) считают эпизод легендарным или непроверяемым. Меньшинство квалифицированных учёных (Франс, Майер) защищает правдоподобие: убийство десятка младенцев в маленькой деревне соответствует документированному поведению Ирода в последние годы и могло не попасть в сохранившиеся источники. Обе стороны работают с одним и тем же скудным материалом, поэтому вердикт по формулировке книги: вопрос по-настоящему открыт. Ослабленная версия (событие такого масштаба правдоподобно, но недоказуемо) получает оценку вероятно. Картину изменила бы независимая нехристианская запись первого века или доказательство, что весь эпизод целиком выведен из иудейских преданий о Моисее.

components.component: Herod the Great ordered and carried out the killing of the young male children of Bethlehem (the event occurred).
components.rationale: Single attestation (Matthew 2:16-18) with no genealogically independent corroboration (Justin Dial. 78 and Macrobius Sat. 2.4.11 both fail independence, see evidence_review). The critical majority (Brown, Luz, Vermes; most recent Herod biographies) reads the episode as legendary or unverifiable on two grounds: Josephus's silence despite his detailed hostile catalog of Herod's crimes, and the Moses/Pharaoh haggadic template shaping Matthew's infancy narrative. The peer-reviewed minority (France, Novum Testamentum 1979; Maier 1998; Ferguson, Keener) answers that a killing of perhaps ten to twenty infants in a village of about a thousand falls below the notice threshold of surviving sources, that arguments from silence are weak, and that the deed fits Herod's documented final-years conduct, a consistency even Brown concedes. Neither side possesses evidence the other lacks; the dispute is over weighting silence versus character-consistency on an identical, tiny evidence base. Positive disproof does not exist (silence and literary shaping show the sole witness is unconfirmed and theologically molded, not that the event did not occur), and positive confirmation does not exist either. Per charter §1 this is qualified disagreement on comparable evidential quality that does not reduce to confessional alignment: genuinely-open.

components.component: The killing aimed to destroy the newborn 'King of Israel' whose birth the Magi had announced (Herod's intent).
components.rationale: The motive is inseparable from the event's sole source: Matthew 2:3-16 explicitly states the intent ('Herod will seek the young child to destroy him'), and no scholar defends the event while disputing this motive. The component is conditional on the fact component: if the event occurred, the only surviving account of why is Matthew's own. The competing mainstream position denies the event rather than offering a rival documented motive, so under §3 the undocumented-motive cap is genuinely-open, which coincides with the fact component's level. Rated genuinely-open, tracking the fact component.

components.component: The scene's setting (the final-judgment panorama in which the wicked behold their deeds) and the identification of the Bethlehem infant as 'the King of Israel' in the christological sense.
components.rationale: Paragraph 15 of chapter 42 is an eschatological vision; the judgment-panorama frame and the messianic identity of the child are theological and receive no ruling (§9). The empirical components above are ruled conditionally on extracting the historical assertion from this devotional setting, which is how the extractor and verifier both read it: White relays the Matthean account as history, the near-universal 19th-century treatment.

rulings.weakened_version: A small-scale killing of Bethlehem's infant boys, as Matthew reports, would be consistent with Herod the Great's documented conduct in his final years and could have fallen below the notice of surviving non-Christian sources, though it cannot be independently confirmed.
rulings.minority_engagement: Not formally required for genuinely-open, but §1 demands positive documentation of disagreement on both sides, given here. Strongest case AGAINST the claim: Brown (The Birth of the Messiah), whose actual argument is cumulative, not a proof of non-occurrence: Josephus's demonstrated appetite for exactly this kind of paranoia-revealing atrocity makes his silence weighty, and the episode's genesis is fully explicable from the first-century Moses haggadah plus Matthew's fulfillment scheme. This does not defeat the claim outright because the template explains shaping without entailing invention, and the silence argument rests on an inference about Josephus's authorial interests that France's small-scale demography genuinely undercuts. Strongest case FOR the claim: France (Novum Testamentum 21, 1979) and Maier (1998), whose small-population and audience-indifference arguments use the same data the skeptics accept and were published in peer-reviewed or academic venues; they fail to establish historicity (plausibility and unfalsifiable silence-explanations cannot substitute for attestation), but they do not fail on dating, provenance, circularity, or superseded data, so per §5.1 they keep the question open rather than being defeated. The defenders' confessional location (largely evangelical) is noted per §4.1 and is not grounds for dismissal; the skeptical side is anchored by a Catholic priest (Brown), so the disagreement does not reduce to confessional alignment. Verdict: neither side's case is qualitatively stronger on evidence; genuinely-open.

sources.ref: Raymond E. Brown, The Birth of the Messiah (Anchor Bible Reference Library, 1977, rev. 1993), on Matt 2:16-18: unverifiable, likely non-historical; Moses-haggadah template; rebuts the too-small-to-notice argument.
sources.independence_note: Standard mainstream commentary; content reached via converging secondary summaries (Wikipedia, Ehrman, ABR article engaging Brown directly), not paged directly, hence academic-partial. Catholic author holding the skeptical position, evidence against confessional alignment of the dispute.

sources.ref: R. T. France, 'Herod and the Children of Bethlehem,' Novum Testamentum 21 (1979) 98-120; also his NICNT Matthew commentary: Bethlehem population ~1,000, perhaps 10-20 boys under two; argument from silence weak; consistent with Herod's final years.
sources.independence_note: Peer-reviewed defense in a leading NT journal; genealogically independent of Brown (contemporaneous, opposed conclusion from the same primary data). Evangelical author, institutional location noted per §4.1.

sources.ref: Paul L. Maier, 'Herod and the Infants of Bethlehem,' in Chronos, Kairos, Christos II (1998): Greco-Roman audience indifference to infanticide; concedes 'most recent biographies of Herod the Great deny it entirely.'
sources.independence_note: Professional ancient historian (Western Michigan University); argument independent of France in detail though same direction; his concession about recent Herod biographies is admission-against-interest evidence for the majority position.

sources.ref: Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho 78, read in scholarship/anf01-ante-nicene-fathers-vol1.txt lines ~21636-21645 ('ordered simply the whole of the children then in Bethlehem to be massacred', with the Jeremiah prooftext).
sources.independence_note: Second-century attestation but genealogically dependent on Matthean tradition (same narrative sequence and prooftext); carries no independent evidential weight for historicity.

sources.ref: Macrobius, Saturnalia 2.4.11 (Loeb; c. 430): Augustus's 'Herod's pig' quip over boys under two killed 'in Syria' including Herod's own son.
sources.independence_note: Discounted as independent evidence: four centuries late, conflates the massacre with the execution of Antipater (so already Lardner; modern assessments concur), and plausibly contaminated by Christian tradition.

sources.ref: Sung J. Cho, Matthew's Account of the Massacre of the Innocents in Light of Its Reception History (T&T Clark / LNTS, 2021).
sources.independence_note: Recent academic monograph; reception-historical rather than adjudicating historicity; documents that the live scholarly conversation has largely moved to reception, consistent with an unresolved historicity question.

sources.ref: Bart Ehrman, 'Massacre of the Innocents: History vs Myth' (bartehrman.com); Wikipedia, 'Massacre of the Innocents'.
sources.independence_note: Digest-level statements of the critical majority position; genealogically downstream of Brown and the commentary tradition, counted once.

sources.ref: Associates for Biblical Research, 'The Slaughter of the Innocents: Historical Fact or Legendary Fiction?'; Beitzel, 'Herod the Great: Another Snapshot of His Treachery?', JETS 57/2 (2014) on Macrobius.
sources.independence_note: Apologetic and confessional-academic statements of the defense; cited as positions per §4.5, useful for mapping the defense's actual arguments (France, Maier, Macrobius) rather than as arbiters.

rulings.would_change_this: Toward well-supported: any genealogically independent first-century attestation (a non-Christian notice, or a securely datable pre-Matthean Jewish source referring to killings of infants at Bethlehem), or an archaeologically datable mass infant burial near Bethlehem from Herod's last years. Toward improbable: a datable pre-Matthean text applying the Moses-infanticide motif to a messianic child (showing the episode existed as a literary topos awaiting attachment), or a peer-reviewed demographic study removing the small-scale defense by showing Bethlehem's size made an unnoticed massacre implausible. The weakened ruling would fall if Herod scholarship overturned the picture of his final-years conduct.
rulings.notes: No verbatim universal quantifiers in the quote; 'the innocent children of Bethlehem' is a definite plural tracking Matthew's own wording, so no §6 burden escalation applies. Impact 2, non-load-bearing, decorative use in an eschatological list of historical actors. The verifier's disputed verdict is confirmed and refined rather than moved: this is a documented qualified standoff, not an exhausted-budget residue.

evidence_review: The dossier covers the Bohemian prologue of GC ch. 6: pre-Hus persecution, Hus's childhood, and the 'two English strangers' picture episode. (1) CONDUIT. Every claim tracks J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, b.3 chs.1-2, verbatim or nearly (local text verified; GC 1911's own appendix refs 125, 126 = Wylie b.3 ch.1, 130 = b.3 ch.2, 128 = Bonnechose). Wylie's cited authority for the persecution imagery, the 1376 burn-decree, the mother anecdote, and the two-Englishmen frescoes is Comenius, Historia persecutionum Ecclesiae Bohemicae (1648, pp. 21-25 and the frescoes passage; plus Krasinski, Slavonia p. 60, itself derivative) - a Unity of Brethren exile martyrology written 250 years after the events, situated by modern study 'between history, identity and martyrology' (academia.edu monographic essay on the Historia persecutionum). Genealogically, the whole legendary layer is ONE source line: Comenius (within the 16th-17th c. Flacius/Foxe martyrological stream) -> Krasinski/Bonnechose -> Wylie -> GC. (2) PRE-HUS PERSECUTION. What changed since the 19th century: A. Patschovsky's discovery and edition of the 14th-century Bohemian inquisition records (Quellen zur boehmischen Inquisition im 14. Jh., 1979) documented the inquisitor Gallus of Neuhaus (c. 1335 - c. 1353) prosecuting German-speaking Waldensian communities in southern Bohemia: the surviving fragment names ~180 heretics with 15 executions, and scholars extrapolate over 2,000 persons touched and up to ~200 burned (Ghegoiu CEU thesis; Utz Tremp; 2026 Journal of Medieval History article on Krems/Prague/Swidnica). Independently, Lea (1888, vol.2, Bohemia chapter, from papal bulls and synodal acts) documents the 1245 Innocent IV decretal, 1257 and 1318/1335 inquisitorial commissions, Arnest of Pardubice's episcopal inquisition and perquisitions (1350s+), and hereditary Waldensian families near Pisek into the 1380s. So real persecution with many executions is well documented; but the records locate clandestine worship in private houses at night (Patschovsky-line scholarship; Lea notes 'caverns' only as a hostile topos), not in forests and mountains, and no blanket decree 'all who depart shall be burned' is known for Bohemia: the law in force (imperial constitutions + canon law applied through the inquisition) burned obstinate and relapsed heretics after process while penitent first offenders received penances. Wylie's '1376 decree' rests on Comenius alone. (3) HUS'S CHILDHOOD. The documentary record is nearly empty: Spinka (John Hus: A Biography, Princeton UP 1968) - 'Nothing is known of his father'; of the family 'even less is known'; the mother's name is unknown; Spinka only conjectures ('Perhaps... it was she who wanted her son to become a priest and thus obtain a respected and secure living'). Fudge ('To Build a Fire', Christian History 68; the leading current specialist): 'His parents were peasants - nameless and unknown. His mother taught Jan to pray and, as he grew older, influenced him toward a career as a priest'; Hus's own documented recollection is that he first sought priesthood 'for the money and prestige'. The kneeling-blessing journey scene (with Wylie's lost-present detail) has no attestation before the Comenius layer and appears in no modern biography. (4) THE TWO ENGLISH STRANGERS AND THE PICTURES. Thirteen searches (English, Czech, German) found no medieval attestation of two Englishmen ('James and Conrad of Canterbury', so named only in the Comenius/Wylie line) arriving c. 1404, disputing against papal primacy, painting the two pictures publicly, drawing crowds, and fleeing. Modern accounts of Wycliffite transmission to Prague (Hudson; Van Dussen, From England to Bohemia, CUP 2012, and his BRRP 7 paper; Smahel; Spinka; Fudge) document different channels: Jerome of Prague returning from Oxford with Wyclif's works (~1401), Mikulas Faulfis and Jiri z Knehnic copying texts in England (1406-7), the Anne of Bohemia court connection, Richard Wyche's 1410 letter to Hus, and Peter Payne's arrival in 1414 - and none includes the two-strangers episode. The MOTIF, however, is genuine and central to Hussite propaganda, but dated 1412+, after Hus's development, not before: Nicholas of Dresden's Tabule veteris et novi coloris (1412; ed. Kaminsky, 'The Old Color and the New', APS 1965), an antithesis of the humble Christ and the pompous modern church made for public promulgation from the Black Rose house school; the Bethlehem Chapel program (Hus's own prison letter from Constance about the 'figures of Jesus Christ which he had got painted upon its walls', plus the De sex erroribus wall inscriptions, partly extant); and the later Jena Codex, originally titled Antithesis Christi et Antichristi (c. 1490-1510, Drobna's monograph), whose facing miniatures include exactly Christ on the donkey versus the pope on horseback - the pairing that reached Cranach's Passional Christi und Antichristi (1521; Taylorian/Royal Holloway edition traces the lineage). A documented 1408 disputation on Wyclif at the Black Rose with ~1000 listeners (Tomek) shows the milieu's publicity, and the authorities' documented suppression of Wycliffism (1403 condemnation of the 45 articles, 1410 book burnings) matches the 'silenced by the authorities' motif in substance. Net state of the question: the substance of each scene (persecution, a poor pious upbringing as tradition, Wycliffite arrival from England, public antithesis pictures, their popular resonance, imagery mattering to Hus) ranges from genuinely open to well documented; the specific staging GC prints as fact (forest worship, blanket burn-decree, the kneeling mother, the two named Englishmen and their flight, the pictures' causal role in Hus's turn to Wyclif) belongs to the martyrological legend layer with no medieval evidence.
narrative_ru: Девять утверждений досье пересказывают богемский пролог главы о Гусе по викторианской книге Уайли, который сам опирался на мартиролог Коменского 1648 года. Ядро каждой сцены историки подтверждают: инквизиция четырнадцатого века действительно казнила богемских вальденсов, виклифитские идеи действительно пришли из Англии в Прагу, а контрастные изображения смиренного Христа и пышного папы действительно выставлялись в гуситской Праге, от стен Вифлеемской капеллы до таблиц Николая Дрезденского. Конкретные же сцены книги, включая коленопреклонённую мать у ворот Праги и двух английских художников с толпами у их рисунков, не имеют ни одного средневекового свидетельства и отсутствуют у современных биографов Гуса, от Спинки до Фаджа. Поэтому в формулировке как написано все девять утверждений получили оценку «маловероятно». В ослабленных версиях четыре поднялись до «твёрдо подтверждено», два до «вероятно», два остались открытыми, а одно смягчить нельзя. Найденная хроника пятнадцатого века об английских гостях или любой документ о семье Гуса изменили бы картину.

quantifier_audit: all who departed from the Romish worship should be burned

components.component: Pre-Hus Bohemian dissenters were persecuted and forced into clandestine worship
components.rationale: Documented independently of the Comenius/Wylie line: Patschovsky's edition of the Gallus of Neuhaus inquisition records (c. 1335-1353, southern Bohemian Waldensians) and Lea's account from papal bulls and synodal acts (1245 decretal, 1257/1318/1335 commissions, episcopal perquisitions under Arnest of Pardubice). Secrecy of worship is explicit in the record.

components.component: The clandestine worship took place in the forests and the mountains
components.rationale: The inquisition records locate Waldensian conventicles in private houses at night; 'caverns' appear in Lea's material only as a hostile accusation topos. Forest-and-mountain worship is martyrological scene-setting from Comenius via Wylie, unattested for Bohemia.

components.component: They were hunted by soldiers, and many were put to death
components.rationale: Many deaths are documented: 15 executions in the surviving trial fragment, with scholarly extrapolation to some 200 burned and over 2,000 prosecuted (Patschovsky-line). Enforcement ran through the inquisition and the secular arm rather than literal soldier-hunts, so the military coloring is loose but the lethal persecution is real.

components.component: After a time it was decreed that all who departed from the Romish worship should be burned
components.rationale: No blanket Bohemian burn-decree is documented; Wylie's '1376 decree' rests on Comenius alone. The law actually in force burned obstinate and relapsed heretics after process, while penitent first offenders received penances and imprisonment - a well-provenanced counterexample to 'all', which per charter 6.3 caps the component at improbable even though death by fire for persistent dissent was genuinely the decreed penalty.

rulings.weakened_version: Pre-Hus Bohemian dissenters, notably the Waldensians, were driven to clandestine worship and hunted down; many were burned, and persistent departure from Roman worship was legally punishable by death by fire.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case FOR the claim as written is the Wylie/Comenius tradition itself (Historia persecutionum pp. 23-24), which asserts forest worship and a 1376 stake-decree; it fails on provenance (a 1648 confessional martyrology, 250+ years after the events, with no medieval corroboration). Conversely, the Phase-1 verifier's position ('not documented for pre-Hussite Bohemia... a Protestant construction') must also be answered: it under-weighted the genuinely documented Bohemian Waldensian persecution recovered by Patschovsky, so the adjudication moves the persecution core UP to well-supported while sustaining the verifier's doubt only against the staging (forests, blanket decree).

sources.ref: A. Patschovsky, Quellen zur boehmischen Inquisition im 14. Jahrhundert (1979), as digested in S. Ghegoiu, 'Waldensians at the Turn of the Fifteenth Century' (CEU thesis, etd.ceu.edu) and K. Utz Tremp, 'Waldensians' handbook entry; cf. Journal of Medieval History 2026 article on Krems/Prague/Swidnica
sources.independence_note: All numeric estimates descend from Patschovsky's edition of the Gallus of Neuhaus fragment - counted as one genealogical line

sources.ref: H. C. Lea, History of the Inquisition of the Middle Ages, vol. 2 (1888), Bohemia chapter (local file, lines ~14610-14898)
sources.independence_note: Pre-Patschovsky, built from papal bulls and Prague synodal acts - genealogically independent of the trial-record line

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, b.3 ch.1, citing Comenius, Historia persecutionum, pp. 23-24 (local file, lines ~13450-13483)
sources.independence_note: The claim's conduit, not corroboration; single line Comenius -> Wylie -> GC

rulings.would_change_this: Publication of Bohemian inquisition depositions locating Waldensian conventicles in forests or mountains, or a documented 14th-century Bohemian enactment with a blanket burning provision (for example in Charles IV's legislation), would raise the two failing components.
rulings.notes: Matches Wylie b.3 ch.1 nearly verbatim; period-standard for 1888/1911 historiography (synthesis: period_standard candidate).

components.component: Hus's mother took an active part in securing education and religious formation for her son
components.rationale: Qualified specialists themselves split: Spinka (Princeton UP 1968) and Fudge both retain a maternal role ('His mother taught Jan to pray and... influenced him toward a career as a priest' - Fudge; 'Perhaps... it was she who wanted her son to become a priest' - Spinka), while the same scholars document that the parents are 'nameless and unknown' and nothing about them is attested. Affirmation-by-tradition versus documented emptiness is a real underdetermination, positively documented on both sides.

components.component: She was pious and regarded education and the fear of God as the most valuable of possessions
components.rationale: A specific inner valuation of an undocumented, unnamed woman asserted as fact. No documentary basis exists; the only documented motive-testimony in the family orbit is Hus's own admission that he first sought priesthood 'for the money and prestige' (primary, quoted by Fudge and Spinka), on which Spinka builds the rival account that the priesthood plan aimed at a 'respected and secure living'. With the rival account anchored in a document (at one remove), the undocumented motive attribution caps at improbable per charter 3; weighed gently as a devotional-biographical commonplace of low stakes.

rulings.weakened_version: Hus's mother had a hand in directing him toward education and the priesthood.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest position FOR the claim is that the leading modern specialists themselves repeat the mother's formative role (Fudge: taught him to pray, influenced him toward priesthood; Spinka: wanted him to become a priest; Britannica/Wikipedia follow them). Engaged and partly conceded: it keeps the weakened core genuinely open. It cannot carry the printed valuation ('education and the fear of God as the most valuable of possessions'), because that wording's genealogy is the hagiographic layer (Comenius p. 25 via Wylie b.3 ch.1), the specialists flag pre-Prague stories as legend, and the one documented motive datum points to material advancement rather than piety.

sources.ref: M. Spinka, John Hus: A Biography (Princeton UP, 1968), early-life chapter: 'Nothing is known of his father...'; 'Perhaps... it was she who wanted her son to become a priest and thus obtain a respected and secure living'
sources.independence_note: Independent modern specialist, pre-Fudge

sources.ref: T. A. Fudge, 'To Build a Fire' (Christian History 68; johnhus.org reprint): 'His parents were peasants - nameless and unknown. His mother taught Jan to pray and... influenced him toward a career as a priest'; Hus 'originally pursued priesthood for the money and prestige'
sources.independence_note: Leading current specialist writing in a popular venue; independent of Spinka's research and of the Wylie line

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, b.3 ch.1 (local file, lines ~13555-13568), footnote to Comenius, Historia persecutionum p. 25
sources.independence_note: The claim's conduit; single Comenius line

rulings.would_change_this: A passage in Hus's own writings or in contemporary testimony mentioning his mother's piety or aims (for example in the critical editions of his Czech works, or in Novotny's or Smahel's documentation) would raise both components; conversely, a critical study tracing the mother tradition wholly to post-1500 hagiography would push the weakened version down.
rulings.notes: Smahel's and Novotny's Czech biographies were not directly consultable within budget; the ruling rests on Spinka/Fudge convergence.

components.component: The widowed, poor mother accompanied Hus on the journey to Prague
components.rationale: Unattested. Earliest identifiable source is the Comenius layer (Wylie b.3 ch.1 cites Historia persecutionum p. 25); modern biographies (Spinka, Fudge) record that nothing is documented about the family - even 'widowed' is an inference stacked on an unknown.

components.component: Nearing the city she kneeled beside the fatherless youth and invoked the blessing of heaven
components.rationale: A devotional set piece with zero medieval attestation, absent from all modern scholarly accounts. Wylie's fuller version (a present for the rector lost on the way, prompting the kneeling prayer) exposes the legendary texture; GC trimmed the folkloric detail and printed the scene as fact.

rulings.weakened_version: Hus's mother supported and blessed his departure to study in Prague.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest position FOR is the Bonnechose/Wylie biographical tradition (ultimately Comenius), defended implicitly by its retention in 19th-century scholarship (Lechler-era biographies). Answer: 250-year gap to the events, martyrological genre (the modern monographic essay on the Historia persecutionum places it between history, identity and martyrology), no medieval trace, and the leading modern biographers exclude the scene while stating the record of Hus's pre-Prague life is empty. The general maternal-support tradition retained by Spinka and Fudge keeps only the weakened version open.

sources.ref: M. Spinka, John Hus: A Biography (Princeton UP, 1968): family undocumented, mother's name unknown; no journey/blessing scene
sources.independence_note: Independent modern specialist

sources.ref: T. A. Fudge, 'To Build a Fire' (Christian History 68): parents 'nameless and unknown'; no journey scene
sources.independence_note: Independent of Spinka and of the Wylie line

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, b.3 ch.1 (local file, lines ~13556-13568) with footnote to Comenius p. 25; GC 1911 appendix ref 126 = Wylie b.3 ch.1
sources.independence_note: Conduit; single Comenius line

rulings.would_change_this: Discovery of a 15th- or 16th-century Bohemian source for the journey or blessing anecdote, or any contemporary document about Hus's family, would reopen the as-written ruling.
rulings.notes: Period-standard for 1911 (printed as sober fact in Wylie/Bonnechose); synthesis period_standard candidate.

components.component: About this time two learned strangers from England arrived in Prague to spread Wycliffite teaching
components.rationale: No medieval attestation surfaced across searches in English, Czech and German. The episode's genealogy is a single line of 16th-17th-century Protestant martyrology (Flacius/Foxe stream; Comenius 1648 supplies the names 'James and Conrad of Canterbury') -> Krasinski/Bonnechose -> Wylie (who himself hedges 'is said to have occurred... 1404') -> GC. Modern scholarship on Wycliffite transmission to Prague (Hudson; Van Dussen, CUP 2012; Smahel; Spinka; Fudge) documents different channels - Jerome of Prague returning from Oxford ~1401, Faulfis and Knehnic copying Wyclif in England 1406-7, Wyche's 1410 letter, Payne's arrival 1414 - and omits this episode entirely.

rulings.weakened_version: Around 1400 Wycliffite teaching reached Prague carried by men of learning traveling from England, who spread it there.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest opposing position is the Comenius/Wylie tradition with its named persons and 1404 date, plus the general plausibility of the milieu (documented Oxford-Prague traffic; an Englishman, Peter Payne, did later settle in Prague). Answer: the tradition fails on provenance (first identifiable appearance 250 years after the alleged event, in a martyrological genre), on silence (no trace in the edited chronicle corpus as reflected in modern scholarship, and no modern specialist account includes it), and on chronology (the documented public antithesis imagery it resembles belongs to 1412+, suggesting retrojection). Plausibility of milieu keeps the weakened, not the as-written, version alive.

sources.ref: M. Van Dussen, From England to Bohemia: Heresy and Communication in the Later Middle Ages (Cambridge UP, 2012); id., 'Bohemia in English Religious Controversy' (BRRP 7, brrp.org)
sources.independence_note: Standard modern account of the England-Bohemia channels; independent of the martyrological line

sources.ref: M. Spinka, John Hus: A Biography (Princeton UP, 1968) and T. A. Fudge's Hus scholarship: standard accounts of Hus's turn to Wyclif without the two-strangers episode
sources.independence_note: Independent modern biographies

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, b.3 ch.2 'The Two Frescoes' (local file, lines ~13712-13790), footnote to Comenius and Krasinski, Slavonia p. 60; GC 1911 appendix ref 130 = Wylie b.3 ch.2
sources.independence_note: Conduit; Krasinski digests Comenius, so genealogically one source

sources.ref: Monographic essay 'Historia Persecutionum Ecclesiae Bohemicae between History, Identity and Martyrology' (academia.edu)
sources.independence_note: Genre assessment of the episode's ultimate source

rulings.would_change_this: A 15th-century Prague source (chronicle entry, university act, archiepiscopal record) attesting English visitors matching the story - for instance in the Old Czech Annals or Hoefler's Geschichtschreiber corpus - would move the ruling toward genuinely-open or probable.
rulings.notes: The Czech chronicle corpus itself could not be exhaustively searched within budget; the ruling rests on its silence as reflected in modern specialist literature.

components.component: The two strangers opened with a public attack on papal supremacy, were silenced by the authorities, and turned to other means
components.rationale: A dependent scene of the same single-line legend (Wylie: 'public disputations... the authorities promptly put a stop to the discussions'). Unattested for these persons. The milieu-level pattern is real: the university condemned Wyclif's 45 articles (1403), a disputation on Wyclif at the Black Rose drew ~1000 listeners (1408, Tomek), and Wycliffite advocacy was officially suppressed (1410 book burnings) while the movement turned to vernacular preaching, songs and images - but none of this attaches to two Englishmen in 1404.

rulings.weakened_version: Open Wycliffite challenges to papal authority in Prague were silenced by the authorities, and the movement's advocates turned to indirect means such as vernacular preaching and images.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest opposing position: the Comenius/Wylie narrative, reinforced by the fact that public disputation culture and official suppression are independently documented, so the scene 'fits'. Answer: fitting the milieu is exactly what a retrojected legend does; with no 15th-century attestation of these disputants and modern specialist silence, verisimilitude cannot substitute for evidence. The documented suppressions (1403, 1408, 1410) involve named local actors, not the two strangers.

sources.ref: Standard accounts of the Prague Wyclif controversy 1403-1412 (Spinka; Fudge; Kaminsky, A History of the Hussite Revolution, California UP 1967); V. V. Tomek, Zaklady stareho mistopisu prazskeho II (1870), p. 188 for the 1408 Black Rose disputation (via cs.wikipedia on the house)
sources.independence_note: Independent of the martyrological line

sources.ref: T. A. Fudge, 'Art and propaganda in Hussite Bohemia' (Religio 1/2, 1993, digilib.phil.muni.cz) - the movement's documented turn to visual and musical propaganda
sources.independence_note: Independent; peer-reviewed venue

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, b.3 ch.2 (local file, lines ~13751-13759)
sources.independence_note: Conduit; Comenius line

rulings.would_change_this: The same chronicle-class find as GC-06-027: any contemporary record of English disputants against papal primacy in Prague around 1404.

components.component: The two English strangers drew the two pictures in a place open to the public
components.rationale: Inherits the legendary frame of GC-06-027. GC additionally amplifies Wylie: Wylie has the drawing made 'in the corridor of the house in which they resided', with the host's leave; GC turns this into 'a place open to the public'. No medieval attestation for the painters or the act.

components.component: Contrasting pictures of Christ's humble entry into Jerusalem versus the pope's rich mounted procession (triple crown, trumpeters, cardinals) were displayed in Hus-era Prague
components.rationale: As imagery, this is the genuine documented Hussite antithesis: Nicholas of Dresden's Tabule veteris et novi coloris (1412; Kaminsky's APS edition) made for public promulgation from the Black Rose school; the Bethlehem Chapel wall program (Hus's own prison letter about the Christ-figures 'he had got painted upon its walls'; De sex erroribus inscriptions partly extant); the Jena Codex, originally titled Antithesis Christi et Antichristi, with facing miniatures of Christ on the donkey versus the pope on horseback; the lineage runs to Cranach's Passional (1521). The documented imagery postdates GC's placement (1412+ versus ~1404) - the legend retrojets a real phenomenon.

rulings.weakened_version: Contrasting pictures of Christ's humility and papal pomp, including Christ on the ass versus the pope's mounted procession, were publicly displayed in Hus's Prague.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest position FOR the claim as written: the Comenius/Wylie tradition, plus the registry's own observation that the antithesis motif is genuine - a defender could argue the legend preserves a real early display. Engaged: the motif's genuineness is exactly what the weakened ruling credits at well-supported; but every documented instance (Tabule 1412, Bethlehem walls, Jena Codex) is later than GC's placement and attached to other actors (the Dresden masters at the Black Rose, Hus's own chapel), so the two-Englishmen-in-1404 staging remains without evidence and is best explained as retrojection.

sources.ref: H. Kaminsky (ed.), Master Nicholas of Dresden: The Old Color and the New (Transactions of the American Philosophical Society 55/1, 1965); id., A History of the Hussite Revolution (1967)
sources.independence_note: Critical edition of the primary antithesis text; independent of the martyrological line

sources.ref: Jena Codex (Antithesis Christi et Antichristi, Prague, KNM IV.B.24, c. 1490-1510); Z. Drobna, The Jena Codex: Hussite Pictorial Satire; Taylorian/Royal Holloway edition of Cranach's Passional tracing the antithesis lineage
sources.independence_note: Manuscript evidence with scholarly apparatus; independent

sources.ref: Hus's prison letter from Constance on the Bethlehem wall pictures (Epist. xliv, quoted in Wylie b.3 ch.6 from Bonnechose's edition of the letters; critical text in Novotny/Spinka editions)
sources.independence_note: Hus's own testimony to Christ-imagery on Bethlehem's walls

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, b.3 ch.2 (local file, lines ~13760-13775)
sources.independence_note: Conduit; note GC's 'place open to the public' amplifies Wylie's 'corridor of the house'

rulings.would_change_this: A pre-1410 Prague source attesting publicly displayed Christ/pope antithesis pictures by foreign visitors would vindicate the as-written version; conversely, a study showing the Bethlehem murals held no Christ/pope contrast would weaken the weakened ruling.
rulings.notes: GC 1911 ref 127 on the quote is Matt. 21:5 (the 'meek, sitting upon an ass' scripture), not a historical source.

components.component: Crowds of all classes came to gaze at the two strangers' drawings
components.rationale: A dependent detail of the unattested episode ('Many came to gaze' - Wylie); with the display itself undocumented, its audience is a fortiori undocumented. The 'all classes' universal has no evidence base of its own.

rulings.weakened_version: Antithetical Christ-versus-pope imagery in Hus's Prague drew broad public attention across social ranks.
rulings.minority_engagement: The defense of the claim is the same single Comenius/Wylie line; it fails on provenance as with GC-06-027. The weakened version, by contrast, has real support: Nicholas of Dresden's Tables were composed for broad public promulgation, Hussite picture-and-song propaganda demonstrably reached lay audiences (Fudge's Religio study), the Bethlehem Chapel drew mass vernacular audiences, and the 1408 Black Rose disputation reportedly drew ~1000 listeners - but audience composition around specific pictures remains inference, hence probable rather than well-supported.

rulings.would_change_this: Any contemporary account of crowds at a specific pictorial display in early-1400s Prague would move the as-written ruling; audience studies of Hussite propaganda could move the weakened ruling either way.

components.component: The commotion over the pictures forced the two strangers to depart Prague for their own safety
components.rationale: Narrative closure of the legend ('the English strangers deemed it prudent to withdraw' - Wylie, from Comenius). The actors being unattested, their endangered departure is doubly so; no independent record of a city commotion over pictures in this period surfaced.

rulings.weakened_version: n/a
rulings.minority_engagement: The only position FOR is the Comenius/Wylie tradition, answered under GC-06-027 (provenance, silence, retrojection). A defender might point to the real dangers faced by antithesis propagandists (Nicholas of Dresden left Prague and was reportedly burned at Meissen), but that concerns different, later, documented persons and cannot rehabilitate this scene.

sources.ref: Modern Hus and Hussite scholarship's complete silence on the episode (Spinka; Fudge; Kaminsky; Van Dussen)
sources.independence_note: Independent negative evidence across unrelated specialist literatures

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, b.3 ch.2 (local file, lines ~13783-13787)
sources.independence_note: Conduit; Comenius line

rulings.would_change_this: The same evidence class as GC-06-027: a contemporary Prague record of the visitors and their expulsion or flight. Weakened ruling is n/a because any rewrite either keeps the legendary actors (equally unattested) or changes the subject.
rulings.notes: framing_gap is null by construction (weakened version n/a per charter 2).

components.component: Hus saw the two strangers' pictures
components.rationale: Inherits the unattested episode: if the display is undocumented, Hus's encounter with it is too. Wylie asserts it ('Among those who came to gaze... was John Huss'); no earlier source does.

components.component: The pictures made a deep impression on Hus and led him to closer study of the Bible and Wycliffe's writings
components.rationale: A causal-psychological characterization resting on the failed factual premise. The documented drivers of Hus's turn to Wyclif are different: university study of Wyclif's philosophical works from the 1390s (Hus's own 1398 manuscript copies survive), Jerome of Prague's imports of the theological works (~1401), and the Czech academic milieu (Spinka; Fudge; Kaminsky). No source connects Hus's Wyclif studies to pictures seen in 1404.

rulings.weakened_version: Antithetical Christ-versus-pope imagery resonated deeply with Hus, who himself adopted such imagery at Bethlehem Chapel as his study of Scripture and Wycliffe deepened.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest position FOR: Wylie's causal narrative, and behind it the genuine observation that images mattered to Hus - his own Constance prison letter dwells on the Christ-pictures painted on Bethlehem's walls and his dream of their effacement and restoration. Engaged: this primary evidence shows Hus valuing and deploying the imagery around 1412, which supports the weakened version at probable (influence-direction remains inference), but it cannot show 1404 pictures by English visitors causing his turn to Wyclif; the documented chronology of his Wyclif engagement (manuscripts from 1398) actually precedes the legend's date.

sources.ref: Hus's prison letter on the Bethlehem wall pictures and the painters' dream (Epist. xliv, quoted in Wylie b.3 ch.6 from Bonnechose; critical editions Novotny/Spinka)
sources.independence_note: Hus's own words; independent of the legend line

sources.ref: M. Spinka, John Hus: A Biography (Princeton UP 1968) and Lechler's documentation of Hus's 1398 Wyclif manuscript (Stockholm Royal Library, cited in Wylie's own footnote from Lechler, Johann von Wiclif ii.113): Hus's Wyclif engagement began in the 1390s via the university
sources.independence_note: Independent documentation of the actual causal chain

sources.ref: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism, b.3 ch.2 (local file, lines ~13788-13792)
sources.independence_note: Conduit; Comenius line

rulings.would_change_this: Any statement by Hus or a contemporary linking his early Wyclif study to publicly displayed pictures (for instance a passage in his Czech works referencing such paintings before 1410) would raise both the as-written and weakened rulings.
rulings.notes: Tie at the claim minimum between the fact and frame components; failing_component_kind takes fact by the charter 3 priority.

evidence_review: The claim is one sentence of GC ch. 5 par. 45: 'There were none who went before him from whose work he could shape his system of reform.' Context: the surrounding paragraph frames Wycliffe as emerging 'from the obscurity of the Dark Ages,' 'raised up like John the Baptist,' 'the herald of a new era,' with a system whose 'unity and completeness' later Reformers 'did not exceed.' STATE OF THE QUESTION. (1) Against the sentence's natural force, Wycliffe's intellectual genealogy is among the best-documented facts of his biography. His dominion theory, the doctrinal engine of his whole reform program (disendowment of a delinquent church, lordship founded in grace, the Crown's right to correct the clergy), was shaped directly from Richard FitzRalph's De pauperie Salvatoris: the dependence is so close that R. L. Poole's Wyclif Society critical edition (1890) prints the first four books of FitzRalph's treatise as an appendix to Wyclif's De dominio divino, and the standard analytic literature (M. Wilks, 'Predestination, Property, and Power: Wyclif's Theory of Dominion and Grace', Studies in Church History; S. E. Lahey, John Wyclif, Oxford UP 2009, and his Stanford Encyclopedia entries) treats FitzRalph as the proximate source, behind whom stands Augustine, whom Wyclif himself credits with 'the outline of the theory.' J. A. Robson (Wyclif and the Oxford Schools, Cambridge UP 1961) names Bradwardine and FitzRalph 'the two great influences on Wyclif'; Bradwardine's De causa Dei fed his Augustinian predestinarianism. Robert Grosseteste held a singular authority for fourteenth-century Oxford thinkers, and Wyclif invoked his writings constantly and held him in the highest regard (SEP 'Robert Grosseteste'; Robson; Levy ed., A Companion to John Wyclif, Brill 2011). Marsilius of Padua's Defensor Pacis (1324) and Ockham's polemics had already drawn more radical antipapal conclusions two generations earlier; how directly Wyclif used Marsilius is contested (some scholarship is criticized for overstating it), so Marsilius functions here as available tradition, not as a documented source. (2) For the sentence's substance, an equally solid body of scholarship holds that no organized reform movement or predecessor reformer's program existed in England for Wycliffe to adapt: England was remarkably free of popular heresy before the late fourteenth century (I. Forrest, The Detection of Heresy in Late Medieval England, Oxford 2005; J. H. Arnold; the Cambridge Companion to Christian Heresy ch. 17), Lollardy was the first and only flourishing medieval English heresy (M. Aston, 'Lollardy: the English heresy?'), and Anne Hudson's The Premature Reformation (Clarendon 1988) canonized the judgment that Wycliffism was a reformation avant la lettre, an original welding of academic critique into a comprehensive reform program with scriptural authority and vernacular Scripture at its center. (3) Period baseline: White's sentence follows the 19th-century morning-star trope (D'Aubigne, Wylie), but the letter of her absolute outruns even that tradition. Wylie himself names Claudius of Turin and Berengarius as 'morning stars' who 'appeared while as yet all was dark' before Wycliffe's 'dawn' (local text, wylie-history-of-protestantism-vol1.txt lines 17782-17807) and devotes pages to FitzRalph's anti-mendicant campaign as prologue to Wycliffe (lines 8794-8829). Deanesly (The Lollard Bible, Cambridge UP 1920, local text lines 9403-9414) documents pre-Wycliffite vernacular scriptures in Anglo-Norman. (4) What changed since 1911: the Wyclif Society editions and the line Workman (1926), Robson (1961), Leff, Hudson (1988), Lahey (2009), Levy (2011) re-embedded Wyclif in his Oxford scholastic context, dissolving the solitary-genius picture while simultaneously confirming his originality as a synthesizer and the absence of any prior English reform movement. The adjudication question therefore reduces to semantics: read as 'no predecessor's work contributed the materials of his system,' the sentence is defeated by documented counterexamples; read as 'no predecessor reformer's system of reform existed for him to adapt,' it is essentially what modern scholarship affirms.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет фразу из пятой главы: у Уиклифа не было предшественников, по чьим трудам он мог бы построить свою систему реформы. В буквальном прочтении это опровергнуто документально: учение о владычестве, стержень его программы, Уиклиф взял из трактата Фицральфа «De pauperie Salvatoris», а Гроссетеста и Брадвардина постоянно цитировал как своих учителей. Поэтому утверждение как написано получило оценку «маловероятно». Ослабленная версия судьбы иной: в Англии до Уиклифа не существовало ни организованного реформного движения, ни готовой программы реформы, и его синтез с Писанием на народном языке в центре наука признаёт первым в своём роде, так что эта версия оценена как «хорошо подтверждена». Картину изменило бы доказательство, что теорию владычества Уиклиф разработал независимо от Фицральфа, либо находка организованного английского реформного движения до него.

components.component: No predecessor produced work from which Wycliffe shaped, or could have shaped, his system of reform (the sentence's natural force: his reform system owed its materials to no earlier thinker).
components.rationale: Under the natural reading the universal negative is defeated by well-provenanced counterexamples: Wyclif's dominion doctrine, the theological ground of his disendowment program, was shaped directly from FitzRalph's De pauperie Salvatoris (Poole's Wyclif Society edition prints FitzRalph's books 1-4 as an appendix to De dominio divino precisely because the dependence is textual; Wilks; Lahey); Robson names Bradwardine and FitzRalph 'the two great influences on Wyclif'; Wyclif invoked Grosseteste as a standing authority. Per charter 6.3 multiple independent counterexamples would normally yield discredited, but their defeating force depends on resolving a genuine semantic bifurcation: if 'system of reform' denotes a predecessor reformer's program-template, then FitzRalph, Grosseteste, and Bradwardine, none of whom was a reformer with a system, are not counterexamples at all, and no such template existed. Because that reading is live in the sentence's own context (GC ch. 5 par. 4 credits Wycliffe's scholastic education, so the author cannot mean zero intellectual debt), the component rests at improbable per charter 1: clear preponderance against the claim's rhetorical force, with a surviving weakened version.

components.component: Wycliffe as originator: herald of a new era whose reform system, taken as a system, had no antecedent he could adapt.
components.rationale: Matches the standard modern judgment: England was remarkably free of heresy before the late fourteenth century (Forrest, Arnold, Aston), Lollardy was the first English reform movement, and Hudson's 'premature reformation' framing treats Wyclif's synthesis as original and unprecedented as a program. Held at probable rather than well-supported because, as printed, the frame effaces the documented Oxford genealogy (FitzRalph, Bradwardine, Grosseteste) and the prior continental antipapal tradition (Marsilius, Ockham) from which the system's components demonstrably or plausibly came.

rulings.weakened_version: Wycliffe was the first to weld existing materials (FitzRalph's dominion doctrine, anti-mendicant and antipapal polemic, Oxford Augustinian theology) into a comprehensive program of church reform with scriptural authority and vernacular Scripture at its center; no organized reform movement or predecessor reformer's program existed in England for him to adapt.
rulings.minority_engagement: The strongest case for the sentence as written is the Phase-1 defender's contextual reading, continuous with the 19th-century morning-star historiography (D'Aubigne, Wylie) White followed: par. 45 contrasts Wycliffe with later Reformers, and 'from whose work he could shape his system of reform' means no prior Reformer supplied a ready-made program, not that Wycliffe had no teachers, which GC ch. 5 par. 4 itself concedes. Answer: the reading is semantically live and this adjudication incorporates it, but it rescues the sentence only by converting it into the weakened version; as a statement of intellectual history in the sense a reader of par. 45 ('obscurity of the Dark Ages', 'herald of a new era') would take it, it fails against Wyclif's own citation practice (FitzRalph as 'Armachanus', Grosseteste as 'Lincolniensis') and against the consensus line from Robson through Lahey and Levy. Even the period sources undercut the letter of the absolute: Wylie names Claudius and Berengarius as pre-Wycliffe 'morning stars' and narrates FitzRalph's anti-mendicant campaign as Wycliffe's prologue. The defender's point is conceded exactly to the extent that it blocks 'discredited': the semantic dispute is real, and what the defensible reading asserts is what the weakened ruling vindicates as well-supported. No current published scholarship defends the no-predecessors picture in the broad sense; its home is superseded 19th-century narrative, weighed here as period baseline, not as live minority.

sources.ref: J. A. Robson, Wyclif and the Oxford Schools (Cambridge UP, 1961); Bradwardine and FitzRalph as 'the two great influences on Wyclif'
sources.independence_note: University-press monograph on Wyclif's scholastic formation; genealogically independent of the SEP/Lahey line the Phase-1 verifier used.

sources.ref: R. L. Poole (ed.), Iohannis Wycliffe De dominio divino libri tres, to which are added the first four books of the treatise De pauperie Salvatoris (Wyclif Society, 1890)
sources.independence_note: Critical edition; the editorial coupling of Wyclif's text with FitzRalph's treatise documents the textual dependence independently of later secondary synthesis.

sources.ref: S. E. Lahey, John Wyclif (Oxford UP, 2009), ch. 'The Oxford Context of Wyclif's Thought'; Lahey, 'John Wyclif's Political Philosophy' (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.independence_note: Same genealogy as the Phase-1 verifier's sole source (Lahey authored the SEP entry); counted as one line alongside the independent Robson and Poole lines.

sources.ref: I. C. Levy (ed.), A Companion to John Wyclif: Late Medieval Theologian (Brill, 2011)
sources.independence_note: Multi-author state-of-the-question companion; partially overlaps Lahey (contributor) but carries independent chapters on Wyclif's sources.

sources.ref: M. Wilks, 'Predestination, Property, and Power: Wyclif's Theory of Dominion and Grace', Studies in Church History (Cambridge Core)
sources.independence_note: Classic analytic study of the dominion doctrine's derivation; independent of Robson and of the Lahey/SEP line.

sources.ref: SEP, 'Robert Grosseteste' (Wyclif invoked Grosseteste's writings; Grosseteste as authoritative figure for 14th-century Oxford)
sources.independence_note: Different author and subject entry from Lahey's Wyclif entries; used only for the Grosseteste-authority point.

sources.ref: A. Hudson, The Premature Reformation: Wycliffite Texts and Lollard History (Clarendon, 1988); M. Aston, 'Lollardy: the English heresy?' (Studies in Church History); I. Forrest, The Detection of Heresy in Late Medieval England (Oxford, 2005); Cambridge Companion to Christian Heresy, ch. 17
sources.independence_note: The favorable evidence base for the weakened version (no prior English heresy or reform movement; Wycliffism as original synthesis); independent of the dominion-genealogy literature.

sources.ref: Local: J. A. Wylie, History of Protestantism vol. 1 (scholarship/wylie-history-of-protestantism-vol1.txt, lines 8794-8829 on FitzRalph's anti-mendicant campaign; lines 17782-17807 on Claudius and Berengarius as pre-Wycliffe 'morning stars')
sources.independence_note: Period baseline (19th-century confessional history in White's own source tradition); shows the letter of the GC absolute exceeds even its period sources.

sources.ref: Local: M. Deanesly, The Lollard Bible (Cambridge UP, 1920) (scholarship/deanesly-lollard-bible-1920.txt, lines 9403-9414: pre-Wycliffite Anglo-Norman vernacular scriptures; Grosseteste and FitzRalph discussions)
sources.independence_note: Older but standard monograph; used for context on the pre-Wycliffite vernacular landscape, not load-bearing for the ruling.

rulings.would_change_this: As written: a scholarly demonstration that Wyclif's dominion framework was formed before and independently of his reading of FitzRalph (for example a redating of De civili dominio's sources), combined with evidence that 'system of reform' in the sentence's source tradition denoted only a reformer's program-template, would move the ruling toward genuinely-open. Weakened: documentation of an organized pre-Wycliffite English reform movement with a comparable program (a datable pre-1370 English heretical or reform corpus) would pull the weakened ruling down from well-supported.
rulings.notes: Phase-1 path: verifier `anachronistic` (medium), defender downgraded to `disputed` on the semantic bifurcation of 'system of reform'; this ruling incorporates the defender's argument as the reason improbable is not discredited and as the substance of the weakened version. Period-standard remark for synthesis: the sentence follows the morning-star trope of D'Aubigne/Wylie, but its absolute letter exceeds even Wylie, who names forerunners (Claudius, Berengarius) and details FitzRalph; the anachronism defense is therefore only partial. No motive or hermeneutic components; no tie at the claim minimum.
